content.text:
Based on my survey, here's a comprehensive findings report on knowledge persistence across your repos.

---

## FINDINGS REPORT: Knowledge Persistence Landscape

### 1. SCALE & TAXONOMY

**Scope**: 21 top-level category directories containing 45 git repositories

**Category breakdown (representative sample examined)**:
- Software projects: AUDIO, BEAM-ELIXIR, DESIGN, DIRTWIRE, GAMES, ITS JUST, TOURLAB, TOURING, UNIX-AGENTICS
- Research/framework: amieval (core), CB (archived versions)
- Personal/productivity: SECOND-BRAIN/1181 (Finances, Home, I&I), SECOND-BRAIN/me-direction, SECOND-BRAIN/me-journal
- Specialized: TERMINAL-TUTS, TUTORIALS, STRATTA, OPENCLAW, SHELL-AGENTICS

---

### 2. KNOWLEDGE ARTIFACT TYPES (BY PROJECT)

**Representative 8 repos examined:**

| Repo | Artifact Types | Organization |
|------|---|---|
| **amieval** | CLAUDE.md, evals/*.md, plans/, positions/, beliefs.json, collections.json, README.md, thesis-strategy.md | Dense multi-layer: 11 subrepos, each with docs + code + metadata |
| **SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain** | index.json (DAG), sources/*.md, assertions/*.md, plans/, research/, templates/ | YAML frontmatter markdown canonical, derived JSON index |
| **SECOND-BRAIN/firewaterairvoid** | CLAUDE.md, STRATEGY.md, USER.md, DESIGN.md, plan.md, USER_STORIES.md, TESTING.md | Narrative docs + plan.md index pointing to git history |
| **TOURLAB/tourlab** | CLAUDE.md, README.md, SYSTEM.md | Minimal: guidance + system overview only |
| **UNIX-AGENTICS/you-should-build-an-agent** | CLAUDE.md, README.md, prompt.md | Teaching-focused: CLAUDE.md teaches the agent conventions |
| **DESIGN** | principles.md, SETUP.md, README.md, references/README.md, foundations/README.md, components/README.md, .claude/settings.local.json | Hierarchical folders with README scaffolding |
| **AUDIO/Way-of-SunVox** | README.md, Sitemap.md, multiple tutorial .md files | Linear teaching material + reference docs |
| **DIRTWIRE/bandlab-data-dirtwire** | CLAUDE.md, CB-RESTRUCTURE-*.md, RECONCILIATION-INVENTORY.md, org/ (subdirs), ops/plans/ | Migration docs + operational plans + structured org folders |

---

### 3. FORMATS IN ACTIVE USE

**Markdown with YAML frontmatter** (primary canonical format):
```yaml
---
type: assertion | source | directive | convention
tier: primitive | compound | inference | rule
claim: "The statement"
confidence: high | medium | low
tags: [tag1, tag2]
evidence: 
  - source: "[[wikilink]]"
    type: citation | supporting
deps: ["[[other-id]]"]
date_captured: "2026-03-01"
---

Prose content here...
```

**JSON structures** (secondary - derived or metadata):
- `beliefs.json` — Array of belief objects (amieval standard)
- `collections.json` — Metadata for cross-repo resolution
- `index.json` — Derived DAG: sources[], assertions[], edges[]
- `manifest.json` — Collection metadata (namespace, depends_on, description)

**Plain markdown** (unstructured):
- READMEs, guides, tutorials (AUDIO/Way-of-SunVox, UNIX-AGENTICS)
- Plan files (plan.md, USER_STORIES.md, DESIGN.md as strategy prose)

**Special metadata files**:
- `CLAUDE.md` — AI context + conventions (appears in 20+ projects) — typically uppercase
- `claude.md` — Same function, lowercase variant (amieval composable-beliefs, AUDIO)
- `.claude/` directories — Project-level config (settings.local.json, worktrees/, skills/)
- `settings.json` / `settings.local.json` — Claude Code harness config

---

### 4. FRONTMATTER CONVENTIONS (SAMPLED)

**Convention 1: Assertion Graph** (`SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain`):
```yaml
---
type: assertion
tier: primitive | compound
claim: "Atomic claim statement"
confidence: high | medium
evidence:
  - source: "[[source-id]]"
    type: citation | supporting
tags: [semantic, tag, list]
---
```

**Convention 2: Composable Beliefs** (`amieval/belief-collections`):
```json
{
  "id": "namespace:local-id",
  "type": "directive|primitive|compound|inference",
  "kind": "rule|enum-registry|verdict|observation",
  "domain": "system|library|method",
  "claim": "Statement",
  "confidence": "high|medium|low",
  "evidence": [{date, detail, artifact}],
  "deps": ["namespace:id"],
  "subjects": [{ref, type}],
  "status": "active|superseded|stale",
  "created": "2026-01-05"
}
```

**Convention 3: Strategy/Plan Prose** (`SECOND-BRAIN/firewaterairvoid`):
- No frontmatter; narrative markdown
- Index via `plan.md` (~150 lines): pointers to detailed docs
- Git commit history is the version control layer
- Pattern: `plan.md` → (DESIGN.md, STRATEGY.md, USER_STORIES.md)

**Convention 4: Source Ingestion** (`SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain`):
```yaml
---
type: source
source_type: article | video | conversation
title: "Article Title"
url: "https://..."
tags: [semantic, tags]
date_captured: "2026-03-01"
summary: >
  Multi-line summary.
---

Extracted full text or notes...
```

**No frontmatter found**: .org files (zero), TOML configs, or custom protocols across the sample.

---

### 5. EXISTING SUMMARY/INDEX/METADATA LAYERS

**Derived index files** (machine-generated, never hand-edited):
- `/SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain/index.json` — sources[], assertions[], edges[] DAG
- `/amieval/belief-collections/collections.json` — namespace→path resolution map
- `/amieval/belief-collections/library/manifest.json` — namespace, depends_on, description

**Hand-written index/TOC files**:
- `/SECOND-BRAIN/firewaterairvoid/plan.md` (~60 lines) — points to DESIGN/STRATEGY/USER_STORIES docs
- `/AUDIO/Way-of-SunVox/Sitemap.md` — linear navigation map
- `/amieval/evals/README.md` — table of runs + status, maps blog drafts to real experiments

**Metadata in `CLAUDE.md`** (AI conventions layer):
- File structure overview
- Session workflow conventions
- Branch/commit naming rules
- Context-management philosophy

**Schema documentation**:
- `/amieval/belief-collections/library/SCHEMA.md` — enum contracts
- `/amieval/composable-beliefs/lib/cb/belief.ex` — canonical schema (code is spec)

---

### 6. AMIEVAL IN DETAIL

**Structure** (11 subrepos under `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/amieval/`):

| Subrepo | Purpose | Key files |
|---------|---------|-----------|
| `composable-beliefs` | Core framework (Elixir) | CLAUDE.md (generated), beliefs/, lib/, test/, plans/, positions/, skills/ |
| `belief-collections` | Worked examples + owned collections | agent-behavior/, paradigm/, library/, method/, eval-provenance/, toy-eval/, sources/, positions/ |
| `evals` | Append-only eval archive | boundary-blindness/, purity-test/, conceptual-prototype/, blog/, METHODOLOGY.md, IMPLICATIONS.md, ASSERTION_DAGS_THESIS_SECTION.md |
| `cb-direction` | Private strategy (direction/) | thesis-strategy.md, plans/, comparisons/ |
| `amieval-direction` | Cross-system strategy | chronicles/, direction/ |
| `cb-site` | Public blog/docs | posts/ (Markdown + metadata) |
| `planning-data` | Canonical plans | plans/, analyses/, process/, shell/, docs/ |
| `bench` | Eval infra & runs | dag-vs-prose-v2/, smoke/, plans/ |
| `cb-dashboard` | Observability/audit UI | Elixir Phoenix app |
| `plan-app` | Planning tool | Elixir Phoenix app |
| `elixir-wiki`, `eval-tut` | Teaching materials | Markdown tutorial structure |

**Key metadata patterns**:
- Every dir has README.md + .git + (often) CLAUDE.md
- Beliefs are the "atomic unit" (primitives + compounds + inferences)
- Cross-namespace resolution via `collections.json` + `manifest.json`
- CI-generated CLAUDE.md (via `mix cb.generate.claude_md --check`)
- Direction split: public (checkable claims) vs private (strategic reasoning)

---

### 7. SECOND-BRAIN IN DETAIL

**Structure** (11 subdirectories):

| Dir | Type | Contents |
|-----|------|----------|
| `second-brain/` | **Assertion graph repo** | sources/ (YAML markdown), assertions/ (YAML markdown), index.json (derived), schema/, plans/, templates/ |
| `firewaterairvoid/` | **App + lifecycle docs** | Next.js app + CLAUDE.md + plan.md + STRATEGY.md + DESIGN.md + USER_STORIES.md |
| `me-direction/` | **Personal direction docs** | 10-25-25 eo's chat gpt analysis.md, Contacts/ |
| `me-journal/` | **Personal journal** | Lyrics/ |
| `1181/` | **Personal + financial** | I&I/ (Interests & Inspirations: Music, Health, Mindset, Links, Quotes, Org), Finances/, Home/ |
| `dirtwire/` | **Band project ops** | Merch/, Releases/, Samples/, Socials/, Touring/ |
| `dirtwater-touring/`, `9466/` | Minimal/sparse | (few files) |

**Metadata patterns**:
- Personal: no frontmatter, lightweight markdown (✓ unstructured)
- Professional (firewaterairvoid): plan.md index → detailed docs (✓ structured)
- Assertion graph: strict YAML frontmatter + derived index.json (✓ strongly structured)
- Ops/band: folder hierarchy + minimal labeling (~ ad-hoc)

---

### 8. HAPHAZARDNESS ASSESSMENT (Concrete Examples)

**Pattern 1: Frontmatter inconsistency**
- `amieval` uses JSON canonical (beliefs.json)
- `SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain` uses YAML markdown canonical
- `SECOND-BRAIN/firewaterairvoid` uses narrative prose + plan.md pointer
- `AUDIO/Way-of-SunVox` uses plain markdown + Sitemap.md
- **Consequence**: No unified query language; each must be parsed separately

**Pattern 2: Naming variance**
- `CLAUDE.md` (uppercase) vs `claude.md` (lowercase)
- `plan.md` vs `USER.md` vs `STRATEGY.md` vs `DESIGN.md` (all serve "overview" role, no convention)
- `README.md` vs (no README)
- `/docs/` vs `/plans/` vs `/evals/` vs `/beliefs/` (same semantic purpose, different names)

**Pattern 3: Directory structure mismatch**
- `amieval/evals/` has `.md` files + subdirs with run artifacts
- `SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain/sources/` has only `.md` files
- `DIRTWIRE/bandlab-data-dirtwire/` has `org/`, `ops/`, `lib/louder/` — no consistent semantic layer
- **Consequence**: Folder structure encodes different metadata types across repos

**Pattern 4: Schema fragmentation**
- `amieval/composable-beliefs` has formal Elixir schema (contracts, enums, verification)
- `SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain` has YAML-only, loose typing
- `AUDIO/Way-of-SunVox` has zero schema (pure narrative)
- **Consequence**: No cross-repo schema unification possible

**Pattern 5: Index/TOC layer variance**
- `amieval` uses CI-generated CLAUDE.md (authoritative)
- `SECOND-BRAIN/firewaterairvoid` uses hand-written plan.md (index points to git)
- `SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain` uses generated index.json + hand-written README
- `AUDIO` uses Sitemap.md (linear, not graph-aware)
- **Consequence**: Query/navigation APIs would be incompatible

---

### 9. PRODUCTIVITY VS. SOFTWARE PROJECTS

**Personal/Productivity** (SECOND-BRAIN/1181 + me-*):
- Format: Plain markdown, no schema
- Metadata: Folder hierarchy (I&I/Music/, I&I/Health/, Finances/, Home/)
- Indexing: None (implicit folder navigation)
- Version control: Git, but no commit discipline
- **Example**: `/SECOND-BRAIN/1181/I&I/Health/health.md` (no frontmatter, no tags, no hierarchy metadata)

**Software/Research Projects** (amieval, composable-beliefs, SECOND-BRAIN/firewaterairvoid):
- Format: Structured (JSON, YAML frontmatter, or strict markdown)
- Metadata: Explicit tags, types, dependencies
- Indexing: Derived (index.json, manifest.json) or hand-written (plan.md, CLAUDE.md)
- Version control: Disciplined branching, commit messages
- **Example**: `amieval/belief-collections/library/beliefs.json` (full schema, verification contracts)

**Hybrid** (DIRTWIRE, TOURLAB):
- Mix of ops/ folders (productivity-like) and code projects
- Metadata: Minimal — mostly README + CLAUDE.md

---

### 10. SPECIFIC FILES & PATHS

**Critical paths for OKF migration planning**:

| Path | Format | Metadata Density | Scope |
|------|--------|------------------|-------|
| `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/amieval/composable-beliefs/CLAUDE.md` | Markdown (generated) | Medium | Framework intro + conventions |
| `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/amieval/belief-collections/collections.json` | JSON | High | Cross-repo resolution map |
| `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain/index.json` | JSON (derived) | High | DAG index (sources + assertions + edges) |
| `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain/sources/*.md` | YAML markdown | High | Source ingestion + citation |
| `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/SECOND-BRAIN/firewaterairvoid/plan.md` | Markdown | Low | Index pointers (git is source of truth) |
| `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/DESIGN/.claude/settings.local.json` | JSON | Low | Claude Code config (not knowledge) |
| `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/SECOND-BRAIN/1181/I&I/Health/health.md` | Plain markdown | None | Personal knowledge (no schema) |

**Frontmatter samples (quoted)**:

From `SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain/assertions/markdown-canonical-with-derived-query-layer-is-optimal-path.md`:

content.text:
Based on my thorough exploration of the composable-beliefs codebase, here's the deep architectural understanding:

## 1. What is Composable Beliefs?

Composable Beliefs (CB) is a **persistent, source-grounded reasoning DAG for AI agents that survives session boundaries**. From `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/CB/composable-beliefs(also old)/CLAUDE.md` and `README.md`:

> "A paradigm for giving AI agents persistent, source-grounded, inspectable reasoning that survives session boundaries and composes into understanding the agent never explicitly derived."

The system is **not** a retrieval/RAG system—it's a **composition system**. The primary value is forcing adjacency: when you write both "loan period is 21 days" and "member m-1042 has 5 loans overdue" into the same graph, their overlap becomes structurally visible and implications write themselves.

## 2. The DAG Structure

**Nodes (three types):**
- **Primitive**: Atomic claims grounded in a single artifact (URI). Never edited, only superseded/retracted.
- **Compound**: Claims derived by composing 2+ other beliefs via `deps` array. `claim` + `deps` carries the meaning.
- **Implication**: Compound claims that identify actions/gaps. May be contract-grade (have `contract: true` with non-empty `rules`/`invariants`).

**Edges:** The `deps` array—each element is an ID pointing to upstream beliefs. Example from `examples/assertions.json` (line 144-147):

```json
"deps": [
  "a051",
  "a052"
],
```

**Epistemic overlays / provenance:** Expressed as *primitive beliefs with subjects array*. Example (lines 98-100):

```json
"subjects": [
  {"ref": "policy/loan-period", "type": "policy"}
],
```

The `subjects` field links a belief to entities it concerns, enabling subject-based queries (`mix bs subjects policy/loan-period`). There's no separate "epistemic overlay" file—provenance is baked into the primitive's `artifact` and `evidence` fields.

**Storage format:** Single JSON array at `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/CB/composable-beliefs(also old)/examples/assertions.json` (232 KB, 208+ beliefs). Loaded into Elixir as `CB.Belief` structs, indexed in memory by ID.

## 3. Provenance Docs

**Definition:** External source documents (policies, emails, analyses, transcripts) that back primitives.

**Location:** Conceptually live in `sources/` directory (per `/docs/assertion-dag.md` line 294-305), though the example repo doesn't populate it yet. Convention: `YYYY-MM-DD-slug.md`.

**Formats:**
- Cleaned markdown text (binary attachments stripped, quoted text trimmed)
- Email transcripts (From, To, Cc, Date, Subject metadata preserved)
- Analysis documents (agent/user-generated)
- External research (PDFs, papers, URLs)

**Concrete example:** The file `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/CB/composable-beliefs(also old)/analyses/2026-06-01-shared-provenance-shallow-clone-parable.md` demonstrates how provenance docs are authored—they reference beliefs inline to make relationships explicit. Line 24-25 mentions:

> "This is the CB thesis in miniature."

Analysis documents can reference beliefs directly (e.g., mentioning `a050`, `a054` assertions about agent patterns).

**Artifact URI schemes (from c040 contract, lines 284-290):**
```
gmail:<thread-id>      # Email threads
document:<repo-path>   # doc:policy/loan-policy-v3.md
source:<slug>          # Cached under sources/
user:<name>:<date>     # Direct user statements
session:<descriptor>   # Working session references
https:<URL>            # External URLs
```

## 4. Assertions Structure & Addressing

**What is an "assertion"?** A belief node in the DAG. IDs: `a001`–`a###` (primitives, compounds, implications); `c001`–`c###` (contracts).

**Full schema** is in `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/CB/composable-beliefs(also old)/lib/cb/belief.ex` (lines 48-71):

```elixir
@fields [
  :id,
  :type,
  :kind,
  :domain,
  :tags,
  :name,
  :who,
  :claim,
  :rules,
  :invariants,
  :contract,
  :artifact,
  :evidence,
  :subjects,
  :deps,
  :materialized,
  :status,
  :superseded_by,
  :retracted_on,
  :retracted_reason,
  :created,
  :_keys
]
```

**Concrete example - Primitive (a050, lines 2-26):**
```json
{
  "id": "a050",
  "type": "primitive",
  "kind": "reasoning-error",
  "tags": [],
  "who": "agent",
  "claim": "Agent performs lossy compression on unstructured data retrieval - omits fields without awareness that information is being lost",
  "artifact": "user:2026-03-12",
  "evidence": [
    {
      "date": "2026-03-12",
      "detail": "To editorialize, the agent must at least consider the information it is choosing not to present.",
      "artifact": "user:2026-03-12"
    }
  ],
  "subjects": [{"ref": "agent", "type": "agent"}],
  "deps": [],
  "status": "active",
  "created": "2026-03-12"
}
```

**Concrete example - Compound (a055, lines 132-149):**
```json
{
  "id": "a055",
  "type": "compound",
  "kind": "error-pattern",
  "claim": "Agent's self-reporting about its own behavior is systematically biased toward flattering interpretations",
  "deps": ["a051", "a052"],
  "status": "active",
  "created": "2026-03-12"
}
```

**Concrete example - Contract with rules/invariants (c038, lines 4795-4850):**
```json
{
  "id": "c038",
  "type": "implication",
  "kind": "schema",
  "contract": true,
  "claim": "Schema discipline: belief provenance is carried by an artifact field; ...",
  "rules": [
    "The artifact field is the canonical provenance carrier on beliefs...",
    "The kind field's value is null or one of the values declared by c039..."
  ],
  "invariants": [
    "For all beliefs b: b.contract == true implies b.type == 'implication'.",
    "For all beliefs b: ((b.rules // []) ++ (b.invariants // [])) non-empty iff b.contract == true."
  ],
  "deps": ["a397", "a398", ...],
  "status": "active",
  "created": "2026-05-15"
}
```

**Immutability & Status Lifecycle (per c029):**
- `active` — currently believed
- `superseded` — replaced (has `superseded_by: "id"` field pointing to replacement)
- `retracted` — withdrawn with reason (has `retracted_on` and `retracted_reason`)
- `retired` — contract no longer enforced (contracts-only)

Example: `a806` (14-day loan period) is marked `superseded` with `superseded_by: "a801"` (21-day period).

## 5. "The Desk" & `mix bs list`

**"The desk"** is the **belief shell**—a deterministic, pure-graph-query CLI with no LLM involved.

**Implemented in:** `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/CB/composable-beliefs(also old)/lib/mix/tasks/bs.ex` (417 lines).

**Commands:**
```bash
mix bs list [filters]         # List beliefs (active by default)
mix bs show <id>              # Full detail on one node
mix bs tree <id>              # Dependency tree visualization
mix bs deps <id>              # Direct dependencies (--deep for transitive)
mix bs dependents <id>        # Reverse lookup (--deep for transitive)
mix bs stale                  # Find beliefs with superseded/retracted deps
mix bs path <id1> <id2>       # Find connection between two beliefs
mix bs history <id>           # Supersession chain
mix bs subjects <ref|type>    # Find beliefs by subject
mix bs stats                  # Graph statistics
```

**Filters (for `list`):**
```
primitive|compound|implication   # By structural type
active|superseded|retracted|all  # By status
contracts                        # Contract-grade implications only
unlinked                         # Implications with no materialized items
tag:<tag>, kind:<kind>, domain:<domain>, subject_type:<type>
<namespace>/<ref>               # Filter by subject ref (e.g., policy/loan-period)
```

**Example queries:**
```bash
mix bs list domain:ops                    # All operational beliefs
mix bs list contracts                     # All contract-grade beliefs
mix bs show a810                          # Full detail on compound a810
mix bs tree a820                          # Full dependency tree from a820
mix bs subjects policy/loan-period        # All beliefs about loan-period policy
mix bs stale                              # Beliefs with bad deps
mix bs stats                              # Graph overview
```

**Stats output** (from `Graph.stats/1`, lines 231-280) aggregates:
- Total count by type (primitive, compound, implication)
- Counts by status (active, superseded, retracted, retired)
- Stale count (compounds/implications with superseded/retracted deps)
- Unlinked implications (candidates for `/materialize`)
- Artifact schemes distribution
- Dependency depth (max, mean)
- Most depended-on beliefs (hub nodes)

## 6. File Formats for Persistence

**Primary storage: JSON**
- **File:** `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/CB/composable-beliefs(also old)/examples/assertions.json`
- **Format:** Array of belief objects, pretty-printed (via Jason), byte-stable serialization (fields in canonical order per `@ordered_keys`)
- **Serialization:** Handled by `CB.Belief.to_map/1` (lines 197-209) and `CB.Belief.from_map/1` (lines 165-195)

**Secondary storage: Markdown**
- **Provenance docs:** `sources/YYYY-MM-DD-slug.md` (convention from `docs/assertion-dag.md` line 306)
- **Generated implications digest:** `examples/_implications.md` (per `CB.Config.implications_path/0`)
- **Example analyses:** `/analyses/*.md` (e.g., `2026-06-01-eval-design-depth-non-optional.md`)

**No frontmatter convention.** Markdown docs use YAML/metadata headers for human reference (Date, Type, Status, Origin), but these are not parsed by the system. The binding between a markdown doc and beliefs is the `artifact: "source:slug"` reference in primitives.

**Store layer** (`/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/CB/composable-beliefs(also old)/lib/cb/belief/store.ex`):
```elixir
def read do
  path = Config.assertions_path()
  if File.exists?(path) do
    with {:ok, data} <- JSON.read(path) do
      {:ok, Enum.map(data, &Belief.from_map/1)}
    end
  else
    {:ok, []}
  end
end

def write(assertions, path \\ nil) do
  path = path || Config.assertions_path()
  ordered = Enum.map(assertions, &Belief.to_map/1)
  content = Jason.encode!(ordered, pretty: true) <> "\n"
  JSON.write_atomic_raw(path, content)
end
```

Writes are atomic (tmpfile + rename) to prevent corruption.

## 7. Session State Persistence into the Graph

**Two mechanisms:**

### A. `/assert-session` skill
Located at `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/CB/composable-beliefs(also old)/skills/assert-session/SKILL.md`. Scans the current session conversation for:

1. **Domain rules stated by user** → Primitives with `artifact: "user:<owner>:YYYY-MM-DD"` and session evidence
2. **Agent reasoning errors caught by user** → Primitives with subject `{"ref": "agent", "type": "agent"}`, linking to existing error patterns (a050-a054)

Example flow (from `assert-session/SKILL.md` lines 56-59):
- User states: "members get a 21-day loan period on standard items"
- Creates primitive: `type: primitive`, `artifact: "user:owner:2026-03-12"`, evidence citing the session context
- Subjects: `[{"ref": "policy/loan-period", "type": "policy"}]`

### B. `/assert` skill
Located at `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/CB/composable-beliefs(also old)/skills/assert/SKILL.md`. Reads artifacts (docs, emails, entities) and creates beliefs:

1. **Identify primitives** - Extract non-reducible facts from source
2. **Scan for composition** - Compare against existing beliefs for cross-entity interactions
3. **Build compounds** - Beliefs that combine primitives
4. **Build implications** - Beliefs that surface actions/gaps
5. **Preflight** - Run `mix cb.preflight --file <tmp.json>` to check for schema conflicts
6. **Write** - Append to `examples/assertions.json`

**Preflight output buckets** (from `assert/SKILL.md` lines 51-58):
1. **Contract-level conflicts** - Block the write outright
2. **DAG-schema / non-contract conflicts** - Require user adjudication
3. **Supportive matches** - Dep candidates to tie in
4. **Neutral matches** - Informational

**Adjudication outcomes** for conflicts:
- `accept_supersede` - New belief replaces existing; existing gets `superseded` + `superseded_by` linkage
- `reject_dep_tie` - New belief reshaped to depend on existing as constraint
- `defer` - No write; conflict recorded for later

### C. Materialization
When an implication is turned into tasks via `/materialize`, the `materialized` field is populated:

```json
"materialized": {
  "date": "2024-09-25",
  "tasks": [
    {"object": "holds/h-2024-q1", "action": "Implement hold-expiry state transition handler"},
    {"object": "holds/h-2024-q1", "action": "Wire notification to next-in-queue on expiry"}
  ]
}
```

This creates bidirectional traceability: "why does this task exist?" traces to the implication, and the implication traces to its deps.

## 8. Self-Referential Schema (The Desk Meta-Design)

The DAG's own schema is expressed as contracts **inside the graph itself**. From `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/CB/composable-beliefs(also old)/docs/actualization.md` and `assertion-dag.md`:

| Contract | Purpose | Location |
|---|---|---|
| `c029` | Status lifecycle & immutability rules | lines 2765-2790+ |
| `c038` | Schema discipline (artifact, contract flag, enum fields) | lines 4795-4850 |
| `c039` | Closed enum of `belief.kind` values | lines 4852-4906 |
| `c040` | Closed enum of artifact URI schemes | (not shown, but referenced) |
| `c041` | Closed enum of `belief.domain` values | (not shown, but referenced) |

**Verification:** `mix cb.verify.schema` checks the Elixir struct (`CB.Belief`) against these contracts—code and declared schema cannot silently drift.

**Self-referential design consequence:** When the agent encodes beliefs about its *own* failure modes (e.g., `a050`: "Agent performs lossy compression"), those beliefs are subject to the same schema contracts as domain beliefs. A self-referential assertion must have an artifact, evidence, well-formed claim—no special epistemic dispensation.

---

## Quick Navigation

**Key files:**
- Schema definition: `/lib/cb/belief.ex`
- Belief DAG storage: `/lib/cb/belief/store.ex`
- Graph traversal: `/lib/cb/belief/graph.ex`
- Query CLI: `/lib/mix/tasks/bs.ex`
- Canonical docs: `/docs/assertion-dag.md`, `/docs/operations.md`, `/docs/actualization.md`
- Example graph: `/examples/assertions.json` (232 KB)
- Visual explorer: `/viewer.html` (standalone, no-build HTML5 canvas)
- Agent skills: `/skills/assert/`, `/skills/assert-session/`, `/skills/materialize/`, `/skills/assertions/`

**The system stands on these principles:**
1. Immutability with versioning (supersession, retraction, retirement)
2. Explicit composition (deps make reasoning traceable)
3. Artifact-grounded primitives (no confidence scores, only evidence)
4. Self-verifying schema (contracts in the graph itself)
5. Shared prosthetic (human + agent both work from same queryable structure)

content.text:
Based on my thorough exploration of the composable-beliefs codebase, here's the deep architectural understanding:

## 1. What is Composable Beliefs?

Composable Beliefs (CB) is a **persistent, source-grounded reasoning DAG for AI agents that survives session boundaries**. From `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/CB/composable-beliefs(also old)/CLAUDE.md` and `README.md`:

> "A paradigm for giving AI agents persistent, source-grounded, inspectable reasoning that survives session boundaries and composes into understanding the agent never explicitly derived."

The system is **not** a retrieval/RAG system—it's a **composition system**. The primary value is forcing adjacency: when you write both "loan period is 21 days" and "member m-1042 has 5 loans overdue" into the same graph, their overlap becomes structurally visible and implications write themselves.

## 2. The DAG Structure

**Nodes (three types):**
- **Primitive**: Atomic claims grounded in a single artifact (URI). Never edited, only superseded/retracted.
- **Compound**: Claims derived by composing 2+ other beliefs via `deps` array. `claim` + `deps` carries the meaning.
- **Implication**: Compound claims that identify actions/gaps. May be contract-grade (have `contract: true` with non-empty `rules`/`invariants`).

**Edges:** The `deps` array—each element is an ID pointing to upstream beliefs. Example from `examples/assertions.json` (line 144-147):

```json
"deps": [
  "a051",
  "a052"
],
```

**Epistemic overlays / provenance:** Expressed as *primitive beliefs with subjects array*. Example (lines 98-100):

```json
"subjects": [
  {"ref": "policy/loan-period", "type": "policy"}
],
```

The `subjects` field links a belief to entities it concerns, enabling subject-based queries (`mix bs subjects policy/loan-period`). There's no separate "epistemic overlay" file—provenance is baked into the primitive's `artifact` and `evidence` fields.

**Storage format:** Single JSON array at `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/CB/composable-beliefs(also old)/examples/assertions.json` (232 KB, 208+ beliefs). Loaded into Elixir as `CB.Belief` structs, indexed in memory by ID.

## 3. Provenance Docs

**Definition:** External source documents (policies, emails, analyses, transcripts) that back primitives.

**Location:** Conceptually live in `sources/` directory (per `/docs/assertion-dag.md` line 294-305), though the example repo doesn't populate it yet. Convention: `YYYY-MM-DD-slug.md`.

**Formats:**
- Cleaned markdown text (binary attachments stripped, quoted text trimmed)
- Email transcripts (From, To, Cc, Date, Subject metadata preserved)
- Analysis documents (agent/user-generated)
- External research (PDFs, papers, URLs)

**Concrete example:** The file `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/CB/composable-beliefs(also old)/analyses/2026-06-01-shared-provenance-shallow-clone-parable.md` demonstrates how provenance docs are authored—they reference beliefs inline to make relationships explicit. Line 24-25 mentions:

> "This is the CB thesis in miniature."

Analysis documents can reference beliefs directly (e.g., mentioning `a050`, `a054` assertions about agent patterns).

**Artifact URI schemes (from c040 contract, lines 284-290):**
```
gmail:<thread-id>      # Email threads
document:<repo-path>   # doc:policy/loan-policy-v3.md
source:<slug>          # Cached under sources/
user:<name>:<date>     # Direct user statements
session:<descriptor>   # Working session references
https:<URL>            # External URLs
```

## 4. Assertions Structure & Addressing

**What is an "assertion"?** A belief node in the DAG. IDs: `a001`–`a###` (primitives, compounds, implications); `c001`–`c###` (contracts).

**Full schema** is in `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/CB/composable-beliefs(also old)/lib/cb/belief.ex` (lines 48-71):

```elixir
@fields [
  :id,
  :type,
  :kind,
  :domain,
  :tags,
  :name,
  :who,
  :claim,
  :rules,
  :invariants,
  :contract,
  :artifact,
  :evidence,
  :subjects,
  :deps,
  :materialized,
  :status,
  :superseded_by,
  :retracted_on,
  :retracted_reason,
  :created,
  :_keys
]
```

**Concrete example - Primitive (a050, lines 2-26):**
```json
{
  "id": "a050",
  "type": "primitive",
  "kind": "reasoning-error",
  "tags": [],
  "who": "agent",
  "claim": "Agent performs lossy compression on unstructured data retrieval - omits fields without awareness that information is being lost",
  "artifact": "user:2026-03-12",
  "evidence": [
    {
      "date": "2026-03-12",
      "detail": "To editorialize, the agent must at least consider the information it is choosing not to present.",
      "artifact": "user:2026-03-12"
    }
  ],
  "subjects": [{"ref": "agent", "type": "agent"}],
  "deps": [],
  "status": "active",
  "created": "2026-03-12"
}
```

**Concrete example - Compound (a055, lines 132-149):**
```json
{
  "id": "a055",
  "type": "compound",
  "kind": "error-pattern",
  "claim": "Agent's self-reporting about its own behavior is systematically biased toward flattering interpretations",
  "deps": ["a051", "a052"],
  "status": "active",
  "created": "2026-03-12"
}
```

**Concrete example - Contract with rules/invariants (c038, lines 4795-4850):**
```json
{
  "id": "c038",
  "type": "implication",
  "kind": "schema",
  "contract": true,
  "claim": "Schema discipline: belief provenance is carried by an artifact field; ...",
  "rules": [
    "The artifact field is the canonical provenance carrier on beliefs...",
    "The kind field's value is null or one of the values declared by c039..."
  ],
  "invariants": [
    "For all beliefs b: b.contract == true implies b.type == 'implication'.",
    "For all beliefs b: ((b.rules // []) ++ (b.invariants // [])) non-empty iff b.contract == true."
  ],
  "deps": ["a397", "a398", ...],
  "status": "active",
  "created": "2026-05-15"
}
```

**Immutability & Status Lifecycle (per c029):**
- `active` — currently believed
- `superseded` — replaced (has `superseded_by: "id"` field pointing to replacement)
- `retracted` — withdrawn with reason (has `retracted_on` and `retracted_reason`)
- `retired` — contract no longer enforced (contracts-only)

Example: `a806` (14-day loan period) is marked `superseded` with `superseded_by: "a801"` (21-day period).

## 5. "The Desk" & `mix bs list`

```bash
mix bs path <id1> <id2>       # Find connection between two beliefs
mix bs history <id>           # Supersession chain
mix bs subjects <ref|type>    # Find beliefs by subject
mix bs stats                  # Graph statistics
```

**Filters (for `list`):**
```
primitive|compound|implication   # By structural type
active|superseded|retracted|all  # By status
contracts                        # Contract-grade implications only
unlinked                         # Implications with no materialized items
tag:<tag>, kind:<kind>, domain:<domain>, subject_type:<type>
<namespace>/<ref>               # Filter by subject ref (e.g., policy/loan-period)
```

**Example queries:**
```bash
mix bs list domain:ops                    # All operational beliefs
mix bs list contracts                     # All contract-grade beliefs
mix bs show a810                          # Full detail on compound a810
mix bs tree a820                          # Full dependency tree from a820
mix bs subjects policy/loan-period        # All beliefs about loan-period policy
mix bs stale                              # Beliefs with bad deps
mix bs stats                              # Graph overview
```

**Stats output** (from `Graph.stats/1`, lines 231-280) aggregates:
- Total count by type (primitive, compound, implication)
- Counts by status (active, superseded, retracted, retired)
- Stale count (compounds/implications with superseded/retracted deps)
- Unlinked implications (candidates for `/materialize`)
- Artifact schemes distribution
- Dependency depth (max, mean)
- Most depended-on beliefs (hub nodes)

## 6. File Formats for Persistence

**Primary storage: JSON**
- **File:** `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/CB/composable-beliefs(also old)/examples/assertions.json`
- **Format:** Array of belief objects, pretty-printed (via Jason), byte-stable serialization (fields in canonical order per `@ordered_keys`)
- **Serialization:** Handled by `CB.Belief.to_map/1` (lines 197-209) and `CB.Belief.from_map/1` (lines 165-195)

**Secondary storage: Markdown**
- **Provenance docs:** `sources/YYYY-MM-DD-slug.md` (convention from `docs/assertion-dag.md` line 306)
- **Generated implications digest:** `examples/_implications.md` (per `CB.Config.implications_path/0`)
- **Example analyses:** `/analyses/*.md` (e.g., `2026-06-01-eval-design-depth-non-optional.md`)

**No frontmatter convention.** Markdown docs use YAML/metadata headers for human reference (Date, Type, Status, Origin), but these are not parsed by the system. The binding between a markdown doc and beliefs is the `artifact: "source:slug"` reference in primitives.

**Store layer** (`/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/CB/composable-beliefs(also old)/lib/cb/belief/store.ex`):
```elixir
def read do
  path = Config.assertions_path()
  if File.exists?(path) do
    with {:ok, data} <- JSON.read(path) do
      {:ok, Enum.map(data, &Belief.from_map/1)}
    end

end

def write(assertions, path \\ nil) do
  path = path || Config.assertions_path()
```

## 7. Session State Persistence into the Graph

**Two mechanisms:**

### A. `/assert-session` skill

- Creates primitive: `type: primitive`, `artifact: "user:owner:2026-03-12"`, evidence citing the session context
- Subjects: `[{"ref": "policy/loan-period", "type": "policy"}]`

### B. `/assert` skill

1. **Identify primitives** - Extract non-reducible facts from source

attachment.prompt:
see this supplementary analysis:
Part 1 - OKF and Composable Beliefs

  What each thing actually is

  OKF is a transport/interchange layer. Strip away the Google framing and it is: markdown files + YAML frontmatter, one required field (type), untyped cross-links,
  optional index.md and log.md, packaged as a tarball/git repo. It is explicitly "minimally opinionated... a format, not a platform." It is the productized
  standardization of Karpathy's LLM-wiki - same three-layer shape (raw / wiki / schema), same index.md + log.md conventions, even credits Karpathy by name. Its
  design goal is the lowest common denominator that "anyone can produce without an SDK, anyone can consume without an integration."

  CB is an epistemic-semantics layer. It is not really competing for the same job. OKF answers "how do I serialize and ship knowledge documents portably?" CB
  answers "what does a claim mean, what does it depend on, on what evidence, is it still true, and what does it obligate?" Typed claims
  (primitive/compound/implication), immutability + dated supersession, provenance with artifact + evidence[], staleness detection over a real dependency DAG, four
  machine-checkable contract interpreters, a self-describing schema expressed as contracts inside the graph, and a deterministic LLM-free read path. None of that
  is in OKF's remit, by OKF's own choice.

  Does CB layer above OKF? Yes - cleanly.

  The layering is almost textbook:

  ┌────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │    Concern     │         OKF / Karpathy-wiki         │                                       CB                                        │
  ├────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Packaging      │ markdown + frontmatter, tarball/git │ its own JSON graph (assertions.json)                                            │
  ├────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Identity       │ file path                           │ belief id                                                                       │
  ├────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Relations      │ untyped markdown links              │ typed, load-bearing deps edges                                                  │
  ├────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Classification │ type (free, producer-defined)       │ kind/domain as closed enums enforced by contracts                               │
  ├────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Provenance     │ resource URL + timestamp            │ artifact URI + evidence[] with detail                                           │
  ├────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ History        │ append-only log.md (prose)          │ immutable supersession chain (bs history), staleness propagation                │
  ├────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Rules          │ none (deliberately)                 │ contracts: state-machine / enum / derivation-table / implies, compiled to tests │
  ├────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Query          │ "any search tool"                   │ deterministic graph traversal, conflict-preflight                               │
  └────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  CB can serialize down to OKF (every belief is trivially a typed markdown doc; deps become cross-links; supersession becomes log entries). OKF cannot reconstruct
  CB up without inventing exactly the discipline CB adds - the up-conversion is lossy. That is the definition of CB sitting above it.

  The one wrinkle: CB does not currently speak OKF. It uses its own JSON. So the relationship today is "conceptual superset, separate serialization."

  What is genuinely redundant

  If OKF is adopted, several CB-adjacent things become commodity baseline rather than differentiators:

  - The markdown-wiki packaging and the "format not platform / no SDK / lives in git / human-readable + agent-parseable" philosophy. CB's direction repo makes
  nearly identical arguments ("every analogous layer won as an open standard: git, JSON Schema, OpenTelemetry, MCP"). OKF just won that argument first for the
  document layer.
  - index.md / log.md overlap CB's implications-summary and bs history.
  - type / tags / timestamp / resource overlap kind / domain / evidence date / artifact.

  The strategically correct read: OKF becoming a standard is good for CB, not a threat. It validates the "format-not-platform" thesis CB already holds, and it
  hands CB a free on-ramp and a serialization target. The obvious move is a CB↔OKF producer/consumer: emit OKF bundles for interop, ingest OKF bundles as
  primitives. CB then markets itself as "the typed, verifiable, supersedable dialect of OKF" - exactly the eval-evidence-ledger positioning the direction repo
  already chose.

  The real risk OKF poses

  It raises the floor CB must clear. The crux your own bet-dossier.md flags - does structured substrate decisively beat strong plain-English instructions? - gets
  harder when the "plain" baseline is now a standardized, agent-maintained OKF wiki with cross-links and history. OKF gives people 70% of the felt value (portable,
  persistent, linkable, agent-curated) for ~0% of the discipline cost. CB has to demonstrate that the remaining 30% - immutability, typed deps, contracts,
  determinism, self-reference - earns its keep on tasks that matter. OKF doesn't refute CB; it sharpens the bar CB was already going to be measured against.

aiTitle: Design knowledge base system using Open Knowledge Format

The key unresolved questions are around the metadata-summary-for-agents piece, whether the wiki should be the agent's reference or just human-readable presentation, and how to centralize the currently scattered thread/ingestion format. I should build on their Part 1 analysis rather than repeat it, and give concrete recommendations on the design boundaries and implementation approach. The missing piece is a generated manifest that aggregates all frontmatter into a single index the agent loads first—each OKF doc gets a concise `description` field (like skill descriptions) that lets the agent decide relevance without reading the full body, then loads only what it needs. This mirrors the MEMORY.md pattern the user already uses. For the wiki itself, I'm thinking about the Karpathy model: the OKF bundle becomes the agent's direct reference source, while the rendered HTML site is just a derived human-facing projection—never the source of truth, always regenerated from the bundle.

The real leverage is synthesizing concept pages at ingest time rather than making the agent re-derive on every query, which maps cleanly onto the CB primitives and compounds structure. Now for the messiest part: consolidating all the scattered artifacts—threads, plans, analyses, design docs—under a unified OKF taxonomy with consistent frontmatter (type, title, description, tags, timestamp, status, links), so everything from a session log to a decision memo lives in the same structure and feeds into one queryable index.

Threads are the biggest gap right now—they're semi-haphazard and need a home. An OKF thread doc captures what was decided and links to downstream plans and analyses, then beliefs extracted from that thread cite it as a source artifact, creating a clean pipeline from session to OKF substrate to CB epistemic layer. On the bridge between CB and OKF: rather than rewriting, build it as a serialization adapter where CB keeps assertions.json as its working store but emits OKF bundles and ingests OKF docs as primitive artifacts—that's the actionable move that avoids the "raises the floor" risk.

Actually, OKF gives CB something better: a standardized, agent-maintained baseline for the dag-versus-prose evaluation. The prose baseline was hand-wavy before; now it's concrete and credible, which strengthens the experiment design itself. On their identity model, file-path identity is fragile once docs start cross-linking heavily—CB's stable IDs are worth adopting even in a lite OKF tier for anything that gets restructured, though file paths work fine for low-churn personal domains.

For the response: I should lead with a direct recommendation, then address each question using structure to keep it tight without being shallow. They want analysis and a call, not throat-clearing. I have enough detail on the OKF structure—required `type` field, optional standardized fields like title and description, the index.md and log.md pattern, plain markdown links for cross-references. Time to write the synthesis.

content.text:
I've now got all three external sources, the CB architecture, the repo survey, and your Part 1 analysis. Your Part 1 layering is correct — I won't restate it. Let me build on it and answer the questions it didn't close.

## Verdict

Adopt OKF — but as a **floor, not a ceiling**, and explicitly as a two-tier system where CB is the opt-in upper tier. The reason this is low-risk: you've already independently reinvented OKF. `SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain` is markdown-canonical + YAML frontmatter + a derived `index.json` DAG. That *is* OKF with different field names. So "adopt OKF" is mostly **renaming a pattern you've already validated and stopping the per-repo drift**, not importing a foreign architecture.

The survey found five incompatible patterns across 45 repos (`plan.md` vs `USER_STORIES.md` vs `DESIGN.md`; `CLAUDE.md` vs `claude.md`; JSON-canonical vs YAML-canonical vs prose). OKF's value here isn't its cleverness — it's that it's *minimal enough that all five can converge to it cheaply*, and it's a published standard so you stop reinventing the field names.

## The two tiers (this is the load-bearing decision)

| | **OKF tier (floor)** | **CB tier (ceiling)** |
|---|---|---|
| Domains | Finances, house, personal, tutorials, band ops, most docs/plans/threads | Evals, policies, anything adjudicated or whose claims must stay true over time |
| Discipline | frontmatter + cross-links + index | typed deps, immutability, supersession, contracts |
| Cost | ~0 (write markdown) | real (assert/preflight/adjudicate) |
| When | default for everything | opt-in when a claim's *correctness over time* matters |

Forcing CB-grade discipline onto "when is the furnace filter due" is the failure mode to avoid. Most productivity knowledge wants OKF-level structure and nothing more. CB earns its cost only where claims get superseded and contradictions are expensive. Keep the floor cheap so it actually gets adopted; reserve the ceiling for where it pays.

## Your metadata-summary question — yes, and you already run this exact pattern

You asked whether OKF can give an agent a high-level understanding without loading the full document, "like Claude skills." It can, and the mechanism is already in this session:

- **Claude skills** load only a `name` + `description` line; the body loads on demand.
- **Your `MEMORY.md`** is one pointer line per memory (`- [Title](file.md) — hook`); the memory body loads only when relevant.

OKF's native version of this is **`index.md` per directory** (progressive disclosure as the agent descends) plus the frontmatter `description` field. Vanilla OKF stops there. The piece you want to add is a **single generated top-level manifest** that harvests every doc's frontmatter (`type`, `title`, `description`, `tags`, `timestamp`, `status`) into one small file the agent loads *first*. Then:

```
load manifest (cheap, all descriptions) → match by description/tags → load only the bodies you need
```

So the convention to enforce is: **every OKF doc's `description` is written as a relevance hook, not a summary** — the same discipline that makes a skill `description` work ("use this when…"). The manifest is build-generated, never hand-edited (same as `index.json` today). This is the highest-leverage single addition and it's a ~50-line generator, not new architecture.

## Wiki: reference or presentation? — Reference. The human wiki is a throwaway projection.

This is the Karpathy distinction and OKF deliberately collapses it: **the OKF bundle is both the agent's reference and its ingestion format — same files.** The agent ingests the OKF docs directly, navigating by the manifest/`description`. It does *not* read a separately-authored human wiki.

The HTML visualizer (Cytoscape graph) is the *human* surface — derived, regenerated from the bundle, never a source of truth. **Do not maintain two artifacts.** One bundle; the wiki is `render(bundle)`.

The one piece of Karpathy worth keeping deliberately: he compiles *synthesis* into entity/concept pages at **ingest** time so the agent doesn't re-derive on every query. So the bundle should hold synthesized concept pages (the "wiki layer") grounded in immutable raw sources (layer 1). The agent reads synthesis by default, drills to raw sources only for provenance. That maps onto CB exactly: **CB primitives cite raw artifacts; CB compounds *are* the synthesis layer.** The wiki-vs-raw split and the CB primitive-vs-compound split are the same split.

## Centralizing the haphazard threads/plans/analyses — the real win

This is where OKF pays off most, because it's your worst-organized layer. The move: every persisted artifact gets the **same frontmatter spine** and a `type`, so one manifest spans all of them. A starter taxonomy from what your repos actually produce:

- `thread` — a persisted session (what was decided/done), `description` = the outcome, links to what it produced
- `plan` — supersedes `plan.md`/`USER_STORIES.md`/`DESIGN.md` chaos
- `analysis` — your dated `analyses/*.md`
- `source` — ingested external (already exists in second-brain)
- `position`/`decision` — settled stances (you already have `positions/` + a `/position` skill)
- `reference` — docs/runbooks

The thread case is the specific gap you named ("threads persisted semi-haphazardly"). Under OKF a session emits a `thread` doc whose `description` lets a future agent find it *without replaying the transcript*, with links to the plans/analyses/beliefs it spawned.

**And this is exactly how OKF sits below CB.** The pipeline:

```
session  →  OKF `thread` doc (artifact, raw-ish)
         →  /assert extracts CB primitives grounded in that artifact (artifact: session:… / source:…)
         →  CB compounds synthesize across artifacts
         →  CB emits OKF bundle back out for interop / human wiki
```

OKF is the **artifact substrate** (the things beliefs point at via `artifact:`); CB is the **epistemic overlay** on top. Your CB artifact URI schemes (`source:`, `document:`, `session:`) are *already* designed to reference exactly these OKF docs. The bridge is a serialization adapter, not a rewrite — CB keeps `assertions.json` as its working store and gains `emit-okf` / `ingest-okf`.

## On your Part 1 — agree, three sharpenings

1. **Build the bridge as an adapter, not a migration.** "Conceptual superset, separate serialization" is right. CB shouldn't switch its store to markdown; it should gain OKF import/export. assertions.json stays the source of truth; OKF is the interchange skin.

2. **OKF is a gift to your eval design, not just a raised floor.** Your `bet-dossier.md` crux — *does structured substrate beat strong plain-English?* — previously had a hand-wavy prose baseline. OKF now *defines* that baseline: a standardized, agent-maintained, cross-linked, history-bearing wiki. That makes "dag-vs-prose" a cleaner, more credible experiment — the control is no longer a strawman. The risk ("OKF gives 70% for 0% discipline cost") is real, but it's the same fact as "the eval now has a legitimate baseline." Lean into it: run the eval *with the OKF wiki as the prose arm*.

3. **One push-back on the table: identity.** You list OKF identity = file path. File-path identity is fragile under restructuring (renames break cross-links) — and your repos restructure constantly (the `CB-RESTRUCTURE-*.md` files prove it). So the graduated path is: OKF-lite with file-path identity for low-churn personal domains (Finances, house), but **stable IDs the moment a domain starts heavy cross-linking or restructuring** — which is the CB-style id. Identity stability, not contracts, is the first thing you'll want to pull *down* from the CB tier into the OKF tier.

---

Concrete next artifact if you want it: a **`KNOWLEDGE.md` spec** (the OKF profile for your repos — field conventions, the type taxonomy, the manifest generator contract, and the two-tier boundary rule) plus a one-repo pilot — I'd pilot on `SECOND-BRAIN/1181` (Finances/Home) since it's pure productivity and currently has zero schema, so it's the cleanest test of the floor. Want me to draft that spec, or pressure-test the two-tier boundary further first?

content: You're deciding whether to build a generalized OKF-based knowledge base across all your repos, with CB as the verifiable upper tier. I recommended a two-tier system (OKF floor, CB ceiling) and offered to draft a KNOWLEDGE.md spec piloted on SECOND-BRAIN/1181. Next: confirm whether to draft that spec. (disable recaps in /config)

First, I'm clarifying whether the synthesis layer serves as the human-facing wiki that both agents and humans reference initially, with the ability to drill down to source materials when needed, and whether synthesis documents follow OKF formatting. Then I'm examining how compound synthesis works in the Karpathy approach—specifically whether wiki and synthesis specifications are defined in OKF or exist only in Karpathy itself.

I'm also considering whether inserting compound synthesis into the synthesis layer represents a genuine improvement or unnecessary complexity. For the identity question, I need to determine if using IDs creates a conflict with OKF's path-as-identity principle and whether this approach remains technically compatible with OKF's schema. Finally, I'm planning to build a demo knowledge repository—a private repo containing the specification, methodology, documentation, and structure that would allow a fresh agent to adopt or convert the system.

Let me be precise about the architecture: there are three distinct layers here. The synthesis or wiki layer consists of compiled knowledge documents in OKF format—markdown with frontmatter—that both agents and humans read as their primary reference. The raw sources form the foundation layer and can either be OKF-wrapped or simply referenced. The HTML visualizer is purely a rendering of the bundle, not a separate authored layer—it's just a human-friendly presentation derived from the same OKF files. So the synthesis documents themselves are the shared reference point for both agent and human consumption, with the ability to trace back to sources when deeper context is needed. Yes, synthesis docs are OKF formatted.

Regarding Karpathy's synthesis approach: at ingest time, the LLM reads new raw sources and updates entity and concept pages, resolving contradictions and strengthening cross-references while maintaining index.md and log.md. The synthesis is performed as a bookkeeping pass by the model—it's prose synthesis maintained continuously without typing, dependencies, or immutability constraints. OKF itself specifies the format (markdown with frontmatter, index.md, log.md, cross-links) and includes a reference enrichment agent for drafting concept docs, but the spec doesn't mandate a specific synthesis methodology. OKF is format-agnostic; the synthesis discipline comes from Karpathy's wiki-maintenance loop, while the synthesis-as-verifiable-epistemics dimension comes from compound synthesis. Now I'm weighing whether adding compound synthesis to this process is a meaningful improvement or overengineering.

The honest answer is that it depends on the domain. For most synthesis work—personal notes, productivity systems—Karpathy's prose approach is sufficient and compound synthesis would be overkill. Compound synthesis only justifies itself when contradictions need mechanical detection rather than relying on LLM judgment, when you need to track why a claim holds and whether it's become stale due to superseded dependencies, or when the synthesis will be audited or adjudicated. Karpathy's method resolves contradictions by having the LLM rewrite the page, so old claims disappear into prose; compound synthesis's value is that supersession gets recorded with dates and staleness propagates automatically. For something like a furnace filter, this doesn't matter—but for loan policies or evaluation results, it matters significantly. The right framing is to use Karpathy-style prose synthesis as the baseline and promote to compound synthesis only where mechanical contradiction-detection and dependency staleness tracking actually matter. Inserting compound synthesis everywhere is overengineering; inserting it selectively is the whole point of the two-tier approach.

On the identity question: OKF's model uses file paths as identity with markdown links for cross-references, and while `id` fields aren't required, frontmatter is producer-extensible, so adding an `id` field is schema-legal. However, there's a real tension with OKF's intention—the whole point is that anyone can consume the files without special tooling, just following markdown links. If you introduce `id`-based linking, a naive OKF consumer won't resolve those links. The clean solution is to keep both: maintain path-based links for generic OKF compatibility while adding `id` and typed dependencies for your compound synthesis layer. The `id` becomes a stable anchor in frontmatter that your tooling uses to rebind path links when files are renamed, so it's additive rather than a replacement.

Now I'm moving to the next concrete task: building the knowledge repo demo set. I'm laying out the directory structure: a README and adoption guides at the root, the spec documentation in a dedicated folder covering the OKF profile and field reference, a working demo bundle that shows the format in practice, and tooling for generating the manifest index. This way the repo dogfoods its own format—it's a living example of what it prescribes.

I'm narrowing the scope to what's essential: the orientation and spec docs, a handful of templates, a small but genuine demo with finances and home sections plus metadata about the system itself, the entry points for adoption and conversion, and a manifest generator. That's a solid first cut without overextending.

Before I build the files, I should answer the three conceptual questions in prose since they directly shape the spec—particularly around whether CB is selective and whether identifiers are additive. Those answers become the actual spec content, so I'll tackle them first, then construct the repo reflecting those decisions. For the manifest generator, Python with only standard library makes sense since any agent should be able to run it without dependencies, even though the broader ecosystem leans Elixir. I'm mapping out the type taxonomy, the three-layer synthesis model (raw sources → synthesis docs → manifest index), and the two-tier rule that anchors everything between OKF/Karpathy prose and CB. The description field needs to be a tight relevance hook—under two lines, written so an agent can decide whether to load the full document. I'm planning the template types: thread, analysis, source, plan, position, reference, concept, and index. For the demo bundle, I'll make it genuinely useful with real examples—finances docs covering household cashflow synthesis and statements, home docs with furnace maintenance and systems overview, and a meta section that documents the design session itself using the format it's defining. The manifest will be generated to show what the tool produces, and the repo elegantly documents its own genesis.

Now I'm deciding whether to write the manifest generator script and actually run it to produce a real manifest.json, which would verify the tool works end-to-end. I have enough information to act, so I'll answer the three conceptual questions concisely, build out the repo files, run the generator, and report back. I'm settling on the path and will batch independent writes in parallel to keep this efficient. Let me start with the conceptual answers, then move into the builds.

The synthesis layer isn't two separate bodies of content — it's one: the OKF markdown docs that both agents and humans reference, drilling to sources when needed. The "wiki" website is just a rendered view of those docs, carrying no content the markdown doesn't already have. Synthesis docs live in the bundle itself as concept pages, while raw sources are captured as immutable `type: source` docs. The distinction is role, not location.

On the three approaches: Karpathy's synthesis is an LLM bookkeeping loop where the model updates entity and concept pages on each new source, resolving contradictions by rewriting, and maintains index.md and log.md — all prose, model-maintained, untyped. OKF specifies the format and ships a producer agent but doesn't mandate the synthesis methodology; it inherits Karpathy's conventions but leaves coherence to the producer. CB takes synthesis further with typed compounds, recorded dependencies, dated supersession, and mechanical contradiction detection. Whether that's improvement or overengineering depends on the domain — Karpathy-prose synthesis is sufficient for personal and productivity use, while CB earns its cost only in domains where contradictions are frequent and stakes are high.

On identity: OKF requires only `type`, and adding `id` to the frontmatter is schema-legal and producer-extensible. The key is keeping both path links and ids in parallel — path links stay canonical so generic OKF consumers can navigate with plain markdown, while `id` becomes an additive stability anchor for typed dependencies and rebinding after renames. You only break OKF if you drop path links entirely and rely solely on ids. So `id` is an OKF-legal tier-2 field, not a replacement, and it's the first CB feature worth pulling down to the floor since repos restructure constantly.

content.text:
Good questions — they each change the spec, so let me answer precisely first, then build the repo.

**1. Synthesis layer = human wiki? Mostly yes, with one correction.** There aren't two bodies of content (agent docs + a separate human wiki). There's **one** body — the synthesis docs, which *are* OKF markdown — and both agent and human read it. The "wiki" as a website is just `render(those docs)`: a throwaway view carrying no content the markdown doesn't. So: yes, both reference synthesis first and drill to raw sources only when needed; and yes, synthesis docs are themselves OKF. Don't picture synthesis as a folder separate from "the bundle" — the synthesis docs *are* the bundle's concept pages; raw sources sit alongside them as `type: source` docs. The difference is role, not location: sources are immutable captures, concept docs are the compiled/maintained synthesis over them.

**2. Karpathy vs OKF vs CB on synthesis — and is CB overengineering?**
- **Karpathy**: synthesis is an LLM *bookkeeping loop*. Each new source → the model updates entity/concept pages, resolves contradictions by rewriting, bumps `index.md` + `log.md`. Prose, model-maintained, untyped.
- **OKF**: specifies the **format** and ships a producer agent, but does **not** mandate a synthesis methodology. The `index.md`/`log.md` conventions are inherited *from Karpathy*; "keep it coherent" is left to the producer. So synthesis-as-process is Karpathy's, not OKF's.
- **CB**: synthesis becomes typed compounds with recorded `deps`, dated supersession, propagating staleness, mechanical contradiction detection.

Improvement *or* overengineering — both, by domain, which is exactly the two-tier line. Karpathy-prose synthesis is the floor and is **enough** for personal/productivity. The tell: Karpathy "resolves" a contradiction by rewriting the page — the old claim just vanishes. CB *records and dates* the supersession and propagates staleness to dependents. If nobody will ever ask "when did that change and what depended on it," CB is overengineering there. Put CB under the ~10% that gets adjudicated (evals, policies), prose-synthesize the rest.

**3. id vs path identity — does it break OKF?** OKF requires only `type`; frontmatter is producer-extensible, so adding `id` is **schema-legal** — still OKF at the file level. The conflict is with the *intent*: OKF uses path-as-identity so a dumb consumer follows plain markdown links with zero integration. Resolution = keep **both** (mirrors your Part 1 "untyped links vs typed deps" row): path links stay canonical so generic OKF tools navigate; `id` is an **additive** stability anchor your tooling uses for typed deps and to rebind path links after renames. You only leave strict OKF if you *drop* path links and rely solely on ids. So id is additive, not a replacement — and it's the first CB feature to pull down into the floor, since your repos restructure constantly.

Now building the `knowledge` repo. Let me confirm the path is clear and scaffold it.

input.content:
# knowledge

A portable, agent- and human-readable knowledge methodology for use across all my
repos and domains (software projects **and** productivity domains — finances, home,
band ops, personal). This repo is **both the specification and a working example of
itself**: the `bundle/` directory is a real knowledge bundle written in the format
the spec defines.

Point a fresh agent here and say one of:

- **"Adopt this methodology for this repo"** → the agent reads [`ADOPT.md`](ADOPT.md).
- **"Convert this existing knowledge store to this format"** → the agent reads [`CONVERT.md`](CONVERT.md).

## What this is, in one breath

Knowledge is **plain markdown files + YAML frontmatter, organized in a directory
hierarchy, with a generated manifest as the agent's entry index.** It is
[Open Knowledge Format (OKF)](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/knowledge-catalog/tree/main/okf)
as the floor, [Karpathy's LLM-wiki](https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f)
as the synthesis discipline, and [Composable Beliefs (CB)](#tiers) as an opt-in
ceiling for knowledge that must stay provably true over time.

## Why it exists

Across ~45 repos, persisted knowledge had drifted into five incompatible patterns
(`plan.md` vs `USER_STORIES.md` vs `DESIGN.md`; `CLAUDE.md` vs `claude.md`;
JSON-canonical vs YAML-canonical vs prose; threads persisted ad hoc). This gives all
of them one cheap shape to converge on, so a fresh agent can build context from any
repo the same way.

## Layout

```
knowledge/
├── README.md            ← you are here
├── ADOPT.md             ← entry point: "adopt this methodology"
├── CONVERT.md           ← entry point: "convert an existing store"
├── spec/
│   ├── KNOWLEDGE.md     ← THE spec (read this first)
│   ├── frontmatter.md   ← the frontmatter spine: field reference
│   ├── types.md         ← the concept-type taxonomy
│   └── tiers.md         ← the OKF floor vs CB ceiling boundary rule
├── templates/           ← copy-paste starting points, one per type
├── tools/
│   └── build_manifest.py  ← regenerates bundle/manifest.json (stdlib only)
└── bundle/              ← a real, self-demonstrating knowledge bundle
    ├── index.md         ← progressive-disclosure root
    ├── manifest.json    ← GENERATED skills-style summary index (do not hand-edit)
    ├── finances/        ← productivity domain (OKF floor)
    ├── home/            ← productivity domain (OKF floor)
    └── meta/            ← knowledge about this system itself (incl. CB-tier docs)
```

## The reading order

1. [`spec/KNOWLEDGE.md`](spec/KNOWLEDGE.md) — the model and its three layers.
2. [`spec/frontmatter.md`](spec/frontmatter.md) — the fields.
3. [`spec/types.md`](spec/types.md) — what kinds of documents exist.
4. [`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md) — when to stay cheap (OKF) vs when to add rigor (CB).
5. [`bundle/`](bundle/) — see all of it actually used.

input.content:
---
type: spec
title: The Knowledge Methodology
description: The canonical spec — three layers, the frontmatter spine, the manifest index, and the OKF/CB two-tier rule. Read this first when adopting or converting.
tags: [spec, okf, cb, methodology]
status: active
timestamp: 2026-06-21
id: spec:knowledge
---

# The Knowledge Methodology

## 1. Lineage (what this is built from)

| Layer | Source | What we take |
|---|---|---|
| Format | **OKF** (Open Knowledge Format) | markdown + YAML frontmatter, directory hierarchy, `index.md` progressive disclosure, cross-links, "format not platform" |
| Synthesis discipline | **Karpathy LLM-wiki** | three layers (raw → wiki → schema), ingest-time synthesis, `index.md`/`log.md`, compounding artifact |
| Epistemic rigor (opt-in) | **Composable Beliefs (CB)** | typed claims, stable ids, typed `deps`, dated supersession, staleness propagation, contracts |

The floor is OKF-compliant: every file is plain markdown any editor renders and any
search tool indexes. The ceiling (CB) is **additive frontmatter** on the same files —
it never breaks OKF compatibility (see §6).

## 2. The three layers

This is the Karpathy shape, made concrete.

```
┌─ Layer 3: SCHEMA ──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  This spec + CLAUDE.md conventions. How the bundle is built.    │
├─ Layer 2: SYNTHESIS (the "wiki") ──────────────────────────────┤
│  Compiled concept docs. Maintained at ingest time. OKF markdown.│
│  ← Both agent AND human read THIS first. ─────────────────────  │
├─ Layer 1: SOURCES ─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Immutable captures (type: source). Drilled into only when the  │
│  synthesis is insufficient or provenance is in question.        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**There is no separate "human wiki."** The synthesis docs *are* the wiki, and they are
OKF markdown that both the agent and a human read. A rendered website (e.g. the OKF
HTML visualizer) is just `render(bundle)` — a throwaway view, never authored, never a
source of truth.

**Default read path:** agent loads `manifest.json` → matches by `description` → loads
the synthesis docs it needs → drills to Layer-1 sources only for provenance or detail.

## 3. The frontmatter spine

Every document carries the same frontmatter spine. Only `type` is strictly required
(OKF rule); the rest are strongly recommended. Full field reference:
[`frontmatter.md`](frontmatter.md).

```yaml
---
type: concept            # REQUIRED. one of the taxonomy types (see types.md)
title: Household cashflow # human label
description: >           # RELEVANCE HOOK — see §4. the most important field.
  Use when reasoning about monthly money in/out, recurring obligations, or runway.
tags: [finances, recurring, runway]
status: active           # active | superseded | retracted | draft
timestamp: 2026-06-21    # last meaningful update (ISO date)
# --- tier-2 (CB) fields, optional, additive ---
id: fin:cashflow         # stable identity (see §6)
deps: [fin:income-sources, fin:fixed-costs]   # typed dependencies
---
```

## 4. `description` is a relevance hook, not a summary

This is the mechanism that lets an agent understand what a doc holds **without loading
it** — the same trick as a Claude skill's `description` or a `MEMORY.md` one-liner.

- Write it as **"Use when…" / "Covers…"** — what question this doc answers.
- ≤ 2 lines. If you need more, the doc is doing too much; split it.
- It must be true and discriminating: an agent decides *load or skip* from this line
  alone, so a vague description silently costs context on every traversal.

Bad: `description: Notes about money.`
Good: `description: Use when reasoning about monthly money in/out, recurring obligations, or runway.`

## 5. The manifest (the summary index)

`bundle/manifest.json` is a **generated** harvest of every doc's frontmatter into one
small file the agent loads first. It is the skills-style summary layer. Contract:

- Produced by [`tools/build_manifest.py`](../tools/build_manifest.py); **never hand-edited**.
- One entry per `.md` doc: `path`, `type`, `title`, `description`, `tags`, `status`,
  `timestamp`, and (if present) `id`, `deps`, `tier`.
- Small by construction — bodies are excluded. An agent reads the whole manifest
  cheaply, then loads only the bodies whose `description` matches its task.
- Regenerated on every write to the bundle (CI or a pre-commit hook; for now, run the
  tool by hand — see ADOPT.md).

`index.md` files complement the manifest for **human** progressive disclosure (browse
a folder, read its index, descend). The manifest is for the agent; `index.md` is for
the human walking the tree. Both are derived from the same docs.

## 6. Identity: path-canonical, id-additive

OKF identity is the **file path**, and cross-links are plain markdown links to paths —
so a generic OKF consumer navigates with zero integration. Keep that as canonical.

`id` is an **additive** tier-2 field (legal OKF — only `type` is required). Your tooling
uses it for two things generic tools can't do:

1. **Typed `deps`** that survive prose (the CB edge), and
2. **Rebinding path links after a rename** — the id is the stable anchor.

Rule: **never drop the path links.** A doc that links *only* by id is no longer OKF.
So: path links canonical (OKF stays intact), id additive (CB tier rides on top). `id`
is the first CB feature worth pulling down into the floor, because these repos
restructure constantly and renames otherwise break cross-links.

## 7. Synthesis: how Layer 2 is maintained

**Default (OKF/Karpathy floor):** when a new source lands, the agent updates the
relevant concept docs in place — integrating the new fact, resolving contradictions by
rewriting, refreshing cross-links and the folder `index.md`. Prose, model-maintained.
This is enough for almost everything.

**Ceiling (CB):** for knowledge that will be *audited or adjudicated*, synthesis is not
a rewrite — it is a typed compound belief with recorded `deps`, dated supersession, and
propagating staleness. The contradiction is not erased; it is dated and superseded, and
dependents are flagged stale. Use this only where someone will later ask "when did this
change and what depended on it." The boundary rule is in [`tiers.md`](tiers.md).

## 8. What a bundle looks like

```
bundle/
├── index.md          # root: what's here, links to domain indexes
├── manifest.json     # generated
├── <domain>/
│   ├── index.md       # domain landing: progressive disclosure
│   ├── <concept>.md   # type: concept   (Layer 2 synthesis)
│   ├── <source>.md    # type: source    (Layer 1 immutable capture)
│   └── ...
└── meta/             # knowledge about the knowledge system itself
```

A bundle is portable: a git repo, a tarball, or a mounted directory. No SDK, no runtime
required to read it. Tooling (the manifest generator, optionally a CB graph) is
additive at the edges, never a prerequisite for reading.

input.content:
---
type: spec
title: Frontmatter Field Reference
description: Use when authoring or validating a doc's YAML frontmatter — every field, whether it's required, and its allowed values.
tags: [spec, frontmatter, schema]
status: active
timestamp: 2026-06-21
id: spec:frontmatter
---

# Frontmatter Field Reference

YAML frontmatter is a fenced `---` block at the very top of the file. Keep it to the
documented subset below — the manifest generator parses this subset deterministically
(it is not a full YAML parser, by design: a constrained spine is easier to validate).

## Tier-1 fields (OKF floor)

| Field | Required | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `type` | **yes** | scalar | The only OKF-required field. One of the taxonomy types — see [`types.md`](types.md). |
| `title` | recommended | scalar | Human label. Defaults to filename if absent. |
| `description` | recommended | scalar / `>` block | The **relevance hook**. "Use when… / Covers…". ≤2 lines. The single most important field — see KNOWLEDGE.md §4. |
| `tags` | recommended | inline list `[a, b]` | Lowercase, hyphenated. Semantic, for filtering. |
| `status` | recommended | scalar | `active` \| `superseded` \| `retracted` \| `draft`. Default `active`. |
| `timestamp` | recommended | ISO date | Last meaningful update, `YYYY-MM-DD`. |
| `resource` | optional | URI | OKF field: link to the external system this doc mirrors (a dashboard, sheet, console). |

## Tier-2 fields (CB ceiling — additive, optional)

Adding these does not break OKF compatibility (only `type` is required; extra fields
are legal). They light up only for CB-aware tooling.

| Field | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `id` | scalar `namespace:local` | Stable identity. Survives renames. Required if other docs depend on this one. See KNOWLEDGE.md §6. |
| `deps` | inline list of ids | Typed dependencies — the load-bearing edges. A `concept` lists the sources/concepts it is synthesized from. |
| `tier` | scalar | `okf` (default) \| `cb`. Marks a doc as governed by CB discipline (immutability, supersession). |
| `kind` | scalar | CB sub-classification (e.g. `directive`, `observation`, `verdict`). Closed enum per the host CB graph. |
| `supersedes` / `superseded_by` | id | Dated supersession chain. Set `status: superseded` alongside `superseded_by`. |
| `artifact` | URI | CB provenance carrier for `source`/`thread` docs: `gmail:…`, `document:…`, `session:…`, `https:…`. |

## Conventions

- **Dates are absolute.** `2026-06-21`, never "today" / "last week".
- **One concept per file.** If `description` needs an "and", split the file.
- **Filenames** are lowercase-hyphenated; date-prefix time-bound docs (`2026-06-21-slug.md`).
- **Status changes are edits to frontmatter**, not deletions. Retire by setting
  `status: retracted` (OKF floor) or via supersession (CB tier).
