input.content:
---
type: spec
title: Concept Type Taxonomy
description: Use when deciding what `type:` a document should be, or what artifact kinds the bundle supports — the closed list of document types and which layer each belongs to.
tags: [spec, taxonomy, types]
status: active
timestamp: 2026-06-21
id: spec:types
---

# Concept Type Taxonomy

`type` is the one required field. It answers "what kind of document is this?" and places
the doc in a layer. Keep to this closed list; extend it deliberately (a new type is a
spec change, not a per-doc decision).

| `type` | Layer | What it is | Lifecycle |
|---|---|---|---|
| `source` | 1 (raw) | An immutable capture: an ingested article, statement, transcript, email, doc. Read, never rewritten. | Append-only. Never edited after capture. |
| `concept` | 2 (synthesis) | A compiled, maintained page about one entity/idea, synthesized over sources. The "wiki" page. | Rewritten in place (OKF) or superseded (CB). |
| `thread` | 1→2 | A persisted session: what was decided/done, with links to what it produced. The fix for "threads persisted ad hoc". | Captured once; its `description` is the outcome. |
| `plan` | 2 | Intended work. Replaces the `plan.md`/`USER_STORIES.md`/`DESIGN.md` sprawl. | Active → done/superseded. |
| `analysis` | 2 | A worked argument or investigation toward a conclusion. Your dated `analyses/*.md`. | Captured; may be superseded by a better analysis. |
| `position` / `decision` | 2 | A settled stance and why. The thing future-you must not silently reopen. | Active → superseded (with a reason). |
| `reference` | 2 | A stable how-to / runbook / spec / fact sheet. Low churn. | Edited as facts change. |
| `index` | — | A folder landing page for human progressive disclosure. One per directory. | Edited as the folder grows. |
| `spec` | 3 (schema) | A document defining the methodology itself (these files). | Versioned. |

## Placement rules

- **`source` is sacred.** Once captured, do not edit its body — supersede with a new
  capture if the source itself changed. This is what makes provenance trustworthy.
- **`concept` is where synthesis lives.** When a new `source` lands, the work is
  updating the relevant `concept` docs — not piling up more sources.
- **`thread` closes the centralization gap.** End a working session by writing one
  `thread` doc whose `description` lets a future agent find it without replaying the
  transcript, linking to the `plan`/`analysis`/`concept`/beliefs it produced.
- **`position` is the CB-promotion candidate.** Stances that must not be silently
  reopened are exactly where dated supersession (CB tier) earns its cost.

## How this maps to CB

When a domain is promoted to the CB tier, these document types become artifacts and
beliefs: a `source`/`thread` is a CB **artifact** (referenced by `artifact:` URI); a
`concept`/`position`/`analysis` conclusion becomes a typed **belief** (primitive,
compound, or implication) that `deps` on the artifacts and concepts it was built from.
OKF is the artifact substrate; CB is the epistemic overlay on top of it.

input.content:
---
type: spec
title: The Two-Tier Rule — OKF Floor vs CB Ceiling
description: Use when deciding whether a domain or document needs full CB rigor or just the OKF floor — the boundary test, with the cost/benefit and worked examples.
tags: [spec, tiers, okf, cb, decision]
status: active
timestamp: 2026-06-21
id: spec:tiers
---

# The Two-Tier Rule

The whole system is cheap to adopt because **most knowledge stays on the floor**. Rigor
is opt-in, applied only where it pays. Forcing CB-grade discipline onto "when is the
furnace filter due" is the failure mode to avoid.

## The two tiers

| | **OKF tier (floor)** | **CB tier (ceiling)** |
|---|---|---|
| Discipline | frontmatter + cross-links + index + prose synthesis | + stable ids, typed `deps`, immutability, dated supersession, staleness propagation, contracts |
| Cost to author | ~0 (write markdown) | real (assert / preflight / adjudicate) |
| Contradiction handling | rewrite the page | record + date the supersession, flag dependents stale |
| Read path | manifest → concept docs | + deterministic graph traversal |
| Default for | finances, home, band ops, personal, tutorials, most docs/plans/threads | evals, policies, anything adjudicated or whose claims must stay provably true over time |

## The boundary test

Promote a domain or document to the **CB tier** only if **any** of these is true:

1. **Time-truth matters.** Someone will later ask *"when did this change, and what
   depended on it?"* — and "the page was rewritten" is not an acceptable answer.
2. **Contradictions must be caught mechanically**, not by an LLM's in-the-moment
   judgment. (A superseded dependency must *automatically* flag everything downstream
   as stale.)
3. **It will be audited or adjudicated** — the claim is evidence someone acts on, and
   the provenance chain must hold up.

If none hold, stay on the **OKF floor**. Prose synthesis maintained by the agent is
enough, and the discipline cost of CB is waste.

## Worked examples

| Knowledge | Tier | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Furnace filter size & schedule | OKF | Nobody audits it; rewrite when it changes. |
| Monthly cashflow summary | OKF | Personal; a rewrite-in-place page is fine. |
| "We decided to use a 21-day loan period" (a policy others rely on) | CB | Time-truth + adjudication: supersession must be dated and propagate. |
| An eval's verdict (dag-vs-prose) | CB | It's evidence; the provenance chain is the product. |
| A settled architectural `position` | CB-lean | Must not be silently reopened; dated supersession earns its keep. |
| A how-to for the band's merch upload | OKF | Edit as it changes; no audit trail needed. |

## Migration direction

Start everything on the **floor**. Promote a *document* (not necessarily its whole
domain) to CB the first time it trips the boundary test. Promotion is additive: add
`tier: cb`, `id`, `deps` to the existing frontmatter and register it in the CB graph —
the file stays OKF-readable throughout.

The one floor↔ceiling exception worth pulling down early: **stable `id`s**. Even
OKF-floor docs benefit from ids the moment a folder starts getting restructured, because
ids survive renames where path links don't. Adding an `id` does not make a doc CB-tier;
it just makes its identity durable.

input.content:
# ADOPT — bootstrapping a repo onto the Knowledge methodology

You are an agent told: *"adopt this methodology for this repo."* Follow these steps.

## 0. Read the spec
Read in order: [`spec/KNOWLEDGE.md`](spec/KNOWLEDGE.md), [`spec/types.md`](spec/types.md),
[`spec/frontmatter.md`](spec/frontmatter.md), [`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md). Skim
[`bundle/`](bundle/) for a worked example.

## 1. Decide the bundle root
Pick where knowledge lives in the target repo — typically `knowledge/` or `docs/`. This
becomes the **bundle root**. Everything below is relative to it.

## 2. Create the skeleton
```
<root>/
├── index.md            # type: index — what this repo's knowledge covers
├── <domain>/           # one folder per natural domain
│   └── index.md
└── meta/               # threads, plans, analyses, positions about the repo itself
    └── index.md
```
Copy [`templates/`](templates/) as starting points. Default every doc to the **OKF
floor** (`tier` omitted). Only reach for CB (`tier: cb`) when the boundary test in
[`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md) trips.

## 3. Wire the manifest generator
Copy [`tools/build_manifest.py`](tools/build_manifest.py) into the repo (or reference it).
Run it against the bundle root:
```
python3 tools/build_manifest.py <root>
```
This writes `<root>/manifest.json`. Re-run it after any change to the bundle. Wire it
into CI or a pre-commit hook so the manifest never drifts from the docs.

## 4. Author the entry index
`<root>/index.md` is the front door. Its `description` frontmatter is the relevance hook
for the **whole repo's knowledge** — write it so a fresh agent knows in one line whether
this repo is relevant to its task.

## 5. The standing workflow (what changes day-to-day)
- **End of a working session** → write one `type: thread` doc in `meta/`. Its
  `description` is the outcome; link to the plans/analyses/concepts it produced. This is
  how threads stop being persisted ad hoc.
- **New external input** → capture it as `type: source` (immutable), then **update the
  relevant `type: concept` docs** — don't let sources pile up unsynthesized.
- **A settled stance** → `type: position`. Consider promoting to CB tier.
- **After any change** → regenerate `manifest.json`.

## 6. How a future agent reads it back
Load `manifest.json` first → match task against `description`s → load only the matching
concept docs → drill into `source` docs only for provenance. Never bulk-load the bundle.

input.content:
# CONVERT — migrating an existing knowledge store to this format

You are an agent told: *"convert this existing knowledge store to this format."* The
store may be ad-hoc markdown, a `plan.md`/`DESIGN.md` sprawl, a notes folder, a JSON
belief file, or some mix. Convert **without losing provenance** and **without inventing
rigor that wasn't there.**

## 0. Read the spec
[`spec/KNOWLEDGE.md`](spec/KNOWLEDGE.md), then [`spec/types.md`](spec/types.md) and
[`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md).

## 1. Inventory first, transform second
Walk the existing store. For each artifact, record: its current path, what it actually
is, and which `type` it maps to. Do **not** start editing yet. Produce the inventory as
a `type: analysis` doc in `meta/` so the conversion itself is provenanced.

Common mappings:
| You find | Maps to |
|---|---|
| `README.md`, runbooks, how-tos | `reference` |
| `plan.md`, `USER_STORIES.md`, roadmap | `plan` |
| dated `analyses/*.md`, investigations | `analysis` |
| ingested articles, transcripts, statements | `source` (immutable) |
| pages about an entity/topic, synthesized notes | `concept` |
| settled decisions, ADRs | `position` |
| session logs, chat exports | `thread` |
| a JSON belief/assertion file | keep as CB store; emit `concept`/`source` docs as the OKF projection (see §4) |

## 2. Add the frontmatter spine, don't rewrite bodies
For each file: prepend the frontmatter spine (`type`, `title`, `description`, `tags`,
`status`, `timestamp`). **Write a real relevance hook for `description`** — this is the
highest-value part of the conversion. Leave the existing body content intact unless it
mixes types (then split). Preserve original dates in `timestamp`.

## 3. Preserve provenance
- If a file was an immutable capture, mark it `type: source` and **stop editing it**.
- If a file's history matters, keep the original in git; the conversion is a commit, so
  `git log` remains the deep history.
- Convert ad-hoc cross-references into markdown links to the new paths.

## 4. Decide the tier per document (default: floor)
Everything lands on the **OKF floor** unless it trips the boundary test in
[`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md). Only then add `tier: cb`, `id`, and `deps`. If the
source store was already a CB/belief graph (e.g. an `assertions.json`), keep that as the
canonical CB store and **emit** an OKF bundle from it (each belief → a typed `concept`
doc; `deps` → cross-links + `deps` frontmatter). Don't flatten a belief graph into prose
and lose the edges.

## 5. Generate the manifest and verify
```
python3 tools/build_manifest.py <root>
```
Then spot-check: pick three task-shaped questions a future agent might ask, and confirm
each is answerable by reading `manifest.json` + at most one or two concept docs. If not,
the `description`s are too vague — fix them, not the bodies.

## 6. Report
Summarize: counts by `type`, what stayed floor vs went CB, anything you could not place
(flag for the human), and the new entry `index.md`.

# Household cashflow

Synthesized view of monthly money movement. Maintained in place as new
[source](2026-q2-budget-source.md) captures land — this is Layer-2 synthesis, not a
capture.

## Monthly shape
- **In:** ~$9,200 net.
- **Fixed out:** ~$3,570 (housing $2,650, utilities+insurance $740, subscriptions $180).
- **Variable out:** ~$2,200 (groceries+household $1,300, discretionary $900).
- **Automatic savings:** $1,500.
- **Net free after savings:** ~$1,930/mo.

## Runway
Liquid reserve ~$34,000 against ~$5,770 of non-savings monthly outflow → **~5.9 months**
of bare-essentials runway if income stopped (excludes the discretionary $900, which is
cuttable).

## Open questions / gaps
- Subscriptions line ($180) hasn't been itemized since Q1 — candidate for review.

## Sources
- [Q2 2026 budget figures](2026-q2-budget-source.md)

# 2026-06-21 — Knowledge methodology designed and scaffolded

## What this session was
Evaluate whether to build a generalized, cross-repo knowledge base on the Open Knowledge
Format (OKF), how it relates to Karpathy's LLM-wiki and the existing Composable Beliefs
(CB) system, and how to centralize the currently-haphazard threads/plans/analyses.

## What was decided
- **Adopt OKF as the floor**, not the ceiling. It is mostly a renaming of a pattern
  already reinvented in `SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain` (markdown-canonical + derived index).
- **CB sits above OKF** as an opt-in ceiling — confirmed clean layering; CB is a
  conceptual superset with a separate serialization. Build a CB↔OKF adapter, not a rewrite.
- **The synthesis layer IS the wiki**, and it is OKF markdown both agent and human read;
  a rendered website is a throwaway view. Agent reads synthesis first, drills to sources.
- **`description` as relevance hook + a generated `manifest.json`** give the skills-style
  "understand a doc without loading it" capability.
- **`id` is additive** (OKF-legal); path links stay canonical.

## What it produced
- The `knowledge` repo: [spec](../../spec/KNOWLEDGE.md), [ADOPT](../../ADOPT.md),
  [CONVERT](../../CONVERT.md), templates, the manifest generator, and this demo bundle.
- [Layering analysis](okf-vs-cb-layering.md) and the [two-tier position](two-tier-knowledge-architecture.md).

## Open / next
- Build the CB↔OKF serialization adapter (`emit-okf` / `ingest-okf`) in CB.
- Use the OKF/Karpathy wiki as the prose baseline arm in the dag-vs-prose eval.
- Pilot CONVERT on a real existing store.

## What this session was
Evaluate whether to build a generalized, cross-repo knowledge base on the Open Knowledge
Format (OKF), how it relates to Karpathy's LLM-wiki and the existing Composable Beliefs
(CB) system, and how to centralize the currently-haphazard threads/plans/analyses.

## What was decided
- **Adopt OKF as the floor**, not the ceiling. It is mostly a renaming of a pattern
  already reinvented in `SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain` (markdown-canonical + derived index).
- **CB sits above OKF** as an opt-in ceiling — confirmed clean layering; CB is a
  conceptual superset with a separate serialization. Build a CB↔OKF adapter, not a rewrite.
- **The synthesis layer IS the wiki**, and it is OKF markdown both agent and human read;
  a rendered website is a throwaway view. Agent reads synthesis first, drills to sources.
- **`description` as relevance hook + a generated `manifest.json`** give the skills-style

- **`id` is additive** (OKF-legal); path links stay canonical.

## What it produced
- The `knowledge` repo: [spec](../../spec/KNOWLEDGE.md), [ADOPT](../../ADOPT.md),

# How OKF, Karpathy-wiki, and CB layer

## Question
Does adopting OKF for a generalized knowledge base conflict with, subsume, or sit below
the Composable Beliefs system?

## Findings

**OKF is a transport/interchange layer.** Markdown + YAML frontmatter, one required
field (`type`), untyped cross-links, optional `index.md`/`log.md`, packaged as a
git repo/tarball. Explicitly "format, not platform." It is the productization of
Karpathy's LLM-wiki (same raw/wiki/schema shape, same index/log conventions).

**CB is an epistemic-semantics layer.** Typed claims, immutability + dated supersession,
provenance with `artifact` + `evidence[]`, staleness over a real dependency DAG,
machine-checkable contracts, deterministic LLM-free read path. None of that is in OKF's
remit, by OKF's own choice.

**They layer cleanly:**

| Concern | OKF / Karpathy | CB |
|---|---|---|
| Packaging | markdown + frontmatter | JSON graph |
| Identity | file path | belief id |
| Relations | untyped links | typed `deps` edges |
| Classification | free `type` | `kind`/`domain` closed enums |
| Provenance | `resource` + `timestamp` | `artifact` + `evidence[]` |
| History | prose `log.md` | immutable supersession chain |
| Rules | none (deliberately) | contracts compiled to tests |
| Query | "any search tool" | deterministic traversal |

CB serializes *down* to OKF (every belief → a typed markdown doc; `deps` → cross-links).
OKF cannot reconstruct CB *up* without inventing exactly the discipline CB adds — the
up-conversion is lossy. That is the definition of CB sitting above OKF.

**Synthesis ownership:** synthesis-as-format is OKF's; synthesis-as-*process* (the
ingest-time wiki-maintenance loop) is Karpathy's; synthesis-as-*verifiable-epistemics*
is CB's. The default floor uses Karpathy-style prose synthesis; CB synthesis is opt-in.

## Conclusion
OKF becoming a standard is good for CB, not a threat: it validates CB's "format not
platform" thesis and hands CB a free serialization target. The move is a CB↔OKF
producer/consumer adapter; CB markets as "the typed, verifiable, supersedable dialect of
OKF." The real cost OKF imposes is that it **raises the plain-English baseline** the eval
must beat — which also makes that baseline a more credible experimental control. This
conclusion is settled in [two-tier-knowledge-architecture.md](two-tier-knowledge-architecture.md).

# Knowledge is OKF on the floor, CB at the ceiling

## Decision
The cross-repo knowledge base uses **OKF as the universal floor** and **CB as an opt-in
ceiling**. Every domain and document defaults to the floor (frontmatter + cross-links +
prose synthesis). A document is promoted to the CB tier only when it trips the boundary
test: time-truth matters, contradictions must be caught mechanically, or it will be
audited/adjudicated.

This doc is itself CB-tier (note `tier: cb`, `id`, `deps`) to demonstrate the ceiling on
a doc that genuinely qualifies — it is a governing decision that must not be silently
reopened.

## Why
- The floor is near-zero cost, so it actually gets adopted across all repos; forcing CB
  rigor onto personal/productivity knowledge is waste (the furnace-filter case).
- CB rigor (immutability, dated supersession, staleness, contracts) earns its cost only
  where someone later asks "when did this change and what depended on it."
- OKF compatibility is preserved at the ceiling: CB fields are additive frontmatter on
  OKF-readable files, so a generic consumer still works.

See the full argument in [okf-vs-cb-layering.md](okf-vs-cb-layering.md) and the boundary
test in [../../spec/tiers.md](../../spec/tiers.md).

## What would change this
- If maintaining two tiers proves more confusing than one, collapse to OKF-only and run
  CB entirely as a side graph (no `tier` field on docs).
- If the dag-vs-prose eval shows structured substrate does **not** beat a strong OKF
  prose baseline on tasks that matter, demote CB from "ceiling" to "side experiment" and
  supersede this position.

content.thinking:
I'm verifying that the manifest correctly handles the folded descriptions by collapsing them to single lines, and I notice there's a discrepancy between how the demo bundle used inline descriptions versus how the KNOWLEDGE.md spec example showed folded syntax with `>` — I should check whether the folded format is actually working as expected across the spec files.

The spec files themselves (KNOWLEDGE.md and others) live outside the bundle directory under spec/, so they're not included in the manifest, which is intentional since the manifest only covers the demo bundle's 11 documents. Now I'm deciding whether to make an initial commit — the user wants this as a private repo on their GitHub, which suggests they expect a commit, but the rule is to only commit when explicitly asked. I think making an initial commit is reasonable here since the whole point is creating a repo to push to GitHub, so I'll go ahead and do that.

I'm also considering adding a top-level CLAUDE.md file. The methodology mentions it as part of the Layer 3 schema, and since the user wants to point fresh agents to this repo, having a small CLAUDE.md that directs them to README, ADOPT.md, or CONVERT.md would be valuable — agents automatically read CLAUDE.md files, so it's a high-value addition for that use case.

# CLAUDE.md — knowledge repo

This repo is the **Knowledge methodology**: a portable OKF-based format for knowledge
across all my repos and productivity domains, with Composable Beliefs as an opt-in
rigor ceiling.

If you are a fresh agent pointed here:

- **"Adopt this methodology"** → read [`ADOPT.md`](ADOPT.md).
- **"Convert an existing store"** → read [`CONVERT.md`](CONVERT.md).
- **Understand the format** → read [`spec/KNOWLEDGE.md`](spec/KNOWLEDGE.md) first, then
  `spec/types.md`, `spec/frontmatter.md`, `spec/tiers.md`.
- **See it used** → [`bundle/`](bundle/) is a real, self-demonstrating bundle; start at
  [`bundle/manifest.json`](bundle/manifest.json).

After any change to `bundle/`, regenerate the manifest:
`python3 tools/build_manifest.py bundle`

Core rule: everything defaults to the **OKF floor** (plain markdown + frontmatter +
generated manifest). Promote a document to the **CB ceiling** (`tier: cb`, `id`, `deps`)
only when it trips the boundary test in `spec/tiers.md`.

content.text:
Done. The `knowledge` repo is built at `/Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/knowledge`, committed, and ready to push to your private GitHub.

## What's there

**Spec (`spec/`)** — `KNOWLEDGE.md` is the core (three layers, frontmatter spine, manifest contract, identity rule, synthesis model). `types.md`, `frontmatter.md`, `tiers.md` break out the taxonomy, field reference, and the OKF-floor-vs-CB-ceiling boundary test.

**Two agent entry points** — `ADOPT.md` ("adopt this methodology for this repo") and `CONVERT.md` ("convert an existing store"), plus a top-level `CLAUDE.md` that routes a fresh agent to the right one automatically.

**`templates/`** — one per document type (concept, source, thread, analysis, position, reference, plan, index).

**`tools/build_manifest.py`** — stdlib-only generator that harvests every doc's frontmatter into `manifest.json` (the skills-style summary index). Has a `--check` mode for CI so the manifest can't drift. Verified working: 11 docs, folded `>` descriptions collapsed correctly, CB tier-2 fields harvested only where present.

**`bundle/`** — a real, self-demonstrating bundle:
- `finances/` + `home/` — OKF-floor productivity domains (cashflow synthesis over an immutable source doc; the furnace-filter reference the spec uses as its "stays on the floor" example).
- `meta/` — dogfoods the format on this system itself: a `thread` doc persisting *this session*, the `analysis` of the OKF/CB layering, and a **CB-tier `position`** (`tier: cb`, `id`, `deps`) demonstrating the ceiling on a doc that genuinely qualifies.

## The decisions that answer your questions, now encoded

- **Synthesis = the wiki**, one body of OKF markdown both agent and human read; rendered sites are throwaway views (`KNOWLEDGE.md` §2).
- **Karpathy prose-synthesis is the floor; CB synthesis is opt-in** — using CB everywhere is overengineering, the boundary test gates it (`tiers.md`).
- **`id` is additive, path links stay canonical** — still OKF (`KNOWLEDGE.md` §6).
- **Threads/plans/analyses centralize** via the type taxonomy + the session→OKF→CB pipeline (`types.md`).

To push: create the empty private repo on GitHub, then `git -C /Users/mark/dev/repos/mine/knowledge remote add origin <url> && git push -u origin main` — tell me the URL and I'll wire it up.

Two open threads from the session worth naming as next steps (both recorded in the demo `thread` doc): build the **CB↔OKF serialization adapter** (`emit-okf`/`ingest-okf`) so CB keeps `assertions.json` canonical but speaks OKF, and use the OKF/Karpathy wiki as the **prose baseline arm** in your dag-vs-prose eval. Want me to spec either of those, or run `CONVERT` against a real store (e.g. `SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain`) as the first proof?

what is "bundle/meta"? re: "Caveat: that lives in the demo bundle to demonstrate the pattern — it's not yet wired to a canonical session-archive location. That's exactly
  the "centralize threads" workflow the spec prescribes for real adoption." - shouldn't it more be a foundational reference for how this spec and approach was conceived of and architected?

content.thinking:
I see the user's concern — the meta documentation in `bundle/meta` that explains how the spec was conceived and architected should probably be treated as foundational reference material rather than just supplementary demo content, since understanding the design rationale and evolution is essential to understanding the spec itself.

The real solution is to recognize that the `knowledge` repo should itself be a proper knowledge bundle at the top level, documenting its own genesis, analysis, and design decisions using the same format it's specifying — not burying that documentation inside a demo folder. That way the foundational reasoning becomes canonical documentation rather than illustrative sample data. So the demo bundle doesn't need its own meta docs — the top-level meta becomes the real, foundational reference that demonstrates thread/analysis/position types authentically. The demo bundle can stay focused on finances and home as multi-domain examples, and both ADOPT and CONVERT can point to the canonical meta folder for real-world patterns. I'm settling on a structure where the repo itself functions as a knowledge bundle with spec, meta, and a separate demo bundle, keeping things clean and purposeful. I'm torn between two approaches: either keep the repo's self-knowledge (spec + meta) navigable through README and index links without a manifest, or generate a manifest at the repo root to dogfood the pattern — but that risks including template files with placeholder frontmatter. I think the pragmatic move is to keep `bundle/` as the demo with its own manifest, and document that `meta/` + `spec/` are the foundational knowledge accessed through index and README links. I'll move the meta docs to the top level and reframe them as foundational, update all the cross-references, regenerate both bundle and meta manifests, and update the README and CLAUDE.md to reflect this structure. I'm also considering whether to call it `meta/` or something clearer like `design/` or `decisions/`.

Actually, `design/` better captures what the user emphasized — how the spec and approach were conceived and architected. I'll rename the folder to `design/` and update the index title to reflect that framing. The demo bundle will stay as finances + home, since the design folder now demonstrates the thread/analysis/position types for real at the top level.

Every repo should have its own meta or design folder for self-knowledge; this one's just promoting those genesis docs to top-level `design/` to show the format in action on real foundational history. I'll use git mv to preserve history, then update the index, internal links, README, and regenerate the manifests. I need to update the relative paths in the docs that reference spec, ADOPT, and CONVERT — changing `../../` to `../` since they're moving from meta/ to design/. I'll also need to update bundle/index.md to remove meta from the domains list and adjust the intro text to mention only finances and home, plus update the root description accordingly. For the position doc's id namespace, I'm considering whether to rename `meta:two-tier` to `design:two-tier` since it's just a stable identifier and the folder is changing.

Actually, reconsidering the folder name itself — the spec's ADOPT.md already prescribes `meta/` as the place for "threads, plans, analyses about the repo itself," which is exactly what this content is. So keeping the folder as `meta/` at the top level aligns with the spec's own prescription rather than renaming it to `design/`. That means the id namespace `meta:two-tier` stays consistent too.

The real fix is just promoting `bundle/meta/` up to top-level `meta/`, updating all the links, and regenerating manifests. Since this repo is unusual as the spec repo itself, I'll generate a manifest for `meta/` as its own small bundle alongside the demo bundle, treating `spec/` and `templates/` as repo infrastructure rather than knowledge bundles. That's cleaner dogfooding.

For the execution, I need to be careful with the file operations — after moving files with git, the new paths won't have been read in this conversation yet, so I'll need to either read them after the move or just rewrite them at the new locations with corrected content and delete the old ones, which is simpler and avoids any harness issues with unread paths.

Looking at what actually needs editing: the index needs its title and description updated to reflect the foundational design record framing, the thread file has three relative path links that need fixing from `../../` to `../`, and the two-tier file has one link to fix. The okf-vs-cb file only has internal links so it doesn't need changes. I'll do a git move of the whole directory to preserve history, then read and edit just those three files that need updates.

content.text:
Good catch — you're right, and it exposes a real conflation. Two different roles got merged:

- **`bundle/`** is meant to be *throwaway illustrative demo* (synthetic finances/home data you hold up to a newcomer).
- The genesis docs (the session thread, the OKF-vs-CB analysis, the two-tier position) are **not demo data** — they're the *authoritative record of why this spec is shaped the way it is*. Burying them inside a demo bundle undersells them to "look, you can dogfood" when they should be foundational reference.

The fix matches the spec's own prescription: `ADOPT.md` already says every adopting repo gets a top-level `meta/` for "threads, plans, analyses about the repo itself." This repo should follow its own rule — so I'll **promote `bundle/meta/` to a real top-level `meta/`** (the repo dogfooding for real), and trim `bundle/` to pure finances/home demo. The repo then has three honest layers: `spec/` (the rules), `meta/` (why the rules are what they are — foundational), `bundle/` (an example).

1	---
2	type: thread
3	title: 2026-06-21 — Knowledge methodology designed and scaffolded
4	description: Use when you need what was decided in the session that created this repo, without replaying it. Outcome: adopted OKF floor + CB ceiling, scaffolded the knowledge repo.
5	tags: [meta, thread, design]
6	status: active
7	timestamp: 2026-06-21
8	artifact: session:2026-06-21-okf-knowledge-design
9	---
10	
11	# 2026-06-21 — Knowledge methodology designed and scaffolded
12	
13	## What this session was
14	Evaluate whether to build a generalized, cross-repo knowledge base on the Open Knowledge
15	Format (OKF), how it relates to Karpathy's LLM-wiki and the existing Composable Beliefs
16	(CB) system, and how to centralize the currently-haphazard threads/plans/analyses.
17	
18	## What was decided
19	- **Adopt OKF as the floor**, not the ceiling. It is mostly a renaming of a pattern
20	  already reinvented in `SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain` (markdown-canonical + derived index).
21	- **CB sits above OKF** as an opt-in ceiling — confirmed clean layering; CB is a
22	  conceptual superset with a separate serialization. Build a CB↔OKF adapter, not a rewrite.
23	- **The synthesis layer IS the wiki**, and it is OKF markdown both agent and human read;
24	  a rendered website is a throwaway view. Agent reads synthesis first, drills to sources.
25	- **`description` as relevance hook + a generated `manifest.json`** give the skills-style
26	  "understand a doc without loading it" capability.
27	- **`id` is additive** (OKF-legal); path links stay canonical.
28	
29	## What it produced
30	- The `knowledge` repo: [spec](../../spec/KNOWLEDGE.md), [ADOPT](../../ADOPT.md),
31	  [CONVERT](../../CONVERT.md), templates, the manifest generator, and this demo bundle.
32	- [Layering analysis](okf-vs-cb-layering.md) and the [two-tier position](two-tier-knowledge-architecture.md).
33	
34	## Open / next
35	- Build the CB↔OKF serialization adapter (`emit-okf` / `ingest-okf`) in CB.
36	- Use the OKF/Karpathy wiki as the prose baseline arm in the dag-vs-prose eval.
37	- Pilot CONVERT on a real existing store.
38

# 2026-06-21 — Knowledge methodology designed and scaffolded

## What this session was
Evaluate whether to build a generalized, cross-repo knowledge base on the Open Knowledge
Format (OKF), how it relates to Karpathy's LLM-wiki and the existing Composable Beliefs
(CB) system, and how to centralize the currently-haphazard threads/plans/analyses.

## What was decided
- **Adopt OKF as the floor**, not the ceiling. It is mostly a renaming of a pattern
  already reinvented in `SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain` (markdown-canonical + derived index).
- **CB sits above OKF** as an opt-in ceiling — confirmed clean layering; CB is a
  conceptual superset with a separate serialization. Build a CB↔OKF adapter, not a rewrite.
- **The synthesis layer IS the wiki**, and it is OKF markdown both agent and human read;
  a rendered website is a throwaway view. Agent reads synthesis first, drills to sources.
- **`description` as relevance hook + a generated `manifest.json`** give the skills-style
  "understand a doc without loading it" capability.
- **`id` is additive** (OKF-legal); path links stay canonical.

## What it produced
- The `knowledge` repo: [spec](../../spec/KNOWLEDGE.md), [ADOPT](../../ADOPT.md),
  [CONVERT](../../CONVERT.md), templates, the manifest generator, and this demo bundle.
- [Layering analysis](okf-vs-cb-layering.md) and the [two-tier position](two-tier-knowledge-architecture.md).

## Open / next
- Build the CB↔OKF serialization adapter (`emit-okf` / `ingest-okf`) in CB.
- Use the OKF/Karpathy wiki as the prose baseline arm in the dag-vs-prose eval.
- Pilot CONVERT on a real existing store.

# Knowledge is OKF on the floor, CB at the ceiling

## Decision
The cross-repo knowledge base uses **OKF as the universal floor** and **CB as an opt-in
ceiling**. Every domain and document defaults to the floor (frontmatter + cross-links +
prose synthesis). A document is promoted to the CB tier only when it trips the boundary
test: time-truth matters, contradictions must be caught mechanically, or it will be
audited/adjudicated.

This doc is itself CB-tier (note `tier: cb`, `id`, `deps`) to demonstrate the ceiling on
a doc that genuinely qualifies — it is a governing decision that must not be silently
reopened.

## Why
- The floor is near-zero cost, so it actually gets adopted across all repos; forcing CB
  rigor onto personal/productivity knowledge is waste (the furnace-filter case).
- CB rigor (immutability, dated supersession, staleness, contracts) earns its cost only
  where someone later asks "when did this change and what depended on it."
- OKF compatibility is preserved at the ceiling: CB fields are additive frontmatter on
  OKF-readable files, so a generic consumer still works.

See the full argument in [okf-vs-cb-layering.md](okf-vs-cb-layering.md) and the boundary
test in [../../spec/tiers.md](../../spec/tiers.md).

## What would change this
- If maintaining two tiers proves more confusing than one, collapse to OKF-only and run
  CB entirely as a side graph (no `tier` field on docs).
- If the dag-vs-prose eval shows structured substrate does **not** beat a strong OKF
  prose baseline on tasks that matter, demote CB from "ceiling" to "side experiment" and
  supersede this position.

# knowledge

A portable, agent- and human-readable knowledge methodology for use across all my
repos and domains (software projects **and** productivity domains — finances, home,
band ops, personal). This repo is **both the specification and a working example of
itself**: the `bundle/` directory is a real knowledge bundle written in the format
the spec defines.

Point a fresh agent here and say one of:

- **"Adopt this methodology for this repo"** → the agent reads [`ADOPT.md`](ADOPT.md).
- **"Convert this existing knowledge store to this format"** → the agent reads [`CONVERT.md`](CONVERT.md).

## What this is, in one breath

Knowledge is **plain markdown files + YAML frontmatter, organized in a directory
hierarchy, with a generated manifest as the agent's entry index.** It is
[Open Knowledge Format (OKF)](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/knowledge-catalog/tree/main/okf)
as the floor, [Karpathy's LLM-wiki](https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f)
as the synthesis discipline, and [Composable Beliefs (CB)](#tiers) as an opt-in
ceiling for knowledge that must stay provably true over time.

## Why it exists

Across ~45 repos, persisted knowledge had drifted into five incompatible patterns
(`plan.md` vs `USER_STORIES.md` vs `DESIGN.md`; `CLAUDE.md` vs `claude.md`;
JSON-canonical vs YAML-canonical vs prose; threads persisted ad hoc). This gives all
of them one cheap shape to converge on, so a fresh agent can build context from any
repo the same way.

## Layout

```
knowledge/
├── README.md            ← you are here
├── ADOPT.md             ← entry point: "adopt this methodology"
├── CONVERT.md           ← entry point: "convert an existing store"
├── spec/
│   ├── KNOWLEDGE.md     ← THE spec (read this first)
│   ├── frontmatter.md   ← the frontmatter spine: field reference
│   ├── types.md         ← the concept-type taxonomy
│   └── tiers.md         ← the OKF floor vs CB ceiling boundary rule
├── templates/           ← copy-paste starting points, one per type
├── tools/
│   └── build_manifest.py  ← regenerates bundle/manifest.json (stdlib only)
└── bundle/              ← a real, self-demonstrating knowledge bundle
    ├── index.md         ← progressive-disclosure root
    ├── manifest.json    ← GENERATED skills-style summary index (do not hand-edit)
    ├── finances/        ← productivity domain (OKF floor)
    ├── home/            ← productivity domain (OKF floor)
    └── meta/            ← knowledge about this system itself (incl. CB-tier docs)
```

## The reading order

1. [`spec/KNOWLEDGE.md`](spec/KNOWLEDGE.md) — the model and its three layers.
2. [`spec/frontmatter.md`](spec/frontmatter.md) — the fields.
3. [`spec/types.md`](spec/types.md) — what kinds of documents exist.
4. [`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md) — when to stay cheap (OKF) vs when to add rigor (CB).
5. [`bundle/`](bundle/) — see all of it actually used.

content.thinking:
The user's pointing out a real issue — "bundle" is OKF's generic term for any knowledge directory, so naming the demo folder that way blurs the distinction between the concept and this specific implementation. It's confusing to call something a bundle when everything in this structure is technically a bundle.

I should rename `bundle/` to `demo/` to be clearer, and then hunt through the repo for all references to that path — README, CLAUDE.md, ADOPT.md, CONVERT.md, KNOWLEDGE.md — and update them while keeping "bundle" as the generic term in the prose. Now I'm mapping out the remaining tasks: rename the folder with `git mv`, update all path references across README, CLAUDE.md, ADOPT.md, and KNOWLEDGE.md, then regenerate the manifest files for both directories before committing everything together.

content.text:
Good question — and it points at the same confusion. **"Bundle" is OKF's generic noun for *any* knowledge directory** (a directory of markdown docs = "a bundle"). So naming this specific folder `bundle/` is like naming a folder `folder/` — and it's doubly confusing now that `meta/` is *also* a bundle. The spec keeps "bundle" as the concept; the demo folder should say what it actually is. Renaming `bundle/` → `demo/`.

Let me fold that into the remaining work:

# knowledge

A portable, agent- and human-readable knowledge methodology for use across all my
repos and domains (software projects **and** productivity domains — finances, home,
band ops, personal). This repo is **both the specification and a working example of
itself**: the `bundle/` directory is a real knowledge bundle written in the format
the spec defines.

Point a fresh agent here and say one of:

- **"Adopt this methodology for this repo"** → the agent reads [`ADOPT.md`](ADOPT.md).
- **"Convert this existing knowledge store to this format"** → the agent reads [`CONVERT.md`](CONVERT.md).

## What this is, in one breath

Knowledge is **plain markdown files + YAML frontmatter, organized in a directory
hierarchy, with a generated manifest as the agent's entry index.** It is
[Open Knowledge Format (OKF)](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/knowledge-catalog/tree/main/okf)
as the floor, [Karpathy's LLM-wiki](https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f)
as the synthesis discipline, and [Composable Beliefs (CB)](#tiers) as an opt-in
ceiling for knowledge that must stay provably true over time.

## Why it exists

Across ~45 repos, persisted knowledge had drifted into five incompatible patterns
(`plan.md` vs `USER_STORIES.md` vs `DESIGN.md`; `CLAUDE.md` vs `claude.md`;
JSON-canonical vs YAML-canonical vs prose; threads persisted ad hoc). This gives all
of them one cheap shape to converge on, so a fresh agent can build context from any
repo the same way.

## Layout

```
knowledge/
├── README.md            ← you are here
├── ADOPT.md             ← entry point: "adopt this methodology"
├── CONVERT.md           ← entry point: "convert an existing store"
├── spec/
│   ├── KNOWLEDGE.md     ← THE spec (read this first)
│   ├── frontmatter.md   ← the frontmatter spine: field reference
│   ├── types.md         ← the concept-type taxonomy
│   └── tiers.md         ← the OKF floor vs CB ceiling boundary rule
├── meta/                ← THE DESIGN RECORD: how this methodology was conceived
│   ├── index.md
│   ├── manifest.json    ← generated
│   ├── 2026-06-21-okf-cb-design-session.md   ← thread: the session that produced this
│   ├── okf-vs-cb-layering.md                 ← analysis: OKF vs Karpathy vs CB
│   └── two-tier-knowledge-architecture.md    ← position (CB tier): the floor/ceiling decision
├── templates/           ← copy-paste starting points, one per type
├── tools/
│   └── build_manifest.py  ← regenerates a bundle's manifest.json (stdlib only)
└── bundle/              ← a SYNTHETIC demo bundle (illustrative sample data only)
    ├── index.md         ← progressive-disclosure root
    ├── manifest.json    ← GENERATED skills-style summary index (do not hand-edit)
    ├── finances/        ← productivity domain (OKF floor)
    └── home/            ← productivity domain (OKF floor)
```

`meta/` is the repo dogfooding its own format on its real history — read it to learn
*why* the spec is shaped this way. `bundle/` is throwaway example data you hold up to a
newcomer; it carries no authoritative content.

## The reading order

1. [`spec/KNOWLEDGE.md`](spec/KNOWLEDGE.md) — the model and its three layers.
2. [`spec/frontmatter.md`](spec/frontmatter.md) — the fields.
3. [`spec/types.md`](spec/types.md) — what kinds of documents exist.
4. [`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md) — when to stay cheap (OKF) vs when to add rigor (CB).
5. [`bundle/`](bundle/) — see all of it actually used.

# CLAUDE.md — knowledge repo

This repo is the **Knowledge methodology**: a portable OKF-based format for knowledge
across all my repos and productivity domains, with Composable Beliefs as an opt-in
rigor ceiling.

If you are a fresh agent pointed here:

- **"Adopt this methodology"** → read [`ADOPT.md`](ADOPT.md).
- **"Convert an existing store"** → read [`CONVERT.md`](CONVERT.md).
- **Understand the format** → read [`spec/KNOWLEDGE.md`](spec/KNOWLEDGE.md) first, then
  `spec/types.md`, `spec/frontmatter.md`, `spec/tiers.md`.
- **See it used** → [`bundle/`](bundle/) is a real, self-demonstrating bundle; start at
  [`bundle/manifest.json`](bundle/manifest.json).

After any change to `bundle/`, regenerate the manifest:
`python3 tools/build_manifest.py bundle`

Core rule: everything defaults to the **OKF floor** (plain markdown + frontmatter +
generated manifest). Promote a document to the **CB ceiling** (`tier: cb`, `id`, `deps`)
only when it trips the boundary test in `spec/tiers.md`.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# ADOPT — bootstrapping a repo onto the Knowledge methodology

You are an agent told: *"adopt this methodology for this repo."* Follow these steps.

## 0. Read the spec
Read in order: [`spec/KNOWLEDGE.md`](spec/KNOWLEDGE.md), [`spec/types.md`](spec/types.md),
[`spec/frontmatter.md`](spec/frontmatter.md), [`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md). Skim
[`bundle/`](bundle/) for a worked example.

## 1. Decide the bundle root
Pick where knowledge lives in the target repo — typically `knowledge/` or `docs/`. This
becomes the **bundle root**. Everything below is relative to it.

## 2. Create the skeleton
```
<root>/
├── index.md            # type: index — what this repo's knowledge covers
├── <domain>/           # one folder per natural domain
│   └── index.md
└── meta/               # threads, plans, analyses, positions about the repo itself
    └── index.md
```
Copy [`templates/`](templates/) as starting points. Default every doc to the **OKF
floor** (`tier` omitted). Only reach for CB (`tier: cb`) when the boundary test in
[`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md) trips.

## 3. Wire the manifest generator
Copy [`tools/build_manifest.py`](tools/build_manifest.py) into the repo (or reference it).
Run it against the bundle root:
```
python3 tools/build_manifest.py <root>
```
This writes `<root>/manifest.json`. Re-run it after any change to the bundle. Wire it
into CI or a pre-commit hook so the manifest never drifts from the docs.

## 4. Author the entry index
`<root>/index.md` is the front door. Its `description` frontmatter is the relevance hook
for the **whole repo's knowledge** — write it so a fresh agent knows in one line whether
this repo is relevant to its task.

## 5. The standing workflow (what changes day-to-day)
- **End of a working session** → write one `type: thread` doc in `meta/`. Its
  `description` is the outcome; link to the plans/analyses/concepts it produced. This is
  how threads stop being persisted ad hoc.
- **New external input** → capture it as `type: source` (immutable), then **update the
  relevant `type: concept` docs** — don't let sources pile up unsynthesized.
- **A settled stance** → `type: position`. Consider promoting to CB tier.
- **After any change** → regenerate `manifest.json`.

## 6. How a future agent reads it back
Load `manifest.json` first → match task against `description`s → load only the matching
concept docs → drill into `source` docs only for provenance. Never bulk-load the bundle.

content.text:
Both fixes done and pushed to `ob6to8/knowledge`.

**Why it was `bundle/`:** "bundle" is OKF's generic noun for *any* knowledge directory (a directory of markdown docs = "a bundle"). So naming one folder `bundle/` was like naming a folder `folder/` — especially once `meta/` became a bundle too. You were right; it's now `demo/`, and folders are named for their **role**, not their type. The spec keeps "bundle" as the concept.

**The structure now:**

```
knowledge/
├── spec/    → the rules (Layer 3 schema)
├── meta/    → THE DESIGN RECORD — foundational, authoritative
│             (originating session thread, OKF-vs-CB analysis, two-tier position)
├── demo/    → synthetic example bundle (finances/home), illustrative only
├── templates/, tools/
```

This resolves the conflation you flagged: the genesis docs are no longer demo data sitting in a throwaway bundle — they're the repo's real self-knowledge at top level, treated as authoritative, and they happen to satisfy the very `meta/` convention `ADOPT.md` prescribes for every adopting repo (the repo following its own rule). `demo/` is now honestly labeled as sample data with no authoritative content.

`meta/` is itself a proper bundle with its own `manifest.json` (4 docs), so an agent can discover the design rationale the same way it discovers any knowledge — load the manifest, match `description`, read what's relevant.

Two latitude notes on naming, in case you want to keep tuning: `meta/` is accurate but slightly jargon-y — `design/` or `decisions/` would read more plainly to a newcomer, at the cost of diverging from the `meta/` term ADOPT.md uses. I kept `meta/` for that consistency. Say the word if you'd rather I switch it.

content: Built a portable OKF-based knowledge methodology repo (OKF floor + Composable Beliefs ceiling), now live and private at ob6to8/knowledge with spec, design record, demo bundle, and a manifest generator. Next: pilot CONVERT on a real store like SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain, or spec the CB↔OKF adapter. (disable recaps in /config)

content.thinking:
I'm tracing where the `meta/` folder concept originated—I introduced it myself in ADOPT.md and again in the spec, not from any external source like OKF or Karpathy. The justification I gave in my previous responses appears to be cut off mid-sentence.

I need to acknowledge that I was being circular in my reasoning—citing my own freshly-written ADOPT.md as established precedent rather than admitting it's a convention I just created this session. It's a sensible organizational choice, but I should own that directly instead of overstating its grounding.

content.text:
Yes — and good catch on the circularity. `meta/` is my coinage, invented earlier *this session* when I first wrote ADOPT.md (the skeleton at `ADOPT.md:20`). It also appears in `spec/KNOWLEDGE.md:140`. It is **not** an OKF, Karpathy, or CB term — OKF just has domain folders plus `index.md`/`log.md`; none of them prescribe a `meta/`.

So when I justified the top-level `meta/` by saying it "matches the `meta/` folder ADOPT.md prescribes," that was **circular** — I was citing my own freshly-written convention as if it were external precedent. It isn't. The move was sound on its merits (promote foundational docs out of the demo), but the appeal to ADOPT.md as authority was self-referential and I overstated it.

Honest version: I coined `meta/` for "a bundle's knowledge about itself," used it in two places, then cited one to justify the other. It stands or falls as a fresh design choice, not as established practice.

Given that, the naming is fully open. Options, neutrally:
- `meta/` — accurate, but jargon-y and (as you noted) was self-justified.
- `design/` or `decisions/` — plainer for a newcomer; reads as "why this exists."
- `genesis/` — emphasizes origin specifically.

Since I invented the term rather than inherited it, there's no consistency cost to changing it — only the two references above need updating. Want me to rename it, or leave `meta/` and just drop the false "ADOPT prescribes it" framing from the docs?

message.content: keep meta, though what is adopt exactly? is that for reference to other agents?

content.thinking:
ADOPT.md is an instruction file I created as an entry point for agents to follow, not primarily for humans—it's a runbook that walks through a step-by-step procedure for bootstrapping a repository.

The key distinction: ADOPT handles the greenfield case (setting up a new, empty repo with this methodology), while CONVERT handles the brownfield case (migrating an existing knowledge store into the format). Both are agent-facing runbooks triggered when a user tells an agent to adopt or convert a repo.

Now I should verify whether the circular framing about meta/ actually exists in the repo docs themselves or just in my chat responses, since the user asked me to keep things meta and I'd mentioned overstating that claim earlier.

content.text:
Right — keeping `meta/`. (No doc change needed: within the repo, `meta/index.md` referencing ADOPT.md is a valid internal cross-reference, since ADOPT.md genuinely does prescribe `meta/`. The overstatement was only in my chat — citing it to you as if it were *external* precedent.)

**What ADOPT.md is:** one of two agent-facing runbooks I wrote. It's not human reference doc — it's a procedure addressed directly to an agent ("You are an agent told: *adopt this methodology for this repo*"). When you point a fresh Claude at this repo and say that phrase, it reads ADOPT.md and executes the steps: read the spec → pick the bundle root → create the skeleton → wire the manifest generator → author the entry index → follow the standing workflow.

It pairs with **CONVERT.md**, the other runbook. The split is greenfield vs brownfield:

| | Trigger | For |
|---|---|---|
| **ADOPT.md** | "adopt this methodology for this repo" | a new/empty repo — set up the format from scratch |
| **CONVERT.md** | "convert this existing store to this format" | a repo with existing ad-hoc knowledge — migrate it in without losing provenance |

`CLAUDE.md` is the router: any agent landing in the repo reads it and gets sent to ADOPT or CONVERT based on what you asked. So the chain is: **you point an agent here + say the trigger phrase → CLAUDE.md routes → ADOPT/CONVERT is the script it runs.** `spec/` is the reference they consult; `meta/` is the why; `demo/` is the worked example.

One gap worth naming: right now ADOPT/CONVERT assume *I* (or another agent) am driving with you in the loop. If you want them to be runnable by an agent with less hand-holding, the next refinement is making each step verifiable (e.g., "run `build_manifest.py --check`; if it fails, the manifest is stale") so the agent can self-check rather than assume success. Want me to tighten them that way, or leave them as guidance?

content: Built the knowledge repo (ob6to8/knowledge, private, pushed): an OKF-floor plus CB-ceiling knowledge methodology with spec, meta design record, demo bundle, and agent runbooks ADOPT/CONVERT. Next: optionally tighten ADOPT/CONVERT with self-check steps, or leave as guidance.

aiTitle: Design knowledge base system using Open Knowledge Format

content.content:
1	# CONVERT — migrating an existing knowledge store to this format
2	
3	You are an agent told: *"convert this existing knowledge store to this format."* The
4	store may be ad-hoc markdown, a `plan.md`/`DESIGN.md` sprawl, a notes folder, a JSON
5	belief file, or some mix. Convert **without losing provenance** and **without inventing
6	rigor that wasn't there.**
7	
8	## 0. Read the spec
9	[`spec/KNOWLEDGE.md`](spec/KNOWLEDGE.md), then [`spec/types.md`](spec/types.md) and
10	[`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md).
11	
12	## 1. Inventory first, transform second
13	Walk the existing store. For each artifact, record: its current path, what it actually
14	is, and which `type` it maps to. Do **not** start editing yet. Produce the inventory as
15	a `type: analysis` doc in `meta/` so the conversion itself is provenanced.
16	
17	Common mappings:
18	| You find | Maps to |
19	|---|---|
20	| `README.md`, runbooks, how-tos | `reference` |
21	| `plan.md`, `USER_STORIES.md`, roadmap | `plan` |
22	| dated `analyses/*.md`, investigations | `analysis` |
23	| ingested articles, transcripts, statements | `source` (immutable) |
24	| pages about an entity/topic, synthesized notes | `concept` |
25	| settled decisions, ADRs | `position` |
26	| session logs, chat exports | `thread` |
27	| a JSON belief/assertion file | keep as CB store; emit `concept`/`source` docs as the OKF projection (see §4) |
28	
29	## 2. Add the frontmatter spine, don't rewrite bodies
30	For each file: prepend the frontmatter spine (`type`, `title`, `description`, `tags`,
31	`status`, `timestamp`). **Write a real relevance hook for `description`** — this is the
32	highest-value part of the conversion. Leave the existing body content intact unless it
33	mixes types (then split). Preserve original dates in `timestamp`.
34	
35	## 3. Preserve provenance
36	- If a file was an immutable capture, mark it `type: source` and **stop editing it**.
37	- If a file's history matters, keep the original in git; the conversion is a commit, so
38	  `git log` remains the deep history.
39	- Convert ad-hoc cross-references into markdown links to the new paths.
40	
41	## 4. Decide the tier per document (default: floor)
42	Everything lands on the **OKF floor** unless it trips the boundary test in
43	[`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md). Only then add `tier: cb`, `id`, and `deps`. If the

46	doc; `deps` → cross-links + `deps` frontmatter). Don't flatten a belief graph into prose
47	and lose the edges.
48	
49	## 5. Generate the manifest and verify

52	```
53	Then spot-check: pick three task-shaped questions a future agent might ask, and confirm

file.content:
# CONVERT — migrating an existing knowledge store to this format

You are an agent told: *"convert this existing knowledge store to this format."* The
store may be ad-hoc markdown, a `plan.md`/`DESIGN.md` sprawl, a notes folder, a JSON
belief file, or some mix. Convert **without losing provenance** and **without inventing
rigor that wasn't there.**

## 0. Read the spec
[`spec/KNOWLEDGE.md`](spec/KNOWLEDGE.md), then [`spec/types.md`](spec/types.md) and
[`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md).

## 1. Inventory first, transform second
Walk the existing store. For each artifact, record: its current path, what it actually
is, and which `type` it maps to. Do **not** start editing yet. Produce the inventory as
a `type: analysis` doc in `meta/` so the conversion itself is provenanced.

Common mappings:
| You find | Maps to |
|---|---|
| `README.md`, runbooks, how-tos | `reference` |
| `plan.md`, `USER_STORIES.md`, roadmap | `plan` |
| dated `analyses/*.md`, investigations | `analysis` |
| ingested articles, transcripts, statements | `source` (immutable) |
| pages about an entity/topic, synthesized notes | `concept` |
| settled decisions, ADRs | `position` |
| session logs, chat exports | `thread` |
| a JSON belief/assertion file | keep as CB store; emit `concept`/`source` docs as the OKF projection (see §4) |

## 2. Add the frontmatter spine, don't rewrite bodies
For each file: prepend the frontmatter spine (`type`, `title`, `description`, `tags`,
`status`, `timestamp`). **Write a real relevance hook for `description`** — this is the
highest-value part of the conversion. Leave the existing body content intact unless it
mixes types (then split). Preserve original dates in `timestamp`.

## 3. Preserve provenance
- If a file was an immutable capture, mark it `type: source` and **stop editing it**.
- If a file's history matters, keep the original in git; the conversion is a commit, so
  `git log` remains the deep history.
- Convert ad-hoc cross-references into markdown links to the new paths.

## 4. Decide the tier per document (default: floor)
Everything lands on the **OKF floor** unless it trips the boundary test in
[`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md). Only then add `tier: cb`, `id`, and `deps`. If the
source store was already a CB/belief graph (e.g. an `assertions.json`), keep that as the
canonical CB store and **emit** an OKF bundle from it (each belief → a typed `concept`
doc; `deps` → cross-links + `deps` frontmatter). Don't flatten a belief graph into prose
and lose the edges.

## 5. Generate the manifest and verify
```
python3 tools/build_manifest.py <root>
```
Then spot-check: pick three task-shaped questions a future agent might ask, and confirm
each is answerable by reading `manifest.json` + at most one or two concept docs. If not,
the `description`s are too vague — fix them, not the bodies.

## 6. Report
Summarize: counts by `type`, what stayed floor vs went CB, anything you could not place
(flag for the human), and the new entry `index.md`.

content.thinking:
I need to make each step self-verifiable so the agent can confirm success or failure rather than assuming things worked. To do this, I'll add validation checks — beyond the existing manifest freshness check — that verify every markdown file has required frontmatter fields like `type`, that the type matches our allowed taxonomy, and that descriptions are present and substantive.

I'm thinking of creating a dedicated `validate.py` tool that runs a comprehensive battery of checks: frontmatter presence, required fields, valid type values, placeholder detection, cross-link resolution, and manifest freshness. The agent can then run this validator and use its exit code to gate progress through ADOPT/CONVERT steps.

Designing the validation checks... Writing validate.py... Now I'm handling link validation—skipping HTTP, mailto, and anchor links, then resolving relative markdown links against the file's directory using path normalization to check if targets exist on disk. For CB tier documents, I need to verify that any dependencies reference IDs from the collected set, which requires a two-pass approach: first collecting all IDs globally, then validating dependencies. I'm also checking that each directory with markdown files has an index.md, and comparing the built manifest against any existing manifest.json to catch staleness. The output will be formatted as "FAIL: <file>: <msg>" and "WARN: ..." with a summary count, exiting with code 1 if there are errors.

The tricky part is that dependencies can reference IDs outside the current bundle root—for example, meta/ documents might depend on spec:tiers which lives in spec/. Validating a single bundle in isolation would incorrectly flag these as failures, so I'm making unresolved dependencies a warning instead of a hard error, with a message indicating the dependency might live in another bundle. Hard errors stay reserved for clearly broken things: missing frontmatter, invalid or missing type, missing or too-short description, placeholder text in frontmatter, missing ID on CB documents, duplicate IDs, stale manifests, and invalid status/timestamp values.

For the description length threshold, 20 characters is reasonable—it's short enough to allow legitimate brief descriptions but long enough to catch placeholders. I'm going to verify the validator against the actual repo structure: meta/ and demo/ bundles should pass without hard errors, with meta's cross-bundle dependencies generating soft warnings as expected.

Now I need to ensure the manifest freshness check works correctly by replicating build_manifest's exact JSON rendering format so the comparison matches. Then I'll write the validation script and update ADOPT and CONVERT with verification gates—each step should include a check like running the validator or confirming the directory structure with ls.

Writing validate.py...

content.text:
Good call — self-verification needs an actual checker, not just prose. I'll add a `tools/validate.py` that runs a battery of hard/soft checks and exits non-zero on failure, then rewrite ADOPT/CONVERT so each mutating step ends in a concrete gate the agent runs.

First the validator:

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Validate a Knowledge bundle. Exit 1 if any hard check fails, 0 otherwise.

This is the self-verification gate for ADOPT.md / CONVERT.md: an agent runs it after
each mutating step and reads pass/fail instead of assuming success. It reuses the
frontmatter parser from build_manifest.py, so it validates the same documented subset.

Usage:
    python3 tools/validate.py <bundle-root>

Hard checks (cause exit 1):
  - every .md has frontmatter with a `type` in the taxonomy
  - `description` present and substantive (>= 20 chars)
  - no unfilled template placeholders (<...>) left in frontmatter values
  - `status` (if present) is a known value; `timestamp` (if present) is YYYY-MM-DD
  - every relative .md link resolves to a file that exists on disk
  - `tier: cb` docs have an `id`; ids are unique across the bundle
  - manifest.json exists and is up to date

Soft checks (printed as WARN, do not fail):
  - a directory with docs but no index.md
  - a `deps` id that doesn't resolve within this bundle (may live in another bundle)
"""
import json
import os
import re
import sys

sys.path.insert(0, os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)))
from build_manifest import parse_frontmatter, build  # noqa: E402

TYPES = {"source", "concept", "thread", "plan", "analysis", "position",
         "decision", "reference", "index", "spec"}
STATUSES = {"active", "superseded", "retracted", "draft"}
DATE_RE = re.compile(r"^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$")
PLACEHOLDER_RE = re.compile(r"<[A-Za-z][^>\n]*>")
# markdown links to a local .md, excluding http(s)/anchors/mailto
LINK_RE = re.compile(r"\[[^\]]*\]\((?!https?:|#|mailto:)([^)#]+\.md)(?:#[^)]*)?\)")


def validate(root):
    root = os.path.abspath(root)
    errors, warnings = [], []
    ids = {}        # id -> relpath (first seen)
    cb_deps = []    # (relpath, [dep ids])
    dirs_with_docs = {}

    md_files = []
    for dirpath, _dirs, files in os.walk(root):
        for f in files:
            if f.endswith(".md"):
                md_files.append(os.path.join(dirpath, f))
    md_files.sort()

    for path in md_files:
        rel = os.path.relpath(path, root)
        text = open(path, encoding="utf-8").read()
        fm = parse_frontmatter(text)
        d = os.path.dirname(rel) or "."
        dirs_with_docs.setdefault(d, set()).add(os.path.basename(rel))

        if not fm:
            errors.append(f"{rel}: no YAML frontmatter")
            continue

        # type
        t = fm.get("type")
        if not t:
            errors.append(f"{rel}: missing required `type`")
        elif t not in TYPES:
            errors.append(f"{rel}: type '{t}' not in taxonomy {sorted(TYPES)}")

        # description
        desc = fm.get("description", "")
        if not isinstance(desc, str) or len(desc.strip()) < 20:
            errors.append(f"{rel}: `description` missing or too short (relevance hook)")

        # placeholders in any frontmatter scalar value
        for k, v in fm.items():
            vals = v if isinstance(v, list) else [v]
            for item in vals:
                if isinstance(item, str) and PLACEHOLDER_RE.search(item):
                    errors.append(f"{rel}: unfilled template placeholder in `{k}`: {item!r}")
                    break

        # status / timestamp
        st = fm.get("status")
        if st is not None and st not in STATUSES:
            errors.append(f"{rel}: status '{st}' not in {sorted(STATUSES)}")
        ts = fm.get("timestamp")
        if ts is not None and not DATE_RE.match(str(ts)):
            errors.append(f"{rel}: timestamp '{ts}' is not YYYY-MM-DD")

        # ids
        _id = fm.get("id")
        if _id:
            if _id in ids:
                errors.append(f"{rel}: duplicate id '{_id}' (also in {ids[_id]})")
            else:
                ids[_id] = rel

        # CB tier integrity
        if fm.get("tier") == "cb":
            if not _id:
                errors.append(f"{rel}: tier: cb requires an `id`")
            deps = fm.get("deps") or []
            cb_deps.append((rel, deps if isinstance(deps, list) else [deps]))
            if st == "superseded" and not fm.get("superseded_by"):
                errors.append(f"{rel}: status superseded but no `superseded_by`")

        # link resolution
        for target in LINK_RE.findall(text):
            resolved = os.path.normpath(os.path.join(os.path.dirname(path), target))
            if not os.path.exists(resolved):
                errors.append(f"{rel}: broken link -> {target}")

    # deps resolve within bundle (soft)
    for rel, deps in cb_deps:
        for dep in deps:
            if dep not in ids:
                warnings.append(f"{rel}: dep '{dep}' not found in this bundle")

    # each doc-bearing directory has an index.md (soft)
    for d, files in sorted(dirs_with_docs.items()):
        if "index.md" not in files:
            warnings.append(f"{d}/: directory has docs but no index.md")

    # manifest freshness (hard)
    man_path = os.path.join(root, "manifest.json")
    expected = json.dumps(build(root), indent=2, ensure_ascii=False) + "\n"
    if not os.path.exists(man_path):
        errors.append("manifest.json: missing (run build_manifest.py)")
    elif open(man_path, encoding="utf-8").read() != expected:
        errors.append("manifest.json: stale (run build_manifest.py)")

    return errors, warnings


def main(argv):
    if len(argv) != 2:
        print(__doc__)
        return 2
    root = argv[1]
    if not os.path.isdir(root):
        print(f"error: {root} is not a directory", file=sys.stderr)
        return 2
    errors, warnings = validate(root)
    for w in warnings:
        print(f"WARN: {w}")
    for e in errors:
        print(f"FAIL: {e}")
    print(f"\n{len(errors)} error(s), {len(warnings)} warning(s) in {root}")
    return 1 if errors else 0


if __name__ == "__main__":
    sys.exit(main(sys.argv))

input.content:
# ADOPT — bootstrapping a repo onto the Knowledge methodology

You are an agent told: *"adopt this methodology for this repo."* Work through the steps.
**Each mutating step ends in a gate you run — do not proceed past a failing gate.** The
two commands you will use to self-verify:

```
python3 tools/build_manifest.py <root>   # (re)generate <root>/manifest.json
python3 tools/validate.py <root>         # exit 0 = pass; prints FAIL: lines + a count
```

`validate.py` is the source of truth for "did that step actually work." Treat a non-zero
exit, or any `FAIL:` line, as a stop condition. `WARN:` lines are advisory.

## 0. Read the spec
Read in order: [`spec/KNOWLEDGE.md`](spec/KNOWLEDGE.md), [`spec/types.md`](spec/types.md),
[`spec/frontmatter.md`](spec/frontmatter.md), [`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md). Skim
[`demo/`](demo/) for a worked example and [`meta/`](meta/) for this repo's design record.

**Gate (self-check):** before continuing, you must be able to answer without re-reading:
(a) which field is the relevance hook, (b) the boundary test that promotes a doc to the
CB tier, (c) what `manifest.json` is for. If you can't, re-read.

## 1. Decide the bundle root
Pick where knowledge lives in the target repo — typically `knowledge/` or `docs/`. This
is the **bundle root** (`<root>` below). Copy [`tools/build_manifest.py`](tools/build_manifest.py)
and [`tools/validate.py`](tools/validate.py) into the repo (e.g. `<root>/../tools/` or
`<root>/_tools/`); adjust the command paths accordingly.

**Gate:** `python3 <path>/validate.py --help` prints usage (confirms the tools run here).

## 2. Create the skeleton
```
<root>/
├── index.md            # type: index — what this repo's knowledge covers
├── <domain>/           # one folder per natural domain
│   └── index.md
└── meta/               # threads, plans, analyses, positions about the repo itself
    └── index.md
```
Copy [`templates/`](templates/) as starting points. **Fill every `<placeholder>`** —
unfilled `<...>` in frontmatter is a hard failure. Default every doc to the **OKF floor**
(omit `tier`); reach for `tier: cb` only when [`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md)'s boundary
test trips (and then the doc needs an `id`).

**Gate:**
```
python3 <path>/build_manifest.py <root> && python3 <path>/validate.py <root>
```
Must exit 0. Fix every `FAIL:` (missing `type`, weak `description`, leftover placeholder,
broken link) before continuing.

## 3. Author the entry index
`<root>/index.md`'s `description` is the relevance hook for the **whole repo's
knowledge** — one line that tells a fresh agent whether this repo is relevant to its task.
Link from it to each domain's `index.md`.

**Gate:** re-run step 2's command (exit 0). Then confirm `manifest.json`'s first entry
for `index.md` has a `description` you'd be willing to route on.

## 4. Backfill initial content
Populate domains with real `concept`/`reference`/`source` docs (not just indexes). Every
new or changed file means a regenerate + validate.

**Gate (the loop):** after any edit, run
`build_manifest.py <root> && validate.py <root>` until it exits 0 with zero `FAIL:`.

## 5. The standing workflow (day-to-day, after adoption)
- **End of a working session** → write one `type: thread` doc in `meta/`; its
  `description` is the outcome, linking to what it produced. (Stops ad-hoc threads.)
- **New external input** → capture as `type: source` (immutable), then **update the
  relevant `type: concept` docs** — don't let sources pile up unsynthesized.
- **A settled stance** → `type: position`; consider CB-tier promotion.
- **After any change** → `build_manifest.py <root> && validate.py <root>`, exit 0.
- **Wire it so it can't drift:** add `validate.py <root>` to CI or a pre-commit hook.
  (`build_manifest.py <root> --check` alone catches only manifest staleness; `validate.py`
  catches that *and* schema/link breakage.)

## 6. Done when (acceptance test)
Adoption is complete only when **all** hold:
1. `python3 <path>/validate.py <root>` exits 0 (no `FAIL:`).
2. `<root>/manifest.json` exists and lists every doc with a real `description`.
3. Pick **three** task-shaped questions a future agent might ask this repo. Each must be
   answerable by reading `manifest.json` + at most two docs it points to. If not, the
   `description`s are too vague — fix them (not the bodies) and re-run.
4. Report to the human: doc counts by `type`, which docs went CB-tier and why, and the
   entry `index.md` description.

Read in order: [`spec/KNOWLEDGE.md`](spec/KNOWLEDGE.md), [`spec/types.md`](spec/types.md),
[`spec/frontmatter.md`](spec/frontmatter.md), [`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md). Skim
[`demo/`](demo/) for a worked example and [`meta/`](meta/) for this repo's design record.

**Gate (self-check):** before continuing, you must be able to answer without re-reading:
(a) which field is the relevance hook, (b) the boundary test that promotes a doc to the
CB tier, (c) what `manifest.json` is for. If you can't, re-read.

## 1. Decide the bundle root
Pick where knowledge lives in the target repo — typically `knowledge/` or `docs/`. This
is the **bundle root** (`<root>` below). Copy [`tools/build_manifest.py`](tools/build_manifest.py)
and [`tools/validate.py`](tools/validate.py) into the repo (e.g. `<root>/../tools/` or
`<root>/_tools/`); adjust the command paths accordingly.

**Gate:** `python3 <path>/validate.py --help` prints usage (confirms the tools run here).

## 2. Create the skeleton
```
<root>/
├── index.md            # type: index — what this repo's knowledge covers
├── <domain>/           # one folder per natural domain
│   └── index.md
└── meta/               # threads, plans, analyses, positions about the repo itself
    └── index.md
```
Copy [`templates/`](templates/) as starting points. **Fill every `<placeholder>`** —
unfilled `<...>` in frontmatter is a hard failure. Default every doc to the **OKF floor**
(omit `tier`); reach for `tier: cb` only when [`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md)'s boundary
test trips (and then the doc needs an `id`).

**Gate:**
```
python3 <path>/build_manifest.py <root> && python3 <path>/validate.py <root>
```
Must exit 0. Fix every `FAIL:` (missing `type`, weak `description`, leftover placeholder,
broken link) before continuing.

## 3. Author the entry index
`<root>/index.md`'s `description` is the relevance hook for the **whole repo's
knowledge** — one line that tells a fresh agent whether this repo is relevant to its task.
Link from it to each domain's `index.md`.

**Gate:** re-run step 2's command (exit 0). Then confirm `manifest.json`'s first entry
for `index.md` has a `description` you'd be willing to route on.

## 4. Backfill initial content
Populate domains with real `concept`/`reference`/`source` docs (not just indexes). Every
new or changed file means a regenerate + validate.

**Gate (the loop):** after any edit, run
`build_manifest.py <root> && validate.py <root>` until it exits 0 with zero `FAIL:`.

## 5. The standing workflow (day-to-day, after adoption)
- **End of a working session** → write one `type: thread` doc in `meta/`; its
  `description` is the outcome, linking to what it produced. (Stops ad-hoc threads.)
- **New external input** → capture as `type: source` (immutable), then **update the
  relevant `type: concept` docs** — don't let sources pile up unsynthesized.
- **A settled stance** → `type: position`; consider CB-tier promotion.
- **After any change** → `build_manifest.py <root> && validate.py <root>`, exit 0.
- **Wire it so it can't drift:** add `validate.py <root>` to CI or a pre-commit hook.
  (`build_manifest.py <root> --check` alone catches only manifest staleness; `validate.py`
  catches that *and* schema/link breakage.)

## 6. Done when (acceptance test)
Adoption is complete only when **all** hold:
1. `python3 <path>/validate.py <root>` exits 0 (no `FAIL:`).
2. `<root>/manifest.json` exists and lists every doc with a real `description`.
3. Pick **three** task-shaped questions a future agent might ask this repo. Each must be
   answerable by reading `manifest.json` + at most two docs it points to. If not, the
   `description`s are too vague — fix them (not the bodies) and re-run.
4. Report to the human: doc counts by `type`, which docs went CB-tier and why, and the
   entry `index.md` description.
