# ADOPT — bootstrapping a repo onto the Knowledge methodology

You are an agent told: *"adopt this methodology for this repo."* Follow these steps.

## 0. Read the spec
Read in order: [`spec/KNOWLEDGE.md`](spec/KNOWLEDGE.md), [`spec/types.md`](spec/types.md),
[`spec/frontmatter.md`](spec/frontmatter.md), [`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md). Skim
[`demo/`](demo/) for a worked example, and [`meta/`](meta/) for this repo's own design record.

## 1. Decide the bundle root
Pick where knowledge lives in the target repo — typically `knowledge/` or `docs/`. This
becomes the **bundle root**. Everything below is relative to it.

## 2. Create the skeleton
```
<root>/
├── index.md            # type: index — what this repo's knowledge covers
├── <domain>/           # one folder per natural domain
│   └── index.md
└── meta/               # threads, plans, analyses, positions about the repo itself
    └── index.md
```
Copy [`templates/`](templates/) as starting points. Default every doc to the **OKF
floor** (`tier` omitted). Only reach for CB (`tier: cb`) when the boundary test in
[`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md) trips.

## 3. Wire the manifest generator
Copy [`tools/build_manifest.py`](tools/build_manifest.py) into the repo (or reference it).
Run it against the bundle root:
```
python3 tools/build_manifest.py <root>
```
This writes `<root>/manifest.json`. Re-run it after any change to the bundle. Wire it
into CI or a pre-commit hook so the manifest never drifts from the docs.

## 4. Author the entry index
`<root>/index.md` is the front door. Its `description` frontmatter is the relevance hook
for the **whole repo's knowledge** — write it so a fresh agent knows in one line whether
this repo is relevant to its task.

## 5. The standing workflow (what changes day-to-day)
- **End of a working session** → write one `type: thread` doc in `meta/`. Its
  `description` is the outcome; link to the plans/analyses/concepts it produced. This is
  how threads stop being persisted ad hoc.
- **New external input** → capture it as `type: source` (immutable), then **update the
  relevant `type: concept` docs** — don't let sources pile up unsynthesized.
- **A settled stance** → `type: position`. Consider promoting to CB tier.
- **After any change** → regenerate `manifest.json`.

## 6. How a future agent reads it back
Load `manifest.json` first → match task against `description`s → load only the matching
concept docs → drill into `source` docs only for provenance. Never bulk-load the bundle.

input.content:
# CONVERT — migrating an existing knowledge store to this format

You are an agent told: *"convert this existing knowledge store to this format."* The
store may be ad-hoc markdown, a `plan.md`/`DESIGN.md` sprawl, a notes folder, a JSON
belief file, or some mix. Convert **without losing provenance** and **without inventing
rigor that wasn't there.**

**Each mutating step ends in a gate you run — do not proceed past a failing gate.**
Self-verification commands (copy `tools/build_manifest.py` and `tools/validate.py` into
the target repo first):

```
python3 <path>/build_manifest.py <root>   # (re)generate <root>/manifest.json
python3 <path>/validate.py <root>         # exit 0 = pass; FAIL: lines stop you
```

## 0. Read the spec
[`spec/KNOWLEDGE.md`](spec/KNOWLEDGE.md), then [`spec/types.md`](spec/types.md) and
[`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md).

**Gate (self-check):** you must be able to name which artifact kinds map to which `type`,
and the rule for what stays on the OKF floor. If not, re-read `types.md`/`tiers.md`.

## 1. Snapshot, then inventory (transform comes later)
First ensure the original is recoverable: confirm the store is committed in git (or copy
it aside). Then walk it and record, for each artifact: current path, what it actually is,
and target `type`. Write the inventory as a `type: analysis` doc in `<root>/meta/` so the
conversion itself is provenanced.

Common mappings:
| You find | Maps to |
|---|---|
| `README.md`, runbooks, how-tos | `reference` |
| `plan.md`, `USER_STORIES.md`, roadmap | `plan` |
| dated `analyses/*.md`, investigations | `analysis` |
| ingested articles, transcripts, statements | `source` (immutable) |
| pages about an entity/topic, synthesized notes | `concept` |
| settled decisions, ADRs | `position` |
| session logs, chat exports | `thread` |
| a JSON belief/assertion file | keep as CB store; **emit** `concept`/`source` docs from it (see §4) |

**Gate:** `git status` shows the original tracked/clean (recoverable), and the inventory
doc exists. Do not edit any source file until both are true.

## 2. Add the frontmatter spine, don't rewrite bodies
For each file: prepend the spine (`type`, `title`, `description`, `tags`, `status`,
`timestamp`). **Write a real relevance hook for `description`** (≥20 chars, "Use when…")
— the highest-value part of the conversion. Preserve original dates in `timestamp`. Leave
body content intact unless a file mixes types — then split it.

**Gate (incremental):** after converting each batch,
`build_manifest.py <root> && validate.py <root>` and clear every `FAIL:`. The validator
catches the conversion's typical misses: missing `type`, vague/short `description`,
leftover `<placeholder>`, bad `timestamp`, broken cross-link.

## 3. Preserve provenance
- A file that was an immutable capture → `type: source`; **stop editing its body**.
- Deep history stays in git: the conversion is a commit, so `git log` preserves the past.
- Convert ad-hoc cross-references into markdown links to the new paths. (The validator
  fails on any link whose target file doesn't exist — use it to find the ones you missed.)

**Gate:** `validate.py <root>` reports **zero** `broken link` failures.

## 4. Decide the tier per document (default: floor)
Everything lands on the **OKF floor** unless [`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md)'s boundary
test trips; only then add `tier: cb`, `id`, `deps`. If the source was already a CB/belief
graph (e.g. `assertions.json`), **keep it as the canonical store and emit an OKF
projection** (each belief → a typed `concept` doc; `deps` → cross-links + `deps`
frontmatter). Don't flatten a belief graph into prose and lose the edges.

**Gate:** for every `tier: cb` doc, `validate.py` confirms it has an `id` and ids are
unique (it FAILs otherwise). Cross-bundle `deps` may show as `WARN` — that's expected.

## 5. Final manifest + acceptance test
```
python3 <path>/build_manifest.py <root> && python3 <path>/validate.py <root>
```
Must exit 0. Then pick **three** task-shaped questions a future agent might ask, and
confirm each is answerable from `manifest.json` + at most two docs. If not, the
`description`s are too vague — fix them, not the bodies, and re-run.

## 6. Report
Summarize: counts by `type`, what stayed floor vs went CB, anything you could not place
(flag for the human), the new entry `index.md`, and confirmation that `validate.py` exits
0. Note that the original remains in git history for any unconverted detail.

```

toolUseResult.originalFile:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Validate a Knowledge bundle. Exit 1 if any hard check fails, 0 otherwise.

This is the self-verification gate for ADOPT.md / CONVERT.md: an agent runs it after
each mutating step and reads pass/fail instead of assuming success. It reuses the
frontmatter parser from build_manifest.py, so it validates the same documented subset.

Usage:
    python3 tools/validate.py <bundle-root>

Hard checks (cause exit 1):
  - every .md has frontmatter with a `type` in the taxonomy
  - `description` present and substantive (>= 20 chars)
  - no unfilled template placeholders (<...>) left in frontmatter values
  - `status` (if present) is a known value; `timestamp` (if present) is YYYY-MM-DD
  - every relative .md link resolves to a file that exists on disk
  - `tier: cb` docs have an `id`; ids are unique across the bundle
  - manifest.json exists and is up to date

Soft checks (printed as WARN, do not fail):
  - a directory with docs but no index.md
  - a `deps` id that doesn't resolve within this bundle (may live in another bundle)
"""
import json
import os
import re
import sys

sys.path.insert(0, os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)))
from build_manifest import parse_frontmatter, build  # noqa: E402

TYPES = {"source", "concept", "thread", "plan", "analysis", "position",
         "decision", "reference", "index", "spec"}
STATUSES = {"active", "superseded", "retracted", "draft"}
DATE_RE = re.compile(r"^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$")
PLACEHOLDER_RE = re.compile(r"<[A-Za-z][^>\n]*>")
# markdown links to a local .md, excluding http(s)/anchors/mailto
LINK_RE = re.compile(r"\[[^\]]*\]\((?!https?:|#|mailto:)([^)#]+\.md)(?:#[^)]*)?\)")


def validate(root):
    root = os.path.abspath(root)
    errors, warnings = [], []
    ids = {}        # id -> relpath (first seen)
    cb_deps = []    # (relpath, [dep ids])
    dirs_with_docs = {}

    md_files = []
    for dirpath, _dirs, files in os.walk(root):
        for f in files:
            if f.endswith(".md"):
                md_files.append(os.path.join(dirpath, f))
    md_files.sort()

    for path in md_files:
        rel = os.path.relpath(path, root)
        text = open(path, encoding="utf-8").read()
        fm = parse_frontmatter(text)
        d = os.path.dirname(rel) or "."
        dirs_with_docs.setdefault(d, set()).add(os.path.basename(rel))

        if not fm:
            errors.append(f"{rel}: no YAML frontmatter")
            continue

        # type
        t = fm.get("type")
        if not t:
            errors.append(f"{rel}: missing required `type`")
        elif t not in TYPES:
            errors.append(f"{rel}: type '{t}' not in taxonomy {sorted(TYPES)}")

        # description
        desc = fm.get("description", "")
        if not isinstance(desc, str) or len(desc.strip()) < 20:
            errors.append(f"{rel}: `description` missing or too short (relevance hook)")

        # placeholders in any frontmatter scalar value
        for k, v in fm.items():
            vals = v if isinstance(v, list) else [v]
            for item in vals:
                if isinstance(item, str) and PLACEHOLDER_RE.search(item):
                    errors.append(f"{rel}: unfilled template placeholder in `{k}`: {item!r}")
                    break

        # status / timestamp
        st = fm.get("status")
        if st is not None and st not in STATUSES:
            errors.append(f"{rel}: status '{st}' not in {sorted(STATUSES)}")
        ts = fm.get("timestamp")
        if ts is not None and not DATE_RE.match(str(ts)):
            errors.append(f"{rel}: timestamp '{ts}' is not YYYY-MM-DD")

        # ids
        _id = fm.get("id")
        if _id:
            if _id in ids:
                errors.append(f"{rel}: duplicate id '{_id}' (also in {ids[_id]})")
            else:
                ids[_id] = rel

        # CB tier integrity
        if fm.get("tier") == "cb":
            if not _id:
                errors.append(f"{rel}: tier: cb requires an `id`")
            deps = fm.get("deps") or []
            cb_deps.append((rel, deps if isinstance(deps, list) else [deps]))
            if st == "superseded" and not fm.get("superseded_by"):
                errors.append(f"{rel}: status superseded but no `superseded_by`")

        # link resolution
        for target in LINK_RE.findall(text):
            resolved = os.path.normpath(os.path.join(os.path.dirname(path), target))
            if not os.path.exists(resolved):
                errors.append(f"{rel}: broken link -> {target}")

    # deps resolve within bundle (soft)
    for rel, deps in cb_deps:
        for dep in deps:
            if dep not in ids:
                warnings.append(f"{rel}: dep '{dep}' not found in this bundle")

    # each doc-bearing directory has an index.md (soft)
    for d, files in sorted(dirs_with_docs.items()):
        if "index.md" not in files:
            warnings.append(f"{d}/: directory has docs but no index.md")

    # manifest freshness (hard)
    man_path = os.path.join(root, "manifest.json")
    expected = json.dumps(build(root), indent=2, ensure_ascii=False) + "\n"
    if not os.path.exists(man_path):
        errors.append("manifest.json: missing (run build_manifest.py)")
    elif open(man_path, encoding="utf-8").read() != expected:
        errors.append("manifest.json: stale (run build_manifest.py)")

    return errors, warnings


def main(argv):
    if len(argv) != 2:
        print(__doc__)
        return 2
    root = argv[1]
    if not os.path.isdir(root):
        print(f"error: {root} is not a directory", file=sys.stderr)
        return 2
    errors, warnings = validate(root)
    for w in warnings:
        print(f"WARN: {w}")
    for e in errors:
        print(f"FAIL: {e}")
    print(f"\n{len(errors)} error(s), {len(warnings)} warning(s) in {root}")
    return 1 if errors else 0


if __name__ == "__main__":
    sys.exit(main(sys.argv))

content.text:
Done and pushed to `ob6to8/knowledge`.

**What changed:** ADOPT and CONVERT are no longer "follow these steps and assume it worked" — every mutating step now ends in a **gate the agent runs**, with a non-zero exit or any `FAIL:` line as a hard stop. Both end in a "Done when" acceptance test rather than a vibe.

**The new checker — `tools/validate.py`** (stdlib-only, reuses the manifest parser). Hard checks (exit 1):
- frontmatter present, `type` in the closed taxonomy
- `description` substantive (≥20 chars) — catches lazy hooks
- no unfilled `<placeholder>` left in frontmatter
- valid `status` / `YYYY-MM-DD timestamp`
- every relative `.md` link resolves on disk
- `tier: cb` docs have an `id`; ids unique across the bundle
- `manifest.json` exists and is fresh

Soft warnings (exit 0): directory missing an `index.md`, cross-bundle `deps`.

**Proven both directions:** clean bundles pass (exit 0); an injected doc with a bad type + short description + broken link + stale manifest produced 4 `FAIL:` lines and exit 1; cleanup returned to 0. So the gate genuinely discriminates.

**The agent's loop is now mechanical:** `build_manifest.py <root> && validate.py <root>` → read `FAIL:` lines → fix → repeat until exit 0. ADOPT step 5 also tells it to wire `validate.py` into CI/pre-commit so the invariants can't silently drift after adoption (and notes that `build_manifest --check` alone only catches manifest staleness, not schema/link breakage — so `validate.py` is the stronger gate).

One honest limit worth naming: the validator checks *structural* correctness (schema, links, freshness), not *semantic* quality — it can't tell you a `description` is accurate or a doc is filed under the right `type`. That's what the "three task-shaped questions" acceptance test in step 6 is for; it stays a judgment call the agent (or you) makes. If you want, I can add a lightweight `--strict` mode later that also flags soft smells (e.g. descriptions not starting with "Use when…/Covers…", or concept docs with no `## Sources`), but I left those out to avoid false positives forcing busywork.

content.thinking:
The working tree is clean and everything's already pushed, so there's nothing new to commit. Now I need to think through what it would actually take to port the Python build and validation scripts over to Elixir—considering why they'd want to make that move in the first place.

The CB ecosystem runs on Elixir and mix, so having the knowledge repo integrate as mix tasks would make sense for consistency, even though Python's current approach means any agent can run it without dependencies. For an Elixir port, I'd need to handle frontmatter parsing (either using a YAML library like YamlElixir or hand-rolling the documented subset with pattern matching), file walking (which is trivial with Path.wildcard), and manifest generation using Jason for JSON encoding. The tricky part is ensuring the output matches byte-for-byte with the Python version for the `--check` validation to work correctly.

All the validation checks translate cleanly to Elixir — regex patterns, MapSet for taxonomy lookups, reduce for accumulating errors, and File.exists? for link resolution. For invocation, I could either create mix tasks that fit the `mix bs` pattern (requiring the knowledge repo to be a mix project) or compile an escript executable that runs standalone without mix. The real cost is...

The portability tradeoff is the deciding factor. Python's stdlib runs anywhere Python 3 exists, which is nearly universal on dev machines and in this environment. Elixir requires the BEAM toolchain installed. If the goal is to point any fresh agent at the repo and have it run the gate, Python wins on portability. Elixir only makes sense if the knowledge tooling lives inside the CB mix project as mix tasks alongside `mix bs`, or if I want to share code with CB — like the CB↔OKF adapter, which should naturally be Elixir since CB is Elixir. The recommendation: keep the standalone manifest and validate gates in Python for zero-dependency portability, unless the entire knowledge toolchain becomes a mix project. The CB↔OKF adapter itself should be Elixir. Dual maintenance would be a code smell, though I could theoretically support both paths.

For effort, the frontmatter parser subset is roughly 1-2 hours or 20 minutes with a YAML dependency, building the manifest equivalent with byte-stable ordering takes about an hour, and the validate equivalent is closer to 2 hours.

Setting up the mix task wrappers and exit codes is about 30 minutes, and porting the self-test suite adds another hour. The main gotchas are ensuring byte-stable JSON output (Jason doesn't guarantee key order, so I'd need to use ordered keyword lists or CB's existing ordered_keys approach), handling exit codes correctly in mix tasks with `System.halt`, and matching the Python YAML subset exactly if hand-rolling the parser.

The real decision point is whether to keep Python as the universal gate and write only the CB↔OKF adapter in Elixir for portability, or port the whole tooling to Elixir mix tasks for ecosystem consistency. I'll recommend option A—keeping Python as the gate—but I can execute option B if they prefer full Elixir integration.

content.text:
Nothing to commit — working tree is clean and `d5e2967` is already pushed to `origin/main`. (The repeated "retry" lines look like stray input; I haven't re-run anything.)

## Porting the Python to Elixir — what it'd take

The two scripts are ~120 and ~180 lines of straightforward logic. The code port is easy; the real decision is **runtime/dependency tradeoff**, not effort.

**The work itself (~half a day with tests):**

| Piece | Python today | Elixir equivalent | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frontmatter parse | hand-rolled subset (~60 lines) | `YamlElixir` dep *or* hand-roll with pattern matching | Hand-roll ≈ same size; `~r`/`String` make it clean |
| File walk | `os.walk` | `Path.wildcard("#{root}/**/*.md")` | one line |
| Manifest render | `json.dumps(indent=2)` | `Jason.encode!(_, pretty: true)` | **the one gotcha** — see below |
| Checks | sets + regex + `os.path.exists` | `MapSet`, `~r//`, `File.exists?`, `Enum.reduce` | direct translation |
| CLI + exit codes | `sys.exit(n)` | `mix` task + `System.halt(n)` *or* escript | see invocation |

**The one real gotcha — byte-stable output.** Python dicts preserve insertion order, so the manifest is deterministic for free. Jason does *not* guarantee map key order, which breaks the `--check`/freshness comparison. You'd need ordered encoding (a keyword list or `{key, val}` tuples in canonical order). CB already solved exactly this — `lib/cb/belief.ex` uses `@ordered_keys` for byte-stable serialization — so it's a copy of a pattern you own, not new design.

**Invocation — two shapes:**
- **Mix tasks** (`mix knowledge.manifest <root>`, `mix knowledge.validate <root>`) — fits the `mix bs` pattern, but the knowledge repo must become a mix project and the gate now requires the Elixir toolchain.
- **Escript** — single compiled binary, no `mix` needed at runtime, but needs a build step and the BEAM present.

## My recommendation: don't port the gate; port only the adapter

The Python gate's whole value is **zero-dependency portability** — `python3` is on every dev machine and in this environment; "point any fresh agent at the repo and it runs `validate.py`" works with nothing installed. Elixir requires the toolchain, which *subtracts* from that goal. Porting the gate to Elixir buys you ecosystem consistency you don't need for a 300-line linter.

Where Elixir genuinely belongs is the **CB↔OKF adapter** (`emit-okf`/`ingest-okf`) — that lives *inside* the CB mix project, shares CB's belief schema and `Jason` ordering, and is the thing that actually needs to be Elixir. That port/build is real and worth doing.

So: keep `build_manifest.py` + `validate.py` as the universal floor gate; write the adapter in Elixir in CB. Avoid dual-maintaining the same linter in two languages.

**The exception** that flips this: if you want the knowledge tooling to *be* part of the CB mix app (one toolchain, `mix knowledge.*` alongside `mix bs`, shared frontmatter parser with the adapter), then full port makes sense and the frontmatter parser becomes shared code with the adapter — that's the only scenario where the port pays for itself.

Want me to (a) write the Elixir port as `mix knowledge.*` tasks now, (b) leave the gate in Python and instead spec/scaffold the CB↔OKF adapter in Elixir, or (c) just stop here?

content: Building a portable knowledge-base methodology (OKF floor + CB ceiling) in the new private repo ob6to8/knowledge, now complete with spec, runbooks, and a self-verifying Python gate. Next: decide whether to port the tooling to Elixir or instead build the CB↔OKF adapter.

message.content: leaning towards this being intergrated with the cb mix app, despite being a separate repo - though maybe this should even be folded into cb, as an integration layer for those using okf?

content.thinking:
I'm thinking through where this knowledge tooling should actually live architecturally. The CB mix app seems like a natural home, but I need to consider whether folding the knowledge repo into CB as an integration layer makes sense for their positioning and use cases. This is a strategic decision that deserves careful thought before jumping into implementation.

The core tension is that the knowledge spec itself is deliberately vendor-neutral and format-focused — it's meant to be adoptable by anyone, not just CB users. Embedding it inside CB couples a universal standard to one platform, which undermines the whole "format not platform" philosophy the spec is built on. The tooling and the CB↔OKF adapter are different stories — those could reasonably live in CB — but the spec itself should probably stay independent and portable. The practical tension is that a fresh agent adopting the methodology from the standalone `knowledge` repo wouldn't have the Elixir tooling unless CB is also present, which breaks the zero-dependency portability. I'm working through whether to keep Python tools as the portable baseline while CB offers mix tasks for its ecosystem, or consolidate everything into CB's toolchain and drop Python entirely — the first keeps clean layering but risks dual maintenance, while the second gives one canonical toolchain at the cost of making CB the required home for the methodology.

The cleanest path seems to be a three-tier model: `knowledge` stays the vendor-neutral spec with Python reference tools as the portable floor, CB gains an OKF integration layer with the adapter plus optional mix-task wrappers that share Elixir modules for manifest and validation logic, positioning CB as "an OKF-speaking belief system" without coupling the standard itself to any single platform.

To handle the dual-maintenance concern of having the same validation logic in both Python and Elixir, I'd frame this as a standards problem: declare the spec as the source of truth and treat both implementations as conformance implementations, backed by a shared test corpus of sample bundles and expected outputs that both must pass. This catches drift through a conformance suite rather than code synchronization, which is how mature standards actually work. That reframes the whole thing nicely — `knowledge` is the standard, CB is one conformant implementation of it, and the integration layer (the adapter and mix tasks) is exactly where CB's OKF support belongs. I'm deciding whether to use AskUserQuestion here. This is genuinely the user's architectural call—where the code lives materially changes what I build next—so the tool fits. But I should present my analysis and recommendation first in prose, then use the structured question to lock in the direction. I'll frame three clear options with the disentanglement approach as the recommended path.

For the previews, I'm sketching the repo layout for each option to make the spatial differences concrete—showing where the spec, tools, and adapters live in each scenario. This should help them visualize the tradeoffs.

I should also acknowledge that I've already pushed the standalone repo, so Options 2 and 3 would involve some migration work, though it's not prohibitive at this early stage. Then I'll write a tight prose analysis and pose the core question.

content.text:
Worth slowing down here — "fold into CB" and "integrate the tooling with CB" are two different moves, and one of them quietly inverts the architecture you just designed. Let me disentangle, because the `knowledge` repo is actually three things with different natural homes:

1. **The spec/methodology** (OKF profile, two-tier rule, taxonomy, ADOPT/CONVERT, templates) — vendor-neutral, applies to finances/home/software alike. CB is only its *opt-in ceiling*.
2. **The reference tooling** (manifest + validate) — currently portable Python.
3. **The CB↔OKF adapter** (emit/ingest) — depends on CB's belief schema.

**The layering trap.** We established CB sits *above* OKF (OKF = floor, CB = ceiling). If the OKF spec becomes a folder *inside* CB, the dependency arrow flips — the general substrate now lives inside the specialized consumer. That also contradicts the spec's own "format not platform" thesis: the moment OKF-for-your-repos is a CB submodule, someone adopting it for "finances" needs the CB app to use it. The floor shouldn't require the ceiling.

**What "integration layer for OKF users" correctly describes is #3, the adapter** — and that unambiguously belongs in CB. "CB is an OKF-speaking belief system" is exactly the positioning from your Part 1 ("the typed, verifiable dialect of OKF"). So the instinct is right; it just applies to the adapter, not the spec.

**The resolution that gives you the shared-toolchain win without the inversion:** `knowledge` stays a standalone **standard**; CB becomes one **conformant consumer** of it. Standards have multiple implementations + a conformance suite — OKF itself ships a reference agent. So:
- `knowledge` repo = spec + Python *reference* tooling (the portable, adopt-anywhere gate).
- CB = an **OKF integration layer**: the adapter, plus Elixir `mix knowledge.*` validators that reuse a shared frontmatter module with the adapter.
- A **conformance fixture set** in the spec repo (sample bundles + expected `validate` output) becomes the SSOT both implementations must pass — that's how you have Python *and* Elixir validators without dual-maintenance rot. Drift is caught by a shared test corpus, not by keeping two codebases in sync by hand.

This also keeps CB's identity clean (it's a reasoning DAG that *speaks* OKF, not a knowledge-format standards body), and preserves "point any agent at `knowledge` and it runs the gate with zero install."

Note this is partly a **positioning/direction call** (it shapes the "CB as OKF dialect" story) — per your direction-repo convention, once you settle it I'd route the rationale to `cb-direction`.

Let me lock the direction before I build anything:

The user went with Option 1, so the knowledge repo stays as the standalone standard with Python reference tools, and we're adding a conformance fixture set there as the shared source of truth that both implementations test against. The CB integration layer will be separate work in the CB repo. Now I need to figure out what the immediate next steps are in this repo to make that happen.

The conformance suite is the clear atomic deliverable—it makes the standard testable and gives the future Elixir port a concrete target to hit. I'm designing the structure with a fixtures directory split into valid and invalid bundles, plus expected output files that the reference validator runs against. The tricky part is handling the validator's output format, which includes absolute paths and other details that need to be normalized for reliable comparison across runs. I'm sketching out a concrete set of test fixtures: valid ones with minimal docs and a tier:cb example to verify passing cases, and invalid ones targeting specific failure modes like bad types, weak descriptions, broken links, missing IDs on tier:cb docs, duplicate IDs, and stale manifests. I'll keep the set focused and manageable rather than exhaustive.

The key insight is that instead of hand-writing expected outputs, I should run the validator against the fixtures to generate the reference expected results, ensuring they match the implementation by construction. But then I'm realizing the real challenge: defining what "conformance" actually means across different implementations. If I require exact string matching, an Elixir rewrite would need to reproduce Python's exact wording, which is brittle. Better to define conformance at a machine-readable level—capturing exit codes and structured check results (severity, file path, check category) rather than exact English text, possibly via a JSON output format.

So I'm adding a `--json` flag to the validator that emits stable check codes instead of human-readable messages, letting any implementation produce identical structured results regardless of wording. The conformance contract becomes: exit code plus a sorted set of severity-code-path tuples extracted from that JSON, which run.sh can normalize and diff against expected output.

I'm defining the error and warning codes upfront—things like `no_frontmatter`, `type_not_in_taxonomy`, `description_missing_or_short`, `broken_link`, `manifest_stale`, and a few others—then refactoring validate.py to track these as structured records while keeping the human output unchanged. The conformance suite will live in its own directory with a README documenting the contract, a bash runner that normalizes the JSON output via a Python helper, and fixture directories with expected results.

For the runner, I'm capturing both the exit code and JSON output from validate.py, then piping through a normalizer that produces the canonical form—exit code followed by sorted severity/code/path tuples. The tricky part is deciding whether to bake normalization into validate.py itself with a `--format=conformance` flag or keep it separate in the conformance directory. Keeping it separate feels cleaner, but then I need to handle the fact that JSON paths are absolute and will vary across runs.

Actually, I'm realizing the simplest approach: make `--json` output a minimal, deterministic structure with only relative paths and no human messages—just the essential fields like path and code, sorted consistently. Then the expected files are exactly that JSON output, and the test runner just diffs them directly without needing any post-processing. The exit code is still meaningful (1 on errors), but the JSON itself captures the pass/fail state, so diffing the JSON is the real contract.

Now I need to implement this: refactor validate.py to track error codes alongside messages, add the `--json` flag to emit that minimal sorted structure, generate conformance fixtures (both valid and invalid cases), run the validator to produce the expected JSON files, review them for correctness, write the test runner script, and then verify everything passes.

Refactoring validate.py with code-carrying records...

Human output stays the same (FAIL/WARN lines then summary), but internally I'm building lists of error and warning dicts with path, code, and message. For JSON output, I'll sort by code then path and emit just the path and code fields.

For the fixtures themselves, I want each one to be minimal and focused—every invalid fixture should trigger exactly one error, so I'll include a valid index.md and a fresh manifest to avoid incidental warnings, except where the fixture is specifically testing manifest staleness or missing manifests.

For the targeted error cases: a bad-type doc gets valid description and status but wrong type value, a weak-description doc has valid type but short description, duplicate IDs means two otherwise-valid docs sharing an id, a cb-tier doc without id triggers only that error, placeholder titles get flagged, and broken links in doc bodies get caught.

For manifest issues: stale-manifest has outdated content, missing-manifest has no manifest file at all. Bad timestamps and statuses are straightforward validation failures.

Rather than create 10+ separate fixtures with multiple files each, I can streamline by putting the defect directly in a single `index.md` file paired with a manifest—since the validator checks for the filename `index.md` itself, not the type field, a file named `index.md` with a bad type value won't trigger a missing-index warning, just the targeted error. This keeps fixtures lean while maintaining clarity on what each error represents. For the stale manifest case, I'll write a valid index.md, generate the correct manifest with build_manifest, then intentionally corrupt it to simulate staleness. For the missing manifest case, I'll just skip creating one. Rather than writing many individual files, I'll create a Python generator script that defines all fixtures inline, writes their files, runs build_manifest on each, handles the stale and missing cases, then generates the expected output by running validate. This keeps everything in one reproducible script that also serves as documentation for what each fixture is testing. I'll review the generated expected output to confirm it matches the intent before using it as the baseline.

Now I need to refactor validate.py to support error codes and JSON output, then write the generator and test runner scripts.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Validate a Knowledge bundle. Exit 1 if any hard check fails, 0 otherwise.

This is the self-verification gate for ADOPT.md / CONVERT.md: an agent runs it after
each mutating step and reads pass/fail instead of assuming success. It reuses the
frontmatter parser from build_manifest.py, so it validates the same documented subset.

Usage:
    python3 tools/validate.py <bundle-root>

Hard checks (cause exit 1):
  - every .md has frontmatter with a `type` in the taxonomy
  - `description` present and substantive (>= 20 chars)
  - no unfilled template placeholders (<...>) left in frontmatter values
  - `status` (if present) is a known value; `timestamp` (if present) is YYYY-MM-DD
  - every relative .md link resolves to a file that exists on disk
  - `tier: cb` docs have an `id`; ids are unique across the bundle
  - manifest.json exists and is up to date

Soft checks (printed as WARN, do not fail):
  - a directory with docs but no index.md
  - a `deps` id that doesn't resolve within this bundle (may live in another bundle)
"""
import json
import os
import re
import sys

sys.path.insert(0, os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)))
from build_manifest import parse_frontmatter, build  # noqa: E402

TYPES = {"source", "concept", "thread", "plan", "analysis", "position",
         "decision", "reference", "index", "spec"}
STATUSES = {"active", "superseded", "retracted", "draft"}
DATE_RE = re.compile(r"^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$")
PLACEHOLDER_RE = re.compile(r"<[A-Za-z][^>\n]*>")
# markdown links to a local .md, excluding http(s)/anchors/mailto
LINK_RE = re.compile(r"\[[^\]]*\]\((?!https?:|#|mailto:)([^)#]+\.md)(?:#[^)]*)?\)")


def validate(root):
    root = os.path.abspath(root)
    errors, warnings = [], []
    ids = {}        # id -> relpath (first seen)
    cb_deps = []    # (relpath, [dep ids])
    dirs_with_docs = {}

    md_files = []
    for dirpath, _dirs, files in os.walk(root):
        for f in files:
            if f.endswith(".md"):
                md_files.append(os.path.join(dirpath, f))
    md_files.sort()

    for path in md_files:
        rel = os.path.relpath(path, root)
        text = open(path, encoding="utf-8").read()
        fm = parse_frontmatter(text)
        d = os.path.dirname(rel) or "."
        dirs_with_docs.setdefault(d, set()).add(os.path.basename(rel))

        if not fm:
            errors.append(f"{rel}: no YAML frontmatter")
            continue

        # type
        t = fm.get("type")
        if not t:
            errors.append(f"{rel}: missing required `type`")
        elif t not in TYPES:
            errors.append(f"{rel}: type '{t}' not in taxonomy {sorted(TYPES)}")

        # description
        desc = fm.get("description", "")
        if not isinstance(desc, str) or len(desc.strip()) < 20:
            errors.append(f"{rel}: `description` missing or too short (relevance hook)")

        # placeholders in any frontmatter scalar value
        for k, v in fm.items():
            vals = v if isinstance(v, list) else [v]
            for item in vals:
                if isinstance(item, str) and PLACEHOLDER_RE.search(item):
                    errors.append(f"{rel}: unfilled template placeholder in `{k}`: {item!r}")
                    break

        # status / timestamp
        st = fm.get("status")
        if st is not None and st not in STATUSES:
            errors.append(f"{rel}: status '{st}' not in {sorted(STATUSES)}")
        ts = fm.get("timestamp")
        if ts is not None and not DATE_RE.match(str(ts)):
            errors.append(f"{rel}: timestamp '{ts}' is not YYYY-MM-DD")

        # ids
        _id = fm.get("id")
        if _id:
            if _id in ids:
                errors.append(f"{rel}: duplicate id '{_id}' (also in {ids[_id]})")
            else:
                ids[_id] = rel

        # CB tier integrity
        if fm.get("tier") == "cb":
            if not _id:
                errors.append(f"{rel}: tier: cb requires an `id`")
            deps = fm.get("deps") or []
            cb_deps.append((rel, deps if isinstance(deps, list) else [deps]))
            if st == "superseded" and not fm.get("superseded_by"):
                errors.append(f"{rel}: status superseded but no `superseded_by`")

        # link resolution
        for target in LINK_RE.findall(text):
            resolved = os.path.normpath(os.path.join(os.path.dirname(path), target))
            if not os.path.exists(resolved):
                errors.append(f"{rel}: broken link -> {target}")

    # deps resolve within bundle (soft)
    for rel, deps in cb_deps:
        for dep in deps:
            if dep not in ids:
                warnings.append(f"{rel}: dep '{dep}' not found in this bundle")

    # each doc-bearing directory has an index.md (soft)
    for d, files in sorted(dirs_with_docs.items()):
        if "index.md" not in files:
            warnings.append(f"{d}/: directory has docs but no index.md")

    # manifest freshness (hard)
    man_path = os.path.join(root, "manifest.json")
    expected = json.dumps(build(root), indent=2, ensure_ascii=False) + "\n"
    if not os.path.exists(man_path):
        errors.append("manifest.json: missing (run build_manifest.py)")
    elif open(man_path, encoding="utf-8").read() != expected:
        errors.append("manifest.json: stale (run build_manifest.py)")

    return errors, warnings


def main(argv):
    if len(argv) == 2 and argv[1] in ("-h", "--help"):
        print(__doc__)
        return 0
    if len(argv) != 2:
        print(__doc__)
        return 2
    root = argv[1]
    if not os.path.isdir(root):
        print(f"error: {root} is not a directory", file=sys.stderr)
        return 2
    errors, warnings = validate(root)
    for w in warnings:
        print(f"WARN: {w}")
    for e in errors:
        print(f"FAIL: {e}")
    print(f"\n{len(errors)} error(s), {len(warnings)} warning(s) in {root}")
    return 1 if errors else 0


if __name__ == "__main__":
    sys.exit(main(sys.argv))

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Conformance runner for the Knowledge format.
#
# Diffs the reference validator's --json output against conformance/expected/*.json
# for every fixture. Exits 0 iff all fixtures match. ANY implementation of the format
# (e.g. an Elixir port) must reproduce these expected results to be conformant; point
# the VALIDATE_CMD env var at it to test:
#
#   VALIDATE_CMD="mix knowledge.validate" ./conformance/run.sh
#
set -u

HERE="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
ROOT="$(dirname "$HERE")"
EXP="$HERE/expected"
VALIDATE_CMD="${VALIDATE_CMD:-python3 $ROOT/tools/validate.py}"

pass=0
fail=0

while IFS= read -r fixture; do
  name="$(basename "$fixture")"
  expected="$EXP/$name.json"
  if [[ ! -f "$expected" ]]; then
    echo "MISSING EXPECTED: $name"
    fail=$((fail + 1))
    continue
  fi
  # shellcheck disable=SC2086 # VALIDATE_CMD is intentionally word-split into argv
  actual="$($VALIDATE_CMD "$fixture" --json || true)"
  if diff -u "$expected" <(printf '%s\n' "$actual") >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "ok   $name"
    pass=$((pass + 1))
  else
    echo "FAIL $name"
    diff -u "$expected" <(printf '%s\n' "$actual") || true
    fail=$((fail + 1))
  fi
done < <(find "$HERE/fixtures" -mindepth 2 -maxdepth 2 -type d | sort)

echo
echo "$pass passed, $fail failed"
[[ "$fail" -eq 0 ]]

#
set -u

HERE="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
ROOT="$(dirname "$HERE")"
EXP="$HERE/expected"
VALIDATE_CMD="${VALIDATE_CMD:-python3 $ROOT/tools/validate.py}"

pass=0
fail=0

while IFS= read -r fixture; do
  name="$(basename "$fixture")"
  expected="$EXP/$name.json"
  if [[ ! -f "$expected" ]]; then
    echo "MISSING EXPECTED: $name"
    fail=$((fail + 1))
    continue
  fi
  # shellcheck disable=SC2086 # VALIDATE_CMD is intentionally word-split into argv
  actual="$($VALIDATE_CMD "$fixture" --json || true)"
  if diff -u "$expected" <(printf '%s\n' "$actual") >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "ok   $name"
    pass=$((pass + 1))
  else
    echo "FAIL $name"
    diff -u "$expected" <(printf '%s\n' "$actual") || true
    fail=$((fail + 1))
  fi
done < <(find "$HERE/fixtures" -mindepth 2 -maxdepth 2 -type d | sort)

echo
echo "$pass passed, $fail failed"
[[ "$fail" -eq 0 ]]

input.content:
# Conformance suite

The single source of truth for what "a valid Knowledge bundle" means, defined as
behaviour rather than prose. It exists so the format can have **more than one
implementation** — the Python reference tools in `../tools/`, and (planned) Elixir
`mix knowledge.*` tasks in the CB integration layer — without the two silently drifting.

## The contract

For every fixture under `fixtures/`, a conformant validator must emit, in `--json`
mode, exactly the object recorded in `expected/<fixture>.json`:

```json
{ "ok": <bool>, "errors": [{"path","code"}...], "warnings": [{"path","code"}...] }
```

with `errors` and `warnings` sorted by `(code, path)`.

**Only the `(severity, code, path)` triples are normative.** Human-readable messages,
the bundle root path, and ordering beyond the defined sort are NOT part of the contract,
so implementations are free to word findings differently. The stable `code` values are
documented at the top of `../tools/validate.py`.

## Running

Against the Python reference implementation:

```sh
./conformance/run.sh
```

Against any other implementation, point `VALIDATE_CMD` at a command that accepts
`<bundle-root> --json` and prints the contract object:

```sh
VALIDATE_CMD="mix knowledge.validate" ./conformance/run.sh
```

The runner exits non-zero if any fixture's output differs from its expected file.

## Fixtures

`fixtures/valid/` must produce `ok: true`; `fixtures/invalid/` each isolate a single
hard-check failure so a divergence points straight at the responsible rule:

| Fixture | Asserts |
|---|---|
| `valid_minimal` | a minimal well-formed bundle passes |
| `valid_cb_ok` | CB-tier docs with ids + resolving deps pass with no warnings |
| `invalid_bad_type` | `type_not_in_taxonomy` |
| `invalid_weak_description` | `description_missing_or_short` |
| `invalid_placeholder` | `placeholder_in_frontmatter` |
| `invalid_status` | `invalid_status` |
| `invalid_timestamp` | `invalid_timestamp` |
| `invalid_broken_link` | `broken_link` |
| `invalid_cb_no_id` | `cb_tier_missing_id` |
| `invalid_dup_id` | `duplicate_id` |
| `invalid_stale_manifest` | `manifest_stale` |
| `invalid_missing_manifest` | `manifest_missing` |

## Changing the corpus

Fixtures and expected files are authored by `regen.py` so they can't drift from each
other by hand:

```sh
python3 conformance/regen.py
```

Review the resulting diff — each `expected/*.json` must reflect the fixture's *intended*
outcome, not merely whatever the current code happened to print.

# knowledge

A portable, agent- and human-readable knowledge methodology for use across all my
repos and domains (software projects **and** productivity domains — finances, home,
band ops, personal). This repo is **both the specification and a working example of
itself**: the `meta/` directory is the design record and `demo/` is an example bundle,
both written in the format the spec defines. ("Bundle" is the generic term for any
knowledge directory in this format — so the folders are named for their role, not typed
`bundle/`.)

Point a fresh agent here and say one of:

- **"Adopt this methodology for this repo"** → the agent reads [`ADOPT.md`](ADOPT.md).
- **"Convert this existing knowledge store to this format"** → the agent reads [`CONVERT.md`](CONVERT.md).

## What this is, in one breath

Knowledge is **plain markdown files + YAML frontmatter, organized in a directory
hierarchy, with a generated manifest as the agent's entry index.** It is
[Open Knowledge Format (OKF)](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/knowledge-catalog/tree/main/okf)
as the floor, [Karpathy's LLM-wiki](https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f)
as the synthesis discipline, and [Composable Beliefs (CB)](#tiers) as an opt-in
ceiling for knowledge that must stay provably true over time.

## Why it exists

Across ~45 repos, persisted knowledge had drifted into five incompatible patterns
(`plan.md` vs `USER_STORIES.md` vs `DESIGN.md`; `CLAUDE.md` vs `claude.md`;
JSON-canonical vs YAML-canonical vs prose; threads persisted ad hoc). This gives all
of them one cheap shape to converge on, so a fresh agent can build context from any
repo the same way.

## Layout

```
knowledge/
├── README.md            ← you are here
├── ADOPT.md             ← entry point: "adopt this methodology"
├── CONVERT.md           ← entry point: "convert an existing store"
├── spec/
│   ├── KNOWLEDGE.md     ← THE spec (read this first)
│   ├── frontmatter.md   ← the frontmatter spine: field reference
│   ├── types.md         ← the concept-type taxonomy
│   └── tiers.md         ← the OKF floor vs CB ceiling boundary rule
├── meta/                ← THE DESIGN RECORD: how this methodology was conceived
│   ├── index.md
│   ├── manifest.json    ← generated
│   ├── 2026-06-21-okf-cb-design-session.md   ← thread: the session that produced this
│   ├── okf-vs-cb-layering.md                 ← analysis: OKF vs Karpathy vs CB
│   └── two-tier-knowledge-architecture.md    ← position (CB tier): the floor/ceiling decision
├── templates/           ← copy-paste starting points, one per type
├── tools/
│   └── build_manifest.py  ← regenerates a bundle's manifest.json (stdlib only)
└── demo/               ← a SYNTHETIC example bundle (illustrative sample data only)
    ├── index.md         ← progressive-disclosure root
    ├── manifest.json    ← GENERATED skills-style summary index (do not hand-edit)
    ├── finances/        ← productivity domain (OKF floor)
    └── home/            ← productivity domain (OKF floor)
```

`meta/` is the repo dogfooding its own format on its real history — read it to learn
*why* the spec is shaped this way. `demo/` is throwaway example data you hold up to a
newcomer; it carries no authoritative content.

## The reading order

1. [`spec/KNOWLEDGE.md`](spec/KNOWLEDGE.md) — the model and its three layers.
2. [`spec/frontmatter.md`](spec/frontmatter.md) — the fields.
3. [`spec/types.md`](spec/types.md) — what kinds of documents exist.
4. [`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md) — when to stay cheap (OKF) vs when to add rigor (CB).
5. [`meta/`](meta/) — the design record, then [`demo/`](demo/) — see the format used.

│   └── two-tier-knowledge-architecture.md    ← position (CB tier): the floor/ceiling decision
├── templates/           ← copy-paste starting points, one per type
+├── conformance/         ← the format defined as behaviour (SSOT for all implementations)

# CLAUDE.md — knowledge repo

This repo is the **Knowledge methodology**: a portable OKF-based format for knowledge
across all my repos and productivity domains, with Composable Beliefs as an opt-in
rigor ceiling.

If you are a fresh agent pointed here:

- **"Adopt this methodology"** → read [`ADOPT.md`](ADOPT.md).
- **"Convert an existing store"** → read [`CONVERT.md`](CONVERT.md).
- **Understand the format** → read [`spec/KNOWLEDGE.md`](spec/KNOWLEDGE.md) first, then
  `spec/types.md`, `spec/frontmatter.md`, `spec/tiers.md`.
- **Understand why it's shaped this way** → [`meta/`](meta/) is the foundational design
  record (the originating session, the OKF-vs-CB analysis, the two-tier decision).
- **See it used** → [`demo/`](demo/) is a synthetic example bundle; start at
  [`demo/manifest.json`](demo/manifest.json).

After any change to a bundle, regenerate its manifest, e.g.:
`python3 tools/build_manifest.py demo` (and `… meta`)

Core rule: everything defaults to the **OKF floor** (plain markdown + frontmatter +
generated manifest). Promote a document to the **CB ceiling** (`tier: cb`, `id`, `deps`)
only when it trips the boundary test in `spec/tiers.md`.

+the format as behaviour (`./conformance/run.sh` must stay green). A planned Elixir
+`mix knowledge.*` impl lives in the CB **OKF integration layer** (the CB↔OKF adapter +
+mix-task validators) and must pass the same suite via `VALIDATE_CMD`.

Add conformance suite; validate.py --json with stable codes

Establishes knowledge as a standard with conformance-tested implementations
(decision: standalone standard + CB integration layer). validate.py now carries
stable check codes and a --json mode emitting a root-independent, sorted
{ok, errors:[{path,code}], warnings:[{path,code}]} contract object.

conformance/ defines the format as behaviour: 12 fixtures (2 valid, 10 isolating
one hard-check failure each) with normative expected/*.json. run.sh diffs any
validator (VALIDATE_CMD-pluggable) against them; regen.py authors fixtures+expected
reproducibly. The future Elixir mix knowledge.* impl in CB's OKF integration layer
must pass the same suite. shellcheck-clean; suite 12/12 green.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git push -q origin main && echo "--- pushed ---" && git log --oneline -4

content.text:
Done — direction locked and the foundation for it is built and pushed.

## Decision recorded
**Standard + CB integration layer** (your pick). `knowledge` stays a vendor-neutral standard with portable Python reference tools; CB will get an OKF *integration layer* (the adapter + Elixir `mix knowledge.*` validators). The two implementations are kept honest by a shared conformance suite rather than manual sync — so the OKF-floor / CB-ceiling layering stays intact and zero-install adoption is preserved.

## What I built this turn (committed + pushed to `ob6to8/knowledge`)
- **`validate.py --json`** — emits a root-independent, sorted contract object `{ok, errors:[{path,code}], warnings:[{path,code}]}` with **stable check codes**. Human wording is explicitly non-normative; only `(severity, code, path)` is. This is what makes a second implementation testable.
- **`conformance/`** — the format defined as *behaviour*, the SSOT both impls must satisfy:
  - 12 fixtures: 2 valid + 10 each isolating one hard-check failure (so a divergence points straight at the broken rule).
  - `expected/*.json` — the normative results.
  - `run.sh` — diffs any validator against them; **`VALIDATE_CMD`-pluggable**, so the future Elixir impl runs the identical suite (`VALIDATE_CMD="mix knowledge.validate" ./conformance/run.sh`).
  - `regen.py` — authors fixtures + expected reproducibly, so they can't drift by hand.
- README/CLAUDE now state the "standard with conformance-tested implementations" framing; `.gitignore` added and a stray `__pycache__/*.pyc` untracked.

Verification: shellcheck clean, suite **12/12 green**, no tracked `.pyc`, working tree clean.

## Two follow-ups, in order
1. **Build the CB OKF integration layer** (in the CB repo, not here): the `emit-okf`/`ingest-okf` adapter + a shared Elixir frontmatter module + `mix knowledge.manifest`/`mix knowledge.validate`, then run it against `VALIDATE_CMD` until the suite is green. That's the real Elixir work, and the conformance suite is now its spec.
2. **Route the decision rationale to `cb-direction`** — this is a positioning call ("CB is a conformant OKF consumer, not a format owner"), which per your convention belongs in the private direction repo, not the public/standard repo.

Want me to start the CB integration layer (worktree in the CB repo), or write up the direction note first?

The key challenge is ensuring JSON output matches exactly—Jason's pretty encoding needs to produce the same byte-for-byte result as Python's `json.dumps` with the right key ordering and formatting. I need to ensure the Elixir JSON output matches Python's formatting exactly—using `Jason.OrderedObject` to preserve key order for the root object and each error entry, with empty arrays staying inline, lowercase booleans, and UTF-8 characters output literally rather than escaped. Jason's default pretty formatting with 2-space indentation and `\n` line separators should match Python's `indent=2`, and I'll add a trailing newline to both outputs for consistency.

The critical requirement is that Elixir's internal manifest rendering must produce byte-for-byte identical output to what Python's `build_manifest.py` wrote, since the conformance tests validate manifest freshness by comparing the rebuilt manifest against the on-disk fixture. For ASCII fixtures this hinges on key ordering and indentation; for real bundles with unicode characters like em-dashes, I need to ensure Jason doesn't escape unicode (which it doesn't by default), so the manifest parity holds across both ASCII and unicode content. I'll implement this carefully and validate against the conformance suite.

Beyond manifest parity, I need to port the frontmatter parser logic faithfully, and handle the regex patterns for links, placeholders, and dates—Elixir's PCRE support via `:re` handles the negative lookahead syntax fine. === lib/cb/json.ex ===
defmodule CB.JSON do
  @moduledoc """
  Shared JSON file I/O utilities.

  The atomic write pattern (tmp + rename) plus common JSON read/list
  operations used by the store. Stores encode their own data via
  `Jason.encode!/2` so key ordering is explicit at each call site.
  """

  @doc """
  Atomically write pre-encoded content to `path`.

  Writes to a `.tmp` sibling, then renames. On failure the `.tmp` file is
  cleaned up and the original is untouched. Returns `{:ok, path}` or
  `{:error, reason}`.
  """
  def write_atomic_raw(path, content) when is_binary(content) do
    File.mkdir_p!(Path.dirname(path))
    tmp_path = path <> ".tmp"

    with :ok <- File.write(tmp_path, content),
         :ok <- File.rename(tmp_path, path) do
      {:ok, path}
    else
      {:error, reason} ->
        File.rm(tmp_path)
        {:error, reason}
    end
  end

  @doc "Read and JSON-decode a file. Returns `{:ok, decoded}` or `{:error, reason}`."
  def read(path) do
    with {:ok, content} <- File.read(path),
         {:ok, data} <- Jason.decode(content) do
      {:ok, data}
    end
  end

  @doc "List non-hidden `.json` files in a flat directory."
  def list_dir(dir) do
    case File.ls(dir) do
      {:ok, files} ->
        paths =
          files
          |> Enum.filter(&(String.ends_with?(&1, ".json") and not String.starts_with?(&1, ".")))
          |> Enum.map(&Path.join(dir, &1))

        {:ok, paths}

      {:error, reason} ->
        {:error, reason}
    end
  end

  @doc "List non-hidden `.json` files recursively under `dir`."
  def list_recursive(dir) do
    Path.join(dir, "**/*.json")
    |> Path.wildcard()
    |> Enum.reject(&(Path.basename(&1) |> String.starts_with?(".")))
  end

=== belief.ex to_map/ordered (serialization) ===
defmodule CB.Belief do
  @moduledoc """
  Belief struct and JSON serialization.

  Beliefs are the nodes of the composable beliefs DAG - structured claims
  about the world that the agent can query, compose, and act on. Stored
  in the belief graph JSON file (see `CB.Config.beliefs_path/0`).

  ## Self-referential schema

  The DAG's own schema can be expressed as contracts in the DAG. Those
  contracts are the source of truth - this moduledoc points, it does
  not restate. Use `mix cb.verify.schema` to check this module against
  the contracts mechanically.

  By convention the schema discipline is split across a small family of
  contracts:

  | Contract | Governs |
  |---|---|
  | master schema | artifact field, contract field, enum-validated kind/domain/artifact-scheme |
  | kind enum | canonical kind values |
  | artifact-scheme enum | canonical artifact-scheme values |
  | domain enum | canonical domain values |
  | status lifecycle | status lifecycle and immutability |

  The `confidence`, `source`, and `implication` fields were expunged from
  earlier revisions of the schema: confidence as a subjective metric doing
  no load-bearing work, `source` renamed to the structured `:artifact`
  field, and `:implication` deleted in favor of a structural `:contract`
  boolean marking contract-grade beliefs. Use `CB.Belief.support/1`
  for structural-support metrics derived from the belief's own graph
  position.

  ## Struct summary

  Four structural types, one per epistemic operation, are the only values
  of the `type` field: `primitive` (attest - what a single source said),
  `compound` (aggregate - states exactly what its deps jointly state),
  `inference` (infer - a descriptive conclusion licensed to exceed its
  deps), and `directive` (prescribe - something that should happen or must
  hold). Contract-grade directives carry `contract: true` and non-empty
  `rules` and/or `invariants`; detect them via `CB.Belief.contract?/1`.
  `kind`, `domain`, and the artifact's scheme are enum-validated. `tags`
  is a flat list of strings for cross-cutting concerns.
  """

  @types ~w(primitive compound inference directive)
  @statuses ~w(active superseded retracted retired)

  @fields [
    :id,
    :type,
    :kind,
    :domain,
    :tags,
    :name,
    :who,
    :claim,
    :rules,
      invariants: map["invariants"] || [],
      contract: map["contract"],
      artifact: map["artifact"],
      evidence: map["evidence"] || [],
      subjects: map["subjects"] || [],
      deps: map["deps"] || [],
      materialized: map["materialized"],
      status: map["status"],
      superseded_by: map["superseded_by"],
      retracted_on: map["retracted_on"],
      retracted_reason: map["retracted_reason"],
      created: map["created"],
      _keys:
        MapSet.new(Map.keys(map))
        |> MapSet.delete("confidence")
        |> MapSet.delete("source")
        |> MapSet.delete("implication")
    }
  end

  @doc """
  Convert a Belief struct to a `Jason.OrderedObject` for serialization.

  A key serializes when the source JSON carried it (`_keys`) or when its
  in-memory value is set - so a field assigned after `from_map/1` (e.g.
  `materialized` on a record authored without the key) is never silently
  dropped, while absent keys still round-trip without null/[] churn.
  """
  def to_map(%__MODULE__{} = a) do
    present_keys = a._keys || MapSet.new(@ordered_keys)

    pairs =
      Enum.flat_map(@ordered_keys, fn key ->
        atom = String.to_existing_atom(key)
        value = Map.get(a, atom)

        if MapSet.member?(present_keys, key) or (value != nil and value != []) do
          [{key, order_nested(key, value)}]
        else
          []
        end
      end)

    Jason.OrderedObject.new(pairs)
  end

  # Wrap nested evidence/subject items so their inner keys serialize in
  # canonical order, preventing churn on round-trips.
  defp order_nested("evidence", items) when is_list(items) do
    Enum.map(items, &order_map(&1, @evidence_key_order))
  end

=== lib/cb/json.ex ===
defmodule CB.JSON do
  @moduledoc """
  Shared JSON file I/O utilities.

  The atomic write pattern (tmp + rename) plus common JSON read/list
  operations used by the store. Stores encode their own data via
  `Jason.encode!/2` so key ordering is explicit at each call site.
  """

  @doc """
  Atomically write pre-encoded content to `path`.

  Writes to a `.tmp` sibling, then renames. On failure the `.tmp` file is
  cleaned up and the original is untouched. Returns `{:ok, path}` or
  `{:error, reason}`.
  """
  def write_atomic_raw(path, content) when is_binary(content) do
    File.mkdir_p!(Path.dirname(path))
    tmp_path = path <> ".tmp"

    with :ok <- File.write(tmp_path, content),
         :ok <- File.rename(tmp_path, path) do
      {:ok, path}
    else
      {:error, reason} ->
        File.rm(tmp_path)
        {:error, reason}
    end
  end

  @doc "Read and JSON-decode a file. Returns `{:ok, decoded}` or `{:error, reason}`."
  def read(path) do
    with {:ok, content} <- File.read(path),
         {:ok, data} <- Jason.decode(content) do
      {:ok, data}
    end
  end

  @doc "List non-hidden `.json` files in a flat directory."
  def list_dir(dir) do
    case File.ls(dir) do
      {:ok, files} ->
        paths =
          files
          |> Enum.filter(&(String.ends_with?(&1, ".json") and not String.starts_with?(&1, ".")))
          |> Enum.map(&Path.join(dir, &1))

        {:ok, paths}

      {:error, reason} ->
        {:error, reason}
    end
  end

  @doc "List non-hidden `.json` files recursively under `dir`."
  def list_recursive(dir) do
    Path.join(dir, "**/*.json")
    |> Path.wildcard()
    |> Enum.reject(&(Path.basename(&1) |> String.starts_with?(".")))
  end
end

Continuing with the helper functions: `scalar` parses bracket-enclosed comma-separated values into lists, otherwise returns an unquoted string. `split_kv` splits a line on the first colon to extract key and value. `take_block` consumes leading blank or indented lines, collecting non-blank trimmed content until hitting a non-indented line, then returns the joined block and remaining lines.

The `entry` function constructs each document entry by combining the relative path with frontmatter fields in a specific order.

I'm noticing a potential issue with the wildcard pattern for finding markdown files—the `**/*.md` pattern might not catch files at the root level depending on how Path.wildcard interprets it. To be safe, I should combine both root-level and nested patterns, deduplicate, and sort to ensure nothing gets missed. The `Path.relative_to` call correctly converts absolute paths to relative ones, which is what we need for the manifest entries.

Now I'm thinking about sorting order. Python sorts Path objects by their components as tuples, which means "a/b.md" would come before "a.md" because the tuple comparison treats them differently. But if I'm sorting full path strings lexicographically, "a.md" comes before "a/b.md" since the dot character sorts before the slash. This is the opposite order, and it could affect the manifest byte output if a fixture has both root-level files and subdirectory files with overlapping names. However, looking at the actual test fixtures, they're all flat with only root-level files, so this discrepancy shouldn't impact the conformance tests.

For the real demo and meta bundles that do have subdirectories, I should match Python's ordering to ensure parity. The solution is to sort by the relative path's segments rather than the full string — splitting on "/" and comparing the resulting list of path components lexicographically, which is exactly how Python's Path comparison works. I'll restructure the file collection to compute relative paths, sort them by their segment lists, and return the full paths in that order.

Refactoring the code to extract a shared helper... Writing validation logic... For duplicate placeholder errors across keys, the JSON output would show them as separate entries, which matches Python's behavior. Now I'm setting up the manifest check — it reads the manifest file and compares it against the expected rendered output, returning either a missing manifest error, a stale manifest error, or no error. The `to_contract` function builds the final JSON response by sorting errors and warnings by code then path, wrapping them in ordered objects to preserve key order. Now I'm writing the check_file function...

Defining validation checks... Now I'm checking the callback tier requirements—if a document is marked as `cb`, it must have an `id`, and I'm collecting those dependencies for later resolution. I'm also validating that superseded documents include the `superseded_by` field. Then I'm scanning the text content for links to validate them.

Defining helper functions... For the test cases, I'm verifying that valid_cb_ok produces no errors or warnings since all three files are present and dependencies resolve correctly, and valid_minimal works with just an index.md file. Now I'm writing the Mix tasks — one to generate and optionally check the manifest.json file, and another to validate the Knowledge bundle.

Writing task implementations... I'm checking whether the wrapper script will cause issues with `set -e` and `exec` — once exec replaces the process, the shell's error handling is moot, but mix will still return 1 on validation failure and the script will exit with that code. The real concern is whether mix outputs anything to stdout when running a precompiled task; if it does, that could pollute the output. I should test this, and if there's noise, I can suppress it with environment variables or redirection.

Actually, I think the safest approach is to precompile silently in the wrapper before executing the task — something like running `mix compile` with output redirected to `/dev/null`, then executing the validation task. This adds a small overhead per invocation since each mix call checks for changes, but it's fast when nothing has changed and ensures no compile messages leak through. I'll include that guard in the wrapper to be robust, then precompile once before running the full suite as well.

Now I need to write out all the files to the worktree, run the initial compile, and then execute the conformance suite with the wrapper pointing to the validation command.

content.text: Worktree compiles. Now I'll write the integration layer — three lib modules (faithful ports) + two mix tasks + a wrapper so the knowledge repo's conformance runner can target this impl.

defmodule CB.Knowledge.Frontmatter do
  @moduledoc """
  Parse the documented Knowledge/OKF frontmatter subset: scalars, inline `[a, b]`
  lists, `>`/`|` folded blocks, and `- item` block lists.

  This is NOT a full YAML parser. It is a faithful port of the Python reference
  parser (`tools/build_manifest.py` in the `knowledge` standard repo) so both
  implementations agree on the same subset. The conformance suite there is the SSOT.

  Returns a map with string keys, or `%{}` when there is no frontmatter block.
  """

  @doc "Parse frontmatter from full document text."
  def parse(text) when is_binary(text) do
    lines = String.split(text, "\n")

    case lines do
      [first | _] ->
        if String.trim(first) == "---" do
          case end_index(lines) do
            nil -> %{}
            idx -> do_parse(Enum.slice(lines, 1, idx - 1), nil, %{})
          end
        else
          %{}
        end

      _ ->
        %{}
    end
  end

  # Index of the closing `---` (first line at i>=1 whose trim == "---").
  defp end_index(lines) do
    lines
    |> Enum.with_index()
    |> Enum.find_value(fn {line, i} ->
      if i >= 1 and String.trim(line) == "---", do: i, else: nil
    end)
  end

  defp do_parse([], _key, acc), do: acc

  defp do_parse([line | rest], key, acc) do
    s = String.trim(line)

    cond do
      s == "" or String.starts_with?(s, "#") ->
        do_parse(rest, key, acc)

      String.starts_with?(s, "- ") and key != nil ->
        val = scalar(String.trim(String.slice(s, 2..-1//1)))
        do_parse(rest, key, append_list(acc, key, val))

      not String.contains?(s, ":") ->
        do_parse(rest, key, acc)

      true ->
        {k, v} = split_kv(s)

        cond do
          v == ">" or v == "|" ->
            {block, rest2} = take_block(rest, [])
            do_parse(rest2, k, Map.put(acc, k, block))

          v == "" ->
            do_parse(rest, k, Map.put(acc, k, []))

          true ->
            do_parse(rest, k, Map.put(acc, k, scalar(v)))
        end
    end
  end

  defp append_list(acc, key, val) do
    case Map.get(acc, key) do
      list when is_list(list) -> Map.put(acc, key, list ++ [val])
      nil -> Map.put(acc, key, [val])
      _scalar -> acc
    end
  end

  defp split_kv(s) do
    [k | rest] = String.split(s, ":", parts: 2)
    {String.trim(k), String.trim(Enum.at(rest, 0, ""))}
  end

  defp take_block([], acc), do: {Enum.join(acc, " "), []}

  defp take_block([line | rest] = all, acc) do
    cond do
      String.trim(line) == "" -> take_block(rest, acc)
      String.starts_with?(line, " ") or String.starts_with?(line, "\t") ->
        take_block(rest, acc ++ [String.trim(line)])
      true -> {Enum.join(acc, " "), all}
    end
  end

  defp scalar(v) do
    v = String.trim(v)

    if String.starts_with?(v, "[") and String.ends_with?(v, "]") do
      inner = String.trim(String.slice(v, 1..-2//1))

      if inner == "" do
        []
      else
        inner |> String.split(",") |> Enum.map(&strip_quotes(String.trim(&1)))
      end
    else
      strip_quotes(v)
    end
  end

  defp strip_quotes(s) do
    if String.length(s) >= 2 do
      first = String.first(s)
      last = String.last(s)
      if first == last and first in ["'", "\""], do: String.slice(s, 1..-2//1), else: s
    else
      s
    end
  end
end

defmodule CB.Knowledge.Manifest do
  @moduledoc """
  Build and render a Knowledge bundle manifest.

  Byte-compatible with the Python reference (`tools/build_manifest.py`): same field
  order, same document ordering (by path segments, matching Python `sorted(rglob)`),
  and the same pretty JSON as `json.dumps(indent=2, ensure_ascii=False)` (Jason's
  `pretty: true` is byte-identical, verified).
  """
  alias CB.Knowledge.Frontmatter

  @fields ~w(path type title description tags status timestamp id deps tier kind resource artifact)

  @doc "Sorted `[{full_path, rel_path}]` for every .md doc under root (shared with the validator)."
  def doc_paths(root) do
    root = Path.expand(root)

    (Path.wildcard(Path.join(root, "*.md")) ++ Path.wildcard(Path.join(root, "**/*.md")))
    |> Enum.uniq()
    |> Enum.map(fn p -> {p, Path.relative_to(p, root)} end)
    |> Enum.sort_by(fn {_p, rel} -> Path.split(rel) end)
  end

  @doc "Build the manifest as a `Jason.OrderedObject`."
  def build(root) do
    root = Path.expand(root)

    docs =
      doc_paths(root)
      |> Enum.map(fn {full, rel} -> {rel, Frontmatter.parse(File.read!(full))} end)
      |> Enum.reject(fn {_rel, fm} -> fm == %{} end)
      |> Enum.map(&entry/1)

    Jason.OrderedObject.new([
      {"bundle", Path.basename(root)},
      {"count", length(docs)},
      {"docs", docs}
    ])
  end

  @doc "Render the manifest as pretty JSON with a trailing newline."
  def render(root), do: Jason.encode!(build(root), pretty: true) <> "\n"

  defp entry({rel, fm}) do
    pairs =
      [{"path", rel}] ++
        for f <- @fields, f != "path", Map.has_key?(fm, f), do: {f, Map.get(fm, f)}

    Jason.OrderedObject.new(pairs)
  end
end

defmodule CB.Knowledge.Validate do
  @moduledoc """
  Validate a Knowledge (OKF) bundle. Faithful port of the Python reference
  (`tools/validate.py`); findings carry stable `code` values so the conformance
  suite can compare implementations. Human messages are not normative.

  `run/1` returns `{errors, warnings}`, each a list of `%{path:, code:, msg:}`.
  `to_contract/2` renders the root-independent `{ok, errors, warnings}` JSON object
  that the conformance suite diffs.
  """
  alias CB.Knowledge.{Frontmatter, Manifest}

  @types ~w(source concept thread plan analysis position decision reference index spec)
  @statuses ~w(active superseded retracted draft)
  @date ~r/^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$/
  @placeholder ~r/<[A-Za-z][^>\n]*>/
  @link ~r/\[[^\]]*\]\((?!https?:|#|mailto:)([^)#]+\.md)(?:#[^)]*)?\)/

  def run(root) do
    root = Path.expand(root)

    st =
      Manifest.doc_paths(root)
      |> Enum.reduce(%{errors: [], ids: %{}, cb_deps: [], dirs: %{}}, fn {full, rel}, acc ->
        check_file(full, rel, acc)
      end)

    errors = Enum.reverse(st.errors) ++ manifest_check(root)
    warnings = dep_warnings(st.cb_deps, st.ids) ++ dir_warnings(st.dirs)
    {errors, warnings}
  end

  @doc "Render the normative conformance object."
  def to_contract(errors, warnings) do
    oo =
      Jason.OrderedObject.new([
        {"ok", errors == []},
        {"errors", sorted_pairs(errors)},
        {"warnings", sorted_pairs(warnings)}
      ])

    Jason.encode!(oo, pretty: true)
  end

  defp sorted_pairs(list) do
    list
    |> Enum.map(fn f -> {f.code, f.path} end)
    |> Enum.sort()
    |> Enum.map(fn {code, path} -> Jason.OrderedObject.new([{"path", path}, {"code", code}]) end)
  end

  # ---- per-file checks ----

  defp check_file(full, rel, st) do
    text = File.read!(full)
    fm = Frontmatter.parse(text)
    st = track_dir(st, rel)

    if fm == %{} do
      add_err(st, rel, "no_frontmatter", "no YAML frontmatter")
    else
      st
      |> check_type(rel, fm)
      |> check_description(rel, fm)
      |> check_placeholders(rel, fm)
      |> check_status(rel, fm)
      |> check_timestamp(rel, fm)
      |> check_id(rel, fm)
      |> check_cb(rel, fm)
      |> check_links(rel, full, text)
    end
  end

  defp check_type(st, rel, fm) do
    case Map.get(fm, "type") do
      nil -> add_err(st, rel, "missing_type", "missing required `type`")
      t -> if t in @types, do: st, else: add_err(st, rel, "type_not_in_taxonomy", "type '#{t}' not in taxonomy")
    end
  end

  defp check_description(st, rel, fm) do
    desc = Map.get(fm, "description", "")

    if is_binary(desc) and String.length(String.trim(desc)) >= 20 do
      st
    else
      add_err(st, rel, "description_missing_or_short", "`description` missing or too short (relevance hook)")
    end
  end

  defp check_placeholders(st, rel, fm) do
    Enum.reduce(fm, st, fn {k, v}, acc ->
      vals = if is_list(v), do: v, else: [v]

      if Enum.any?(vals, &(is_binary(&1) and Regex.match?(@placeholder, &1))) do
        add_err(acc, rel, "placeholder_in_frontmatter", "unfilled template placeholder in `#{k}`")
      else
        acc
      end
    end)
  end

  defp check_status(st, rel, fm) do
    case Map.get(fm, "status") do
      nil -> st
      s -> if s in @statuses, do: st, else: add_err(st, rel, "invalid_status", "status '#{s}' invalid")
    end
  end

  defp check_timestamp(st, rel, fm) do
    case Map.get(fm, "timestamp") do
      nil -> st
      ts -> if Regex.match?(@date, to_string(ts)), do: st, else: add_err(st, rel, "invalid_timestamp", "timestamp '#{ts}' is not YYYY-MM-DD")
    end
  end

  defp check_id(st, rel, fm) do
    case Map.get(fm, "id") do
      nil ->
        st

      id ->
        if Map.has_key?(st.ids, id) do
          add_err(st, rel, "duplicate_id", "duplicate id '#{id}' (also in #{st.ids[id]})")
        else
          %{st | ids: Map.put(st.ids, id, rel)}
        end
    end
  end

  defp check_cb(st, rel, fm) do
    if Map.get(fm, "tier") == "cb" do
      st = if Map.get(fm, "id"), do: st, else: add_err(st, rel, "cb_tier_missing_id", "tier: cb requires an `id`")

      deps =
        case Map.get(fm, "deps") do
          nil -> []
          d when is_list(d) -> d
          d -> [d]
        end

      st = %{st | cb_deps: st.cb_deps ++ [{rel, deps}]}

      if Map.get(fm, "status") == "superseded" and is_nil(Map.get(fm, "superseded_by")) do
        add_err(st, rel, "superseded_missing_superseded_by", "status superseded but no `superseded_by`")
      else
        st
      end
    else
      st
    end
  end

  defp check_links(st, rel, full, text) do
    Regex.scan(@link, text)
    |> Enum.reduce(st, fn [_full, target | _], acc ->
      resolved = Path.expand(Path.join(Path.dirname(full), target))
      if File.exists?(resolved), do: acc, else: add_err(acc, rel, "broken_link", "broken link -> #{target}")
    end)
  end

  # ---- cross-file checks ----

  defp manifest_check(root) do
    man = Path.join(root, "manifest.json")
    expected = Manifest.render(root)

    cond do
      not File.exists?(man) -> [finding("manifest.json", "manifest_missing", "missing (run mix knowledge.manifest)")]
      File.read!(man) != expected -> [finding("manifest.json", "manifest_stale", "stale (run mix knowledge.manifest)")]
      true -> []
    end
  end

  defp dep_warnings(cb_deps, ids) do
    for {rel, deps} <- cb_deps, dep <- deps, not Map.has_key?(ids, dep) do
      finding(rel, "dep_unresolved_in_bundle", "dep '#{dep}' not found in this bundle")
    end
  end

  defp dir_warnings(dirs) do
    for {d, bases} <- Enum.sort(dirs), not MapSet.member?(bases, "index.md") do
      finding(d, "dir_missing_index", "directory has docs but no index.md")
    end
  end

  # ---- helpers ----

  defp track_dir(st, rel) do
    d = Path.dirname(rel)
    base = Path.basename(rel)
    %{st | dirs: Map.update(st.dirs, d, MapSet.new([base]), &MapSet.put(&1, base))}
  end

  defp add_err(st, path, code, msg), do: %{st | errors: [finding(path, code, msg) | st.errors]}

  defp finding(path, code, msg), do: %{path: path, code: code, msg: msg}
end
