  end

  defp check_type(st, rel, fm) do
    case Map.get(fm, "type") do
      nil -> add_err(st, rel, "missing_type", "missing required `type`")
      t -> if t in @types, do: st, else: add_err(st, rel, "type_not_in_taxonomy", "type '#{t}' not in taxonomy")
    end
  end

  defp check_description(st, rel, fm) do
    desc = Map.get(fm, "description", "")

    if is_binary(desc) and String.length(String.trim(desc)) >= 20 do
      st
    else
      add_err(st, rel, "description_missing_or_short", "`description` missing or too short (relevance hook)")
    end
  end

  defp check_placeholders(st, rel, fm) do
    Enum.reduce(fm, st, fn {k, v}, acc ->
      vals = if is_list(v), do: v, else: [v]

      if Enum.any?(vals, &(is_binary(&1) and Regex.match?(@placeholder, &1))) do
        add_err(acc, rel, "placeholder_in_frontmatter", "unfilled template placeholder in `#{k}`")
      else
        acc
      end
    end)
  end

  defp check_status(st, rel, fm) do
    case Map.get(fm, "status") do
      nil -> st
      s -> if s in @statuses, do: st, else: add_err(st, rel, "invalid_status", "status '#{s}' invalid")
    end
  end

  defp check_timestamp(st, rel, fm) do
    case Map.get(fm, "timestamp") do
      nil -> st
      ts -> if Regex.match?(@date, to_string(ts)), do: st, else: add_err(st, rel, "invalid_timestamp", "timestamp '#{ts}' is not YYYY-MM-DD")
    end
  end

  defp check_id(st, rel, fm) do
    case Map.get(fm, "id") do
      nil ->
        st

      id ->
        if Map.has_key?(st.ids, id) do
          add_err(st, rel, "duplicate_id", "duplicate id '#{id}' (also in #{st.ids[id]})")
        else
          %{st | ids: Map.put(st.ids, id, rel)}
        end
    end
  end

  defp check_cb(st, rel, fm) do
    if Map.get(fm, "tier") == "cb" do
      st = if Map.get(fm, "id"), do: st, else: add_err(st, rel, "cb_tier_missing_id", "tier: cb requires an `id`")

      deps =
        case Map.get(fm, "deps") do
          nil -> []
          d when is_list(d) -> d
          d -> [d]
        end

      st = %{st | cb_deps: st.cb_deps ++ [{rel, deps}]}

      if Map.get(fm, "status") == "superseded" and is_nil(Map.get(fm, "superseded_by")) do
        add_err(st, rel, "superseded_missing_superseded_by", "status superseded but no `superseded_by`")
      else
        st
      end
    else
      st
    end
  end

  defp check_links(st, rel, full, text) do
    Regex.scan(@link, text)
    |> Enum.reduce(st, fn [_full, target | _], acc ->
      resolved = Path.expand(Path.join(Path.dirname(full), target))
      if File.exists?(resolved), do: acc, else: add_err(acc, rel, "broken_link", "broken link -> #{target}")
    end)
  end

  # ---- cross-file checks ----

  defp manifest_check(root) do
    man = Path.join(root, "manifest.json")
    expected = Manifest.render(root)

    cond do
      not File.exists?(man) -> [finding("manifest.json", "manifest_missing", "missing (run mix knowledge.manifest)")]
      File.read!(man) != expected -> [finding("manifest.json", "manifest_stale", "stale (run mix knowledge.manifest)")]
      true -> []
    end
  end

  defp dep_warnings(cb_deps, ids) do
    for {rel, deps} <- cb_deps, dep <- deps, not Map.has_key?(ids, dep) do
      finding(rel, "dep_unresolved_in_bundle", "dep '#{dep}' not found in this bundle")
    end
  end

  defp dir_warnings(dirs) do
    for {d, bases} <- Enum.sort(dirs), not MapSet.member?(bases, "index.md") do
      finding(d, "dir_missing_index", "directory has docs but no index.md")
    end
  end

  # ---- helpers ----

  defp track_dir(st, rel) do
    d = Path.dirname(rel)
    base = Path.basename(rel)
    %{st | dirs: Map.update(st.dirs, d, MapSet.new([base]), &MapSet.put(&1, base))}
  end

  defp add_err(st, path, code, msg), do: %{st | errors: [finding(path, code, msg) | st.errors]}

  defp finding(path, code, msg), do: %{path: path, code: code, msg: msg}
end

defmodule Mix.Tasks.Knowledge.Manifest do
  @shortdoc "Generate manifest.json for a Knowledge (OKF) bundle"
  @moduledoc """
  #{@shortdoc}

      mix knowledge.manifest <bundle-root>          # write <root>/manifest.json
      mix knowledge.manifest <bundle-root> --check  # exit 1 if the manifest is stale

  Part of CB's OKF integration layer. Byte-compatible with the Python reference
  tool in the `knowledge` standard repo.
  """
  use Mix.Task

  @impl Mix.Task
  def run(argv) do
    {opts, rest, _} = OptionParser.parse(argv, switches: [check: :boolean])
    root = List.first(rest) || Mix.raise("usage: mix knowledge.manifest <bundle-root> [--check]")
    content = CB.Knowledge.Manifest.render(root)
    out = Path.join(root, "manifest.json")

    if opts[:check] do
      current = if File.exists?(out), do: File.read!(out), else: ""

      if current == content do
        IO.puts("ok: #{out} up to date")
      else
        IO.puts(:stderr, "stale: #{out} (run mix knowledge.manifest #{root})")
        System.halt(1)
      end
    else
      CB.JSON.write_atomic_raw(out, content)
      IO.puts("wrote #{out}")
    end
  end
end

defmodule Mix.Tasks.Knowledge.Validate do
  @shortdoc "Validate a Knowledge (OKF) bundle (--json for machine output)"
  @moduledoc """
  #{@shortdoc}

      mix knowledge.validate <bundle-root>          # human output
      mix knowledge.validate <bundle-root> --json   # stable conformance object

  Part of CB's OKF integration layer. Conformant with the `knowledge` standard's
  conformance suite: run it there with
  `VALIDATE_CMD="<cb>/bin/knowledge-validate" ./conformance/run.sh`.
  Exits 1 when any hard check fails.
  """
  use Mix.Task

  @impl Mix.Task
  def run(argv) do
    {opts, rest, _} = OptionParser.parse(argv, switches: [json: :boolean])
    root = List.first(rest) || Mix.raise("usage: mix knowledge.validate <bundle-root> [--json]")
    {errors, warnings} = CB.Knowledge.Validate.run(root)

    if opts[:json] do
      IO.puts(CB.Knowledge.Validate.to_contract(errors, warnings))
    else
      Enum.each(warnings, fn w -> IO.puts("WARN: #{w.path}: #{w.msg}") end)
      Enum.each(errors, fn e -> IO.puts("FAIL: #{e.path}: #{e.msg}") end)
      IO.puts("\n#{length(errors)} error(s), #{length(warnings)} warning(s) in #{root}")
    end

    if errors != [], do: System.halt(1)
  end
end

input.content:
defmodule CB.KnowledgeTest do
  use ExUnit.Case, async: true

  alias CB.Knowledge.{Frontmatter, Manifest, Validate}

  setup do
    root = Path.join(System.tmp_dir!(), "kn_#{System.unique_integer([:positive])}")
    File.mkdir_p!(root)
    on_exit(fn -> File.rm_rf(root) end)
    {:ok, root: root}
  end

  defp write(root, rel, contents) do
    path = Path.join(root, rel)
    File.mkdir_p!(Path.dirname(path))
    File.write!(path, contents)
  end

  defp doc(fields, body \\ "Body.\n") do
    fm = Enum.map_join(fields, "\n", fn {k, v} -> "#{k}: #{v}" end)
    "---\n#{fm}\n---\n\n#{body}"
  end

  test "frontmatter parses the documented subset" do
    fm =
      Frontmatter.parse("""
      ---
      type: concept
      title: T
      description: >
        folded line one
        line two
      tags: [a, b]
      deps:
        - x:1
        - x:2
      ---
      body
      """)

    assert fm["type"] == "concept"
    assert fm["description"] == "folded line one line two"
    assert fm["tags"] == ["a", "b"]
    assert fm["deps"] == ["x:1", "x:2"]
  end

  test "no frontmatter yields empty map" do
    assert Frontmatter.parse("# just markdown\n") == %{}
  end

  test "valid bundle passes with fresh manifest", %{root: root} do
    write(root, "index.md", doc(type: "index", title: "Idx",
      description: "Use when orienting to this valid test bundle root index."))

    File.write!(Path.join(root, "manifest.json"), Manifest.render(root))
    {errors, warnings} = Validate.run(root)
    assert errors == []
    assert warnings == []
  end

  test "flags bad type, short description, stale manifest", %{root: root} do
    write(root, "index.md", doc(type: "gizmo", title: "Bad", description: "short"))
    File.write!(Path.join(root, "manifest.json"), "{}\n")

    {errors, _warnings} = Validate.run(root)
    codes = MapSet.new(errors, & &1.code)
    assert "type_not_in_taxonomy" in codes
    assert "description_missing_or_short" in codes
    assert "manifest_stale" in codes
  end

  test "cb-tier doc without id is flagged; resolving deps pass", %{root: root} do
    write(root, "index.md", doc(type: "index", title: "Idx",
      description: "Use when orienting to the CB-tier test bundle index page."))
    write(root, "base.md", doc(type: "concept", title: "Base",
      description: "Use when you need the base belief the derived one depends upon.",
      tier: "cb", id: "t:base"))
    write(root, "derived.md", doc(type: "concept", title: "Derived",
      description: "Use when you need the derived belief composed from the base one.",
      tier: "cb", id: "t:derived", deps: "[t:base]"))

    File.write!(Path.join(root, "manifest.json"), Manifest.render(root))
    {errors, warnings} = Validate.run(root)
    assert errors == []
    assert warnings == []

    # now break the id on base
    write(root, "base.md", doc(type: "concept", title: "Base",
      description: "Use when you need the base belief the derived one depends upon.",
      tier: "cb"))
    File.write!(Path.join(root, "manifest.json"), Manifest.render(root))
    {errors, warnings} = Validate.run(root)
    assert Enum.any?(errors, &(&1.code == "cb_tier_missing_id"))
    assert Enum.any?(warnings, &(&1.code == "dep_unresolved_in_bundle"))
  end

  test "to_contract is deterministic and sorted", %{root: root} do
    write(root, "index.md", doc(type: "gizmo", title: "Bad", description: "short"))
    File.write!(Path.join(root, "manifest.json"), Manifest.render(root))
    {errors, warnings} = Validate.run(root)
    json = Validate.to_contract(errors, warnings)
    decoded = Jason.decode!(json)

    assert decoded["ok"] == false
    codes = Enum.map(decoded["errors"], & &1["code"])
    assert codes == Enum.sort(codes)
  end
end

content.text:
Done — the CB OKF integration layer's tooling half is built, conformance-passing, and committed.

## What's in place (branch `okf-integration`, worktree at `amieval/cb-okf`)
CB is now a **conformant consumer** of the knowledge standard:

| Module / task | Role |
|---|---|
| `CB.Knowledge.Frontmatter` | the documented frontmatter-subset parser (faithful port) |
| `CB.Knowledge.Manifest` | `build`/`render` — byte-identical `manifest.json` |
| `CB.Knowledge.Validate` | stable check codes + `--json` conformance object |
| `mix knowledge.manifest` / `mix knowledge.validate` | the Elixir CLI, `System.halt` exit codes |
| `bin/knowledge-validate` | adapter for the standard's conformance runner |

**Proof it's truly conformant, not just plausible:**
- All **12 conformance fixtures pass** via `VALIDATE_CMD=".../cb-okf/bin/knowledge-validate" ./conformance/run.sh` — byte-identical to the Python reference.
- `mix knowledge.manifest --check` matches the **Python-generated** `meta/` (contains unicode `—`/`↔`) and `demo/` manifests byte-for-byte — so the two implementations agree on the freshness check, the hardest parity bar.
- 6 ExUnit tests green (`mix test test/cb/knowledge_test.exs`), so CB's own CI covers it.

The Jason-vs-Python byte-parity I probed up front held across the board, so no fragile post-processing was needed.

## Not done / decisions for you
1. **The adapter (`emit-okf` / `ingest-okf`)** — the actual CB↔OKF *bridge* (beliefs → typed OKF docs; OKF docs → CB primitives) is the bigger remaining half and depends on CB's belief schema. This turn delivered the shared tooling it'll reuse, not the bridge itself.
2. **Branch is committed but not pushed or merged.** It's in a worktree (`amieval/cb-okf`, deps symlinked, not committed). Your `/end` or `/merge-worktrees` can fold it into `main`, or I can push the branch.
3. **`cb-direction` note** still owed — the "CB is a conformant OKF consumer, not a format owner" positioning.
4. Minor: the `knowledge` repo's README/CLAUDE still say the Elixir impl is "planned" — now it exists and passes; worth a one-line update.

Want me to build the **emit/ingest adapter** next, or wrap up housekeeping first (push/merge the branch, write the direction note, update the knowledge docs)?

content.thinking:
I'm trying to figure out what "commit" means here—the integration layer was already committed when it merged, so there's nothing of mine to commit. The only pending changes are the user's own work in progress files, which I shouldn't touch.

Looking at the sequence the user outlined, I think they're listing the steps for this turn: build the emit/ingest adapter, push it, then handle the remaining tasks. The "commit" might just be a confirmation that the current state is saved, or they may have assumed there was uncommitted work from my integration layer. Either way, I should move forward with building the adapter. I should check the git status of composable-beliefs to confirm there's nothing uncommitted on my end before moving forward. Then I'm diving into the big piece: building the emit/ingest adapter. The emit side needs to transform the CB belief graph into OKF bundles—each belief becomes a typed OKF document. I'm working through the mapping between CB types (primitive, compound, inference, directive) and the knowledge taxonomy, and it looks like the cleanest approach is to emit every belief as a concept doc with tier:cb, capturing the belief's metadata in the frontmatter fields like kind, id, deps, artifact, and status.

The real success criterion is making sure the emitted bundles pass the knowledge validator—that's concrete and testable. So I need to ensure each doc has a valid type from the taxonomy, a meaningful description (at least 20 characters), valid status and timestamp, unique ids (which belief ids already are), resolvable deps within the bundle, and a fresh manifest.

For the description field, I'll use the belief's claim directly since it's usually a full sentence that serves as a relevance hook. Status mapping is trickier: CB has "retired" for contracts, but the knowledge spec only recognizes active/superseded/retracted/draft, so I'll map retired→retracted to keep emitted bundles valid, even though it loses some semantic nuance.

On type mapping, I'm thinking of a reasonable translation from CB's structural types to OKF types: primitive beliefs become references, compound beliefs become concepts, inferences become analysis, and directives become positions. To preserve the original CB type information for round-tripping, I'll store it in a dedicated `cb_type` frontmatter field while using the mapped type for the OKF display type.

For this iteration, I'm scoping the adapter to produce a valid OKF bundle from a beliefs.json file—each active belief becomes a concept-family document with the necessary metadata, dependencies as cross-links, a generated index.md, and a manifest.json that all validates cleanly.

On the ingest side, I'm reading OKF bundles back into CB as primitive beliefs, treating each document as an artifact with the description becoming the claim. This is lossy by design since we're not reconstructing the original type hierarchy or dependencies. I'll build both emit and ingest with focused tests, creating Mix tasks for each. Let me pull the actual belief structure from the codebase to map fields correctly—I need to check the Store to load beliefs and understand the full set of fields I'm working with.

I'm going to time-box this: read the relevant API files (belief.ex, config.ex, store.ex), build the emit side (beliefs to OKF docs with index and manifest), build ingest (OKF docs back to primitive beliefs as JSON), add Mix tasks and tests to validate round-trip integrity, then commit and push. Since the user wants this on main and I need to avoid committing their uncommitted docs, I'll use targeted git adds for only my new files.

content.content:
Exit code 1
=== store.ex ===
defmodule CB.Belief.Store do
  @moduledoc """
  Reads and writes the belief graph JSON file. Atomic writes via tmp +
  rename. All entries are beliefs - contract-grade beliefs are
  distinguished by having `rules` and/or `invariants` fields.
  """

  alias CB.Belief
  alias CB.Config
  alias CB.JSON

  @doc "Read all entries from the belief graph as beliefs."
  def read do
    path = Config.beliefs_path()

    if File.exists?(path) do
      with {:ok, data} <- JSON.read(path) do
        {:ok, Enum.map(data, &Belief.from_map/1)}
      end
    else
      {:ok, []}
    end
  end

  @doc "Alias for read/0. Reads all entries."
  def read_all, do: read()

  @doc "Find a belief by ID or name."
  def find(id_or_name) do
    with {:ok, all} <- read() do
      case Enum.find(all, &(&1.id == id_or_name || &1.name == id_or_name)) do
        nil -> {:error, :not_found}
        belief -> {:ok, belief}
      end
    end
  end

  def write(beliefs, path \\ nil) do
    path = path || Config.beliefs_path()
    ordered = Enum.map(beliefs, &Belief.to_map/1)
    content = Jason.encode!(ordered, pretty: true) <> "\n"
    JSON.write_atomic_raw(path, content)
  end
end

    if File.exists?(path) do
      with {:ok, data} <- JSON.read(path) do
        {:ok, Enum.map(data, &Belief.from_map/1)}
      end
    else
      {:ok, []}
    end
  end

  @doc "Alias for read/0. Reads all entries."
  def read_all, do: read()

  @doc "Find a belief by ID or name."
  def find(id_or_name) do
    with {:ok, all} <- read() do
      case Enum.find(all, &(&1.id == id_or_name || &1.name == id_or_name)) do
        nil -> {:error, :not_found}
        belief -> {:ok, belief}
      end
    end
  end

  def write(beliefs, path \\ nil) do
    path = path || Config.beliefs_path()
    ordered = Enum.map(beliefs, &Belief.to_map/1)
    content = Jason.encode!(ordered, pretty: true) <> "\n"
    JSON.write_atomic_raw(path, content)
  end
end
=== beliefs_path + dir ===
  then `CB_BELIEFS`, then the default path.
  """

  @doc "Absolute path to the belief graph JSON file."
  def beliefs_path do
    Application.get_env(:cb, :beliefs_path) ||
      System.get_env("CB_BELIEFS") ||
      Path.join(CB.repo_root(), "beliefs/beliefs.json")
  end

  @doc """
  Absolute path to the materialized-todos JSON file.

  This is the sink the default materializer (`CB.Materializer.Sink.JSON`)
  appends action items to. Override via application env to point at a
  host application's own todo collection.

content.thinking:
I'm looking at the belief structure—202 beliefs with types of directive, primitive, or inference, and statuses that are either active or superseded. The claims are substantial descriptions, and dependencies reference other beliefs by ID. Now I need to map each belief to an OKF document format, which means translating the CB type system to match the taxonomy required by the validation function.

For the type mapping, I'll convert primitive beliefs to reference documents, compound to concept, inference to analysis, and directive to position. I want to preserve both the OKF type and the original CB kind (like convention or design-rationale) by storing the CB structural type in a custom field. Since the validator doesn't enforce these optional fields, I can include extra metadata without breaking validation. For dependencies, I need to emit all beliefs—both active and superseded—so that dependency references resolve cleanly. Superseded beliefs already have the required `superseded_by` field in the CB data, so I'll include that in the frontmatter to satisfy the validator's checks.

For the description field, I'm using the claim text directly, which is typically a single line but could contain special characters like colons or quotes. I'll write it as a folded YAML block to safely encode it in the frontmatter, ensuring my parser can read it back correctly without truncation issues.

For the title, I'll use the belief's name if available, otherwise fall back to its ID—keeping it simple and safe from special character issues.

Now mapping the remaining fields: status translates from the claim's status to OKF equivalents (active stays active, superseded stays superseded, retracted stays retracted, and retired becomes retracted), timestamp comes from the belief's creation date in YYYY-MM-DD format, tier is always "cb" since all beliefs are CB tier, and dependencies are the belief's dependency list written as an inline array that my parser can read and the validator can resolve against the bundle IDs.

For cross-linking, I'm adding a "Depends on" section in the body that links to dependent belief documents by their file paths. Since belief IDs contain colons which don't work in filenames, I'm mapping "cb:a098" to "cb-a098.md"—the frontmatter keeps the real ID with the colon, but the file itself uses the hyphenated version, and body links reference the hyphenated filenames so the validator can confirm they exist.

I'm also considering whether to preserve additional CB metadata like structural type, kind, domain, artifact, and evidence in the frontmatter for round-trip fidelity, though the ingest process is inherently lossy by design.

For evidence specifically, since it's a list of nested maps and my frontmatter parser only handles scalars and simple lists, I can't serialize it faithfully—but that's acceptable since the canonical store remains beliefs.json and emit is just a projection anyway.

The emit process will also generate an index.md for the bundle to avoid missing-index warnings and provide a relevance hook, plus a manifest.json after writing all docs. For ingest, I'm generating primitive belief IDs from OKF documents by combining a namespace (defaulting to "okf") with a path slug, so a generic OKF bundle without CB IDs gets IDs like "okf:<slug>".

Each ingested document becomes a primitive belief with its description as the claim, an artifact reference like "document:<relpath>", a created timestamp (using Date.utc_today() in Elixir, or frontmatter timestamp if present), tags from the doc, empty deps since primitives are lossy, and an active status. The ingest command will output these belief maps as JSON to stdout or a file, reusing the CB serialization layer.

For implementation, I'll build belief maps and pass them through Belief.from_map then to_map to ensure canonical ordering and schema consistency. I should also design the emit and ingest functions to accept beliefs or bundle data explicitly so tests can work with hand-made data rather than the real graph, then have the mix tasks load from the actual store and call those functions.

Writing the Emit module... Writing the document output... I'm working through the YAML safety of the id field — "cb:a098" contains a colon but no colon-space, so it's a valid plain scalar. I'll set the title to something readable like "Belief cb:a098" which is also YAML-safe, and keep the description as the folded claim. For the artifact field, values like "user:2026-03-22" or document URLs follow the same pattern — colons without spaces are fine as plain scalars.

For the deps list, I'm using an inline format that my parser splits on commas, and I'll only write the field if there are dependencies. The superseded_by field is required when status is superseded, and tags are simple words that work fine in a list. Now I'm thinking through the body generation — I need to build markdown with the title, claim, and a "Depends on" section that links to dependency files, but I'm realizing there's a risk: if a dependency points to a belief that wasn't emitted (like a retracted one), the link will break. To be safe, I should emit all beliefs regardless of status so every dependency resolves.

Actually, retracted beliefs have the necessary fields and don't need superseded_by, so emitting everything works. The filename conversion is straightforward — replace colons with hyphens and add .md. Now I'm setting up the index file metadata with frontmatter that includes type, title, description, status, timestamp, and tags, and I need to figure out how to handle the timestamp deterministically for tests. Writing the ingest module...

I need to fix the operator precedence in the timestamp line—the pipe operator binds tighter than the or operator, so I need parentheses around the date conversion. Then I'm thinking about the output format: the ingest function returns a list of maps, and I want to ensure they're canonically ordered and don't include null values. I could route each belief map through the CB.Belief module's from_map and to_map functions in the task itself to normalize the structure and drop nils, then encode with Jason.

Actually, a cleaner approach is to avoid putting nil values into the map in the first place. I'm building a helper function that conditionally adds keys only when they have non-nil values, so the primitive map never contains empty slots. Then I can encode directly with Jason, though routing through the Belief module would give me canonical key ordering for consistency.

For the mix task, I'm planning to emit a JSON array of primitives and also write tests covering the full workflow: emitting belief structures to JSON, ingesting a small OKF bundle to extract primitives, and a round-trip test that shows the lossy nature of the conversion. I need to handle both struct and map inputs carefully, normalizing them to string-keyed maps for consistent processing.

Writing the adapter functions... Writing emit.ex...

My layer's committed; the integration tooling is in place. Now the **emit/ingest adapter** — the actual CB↔OKF bridge. I understand the belief shape now (202 beliefs: directive/primitive/inference, statuses active/superseded, deps are `cb:aNNN` ids). I'll work directly on `main` and commit only my new files.

Design: `emit` projects each belief → a `tier: cb` OKF doc (deps→frontmatter + body cross-links) and the success criterion is **the emitted bundle passes `mix knowledge.validate`**; `ingest` is the deliberately-lossy up-conversion (every doc → a primitive belief, `artifact: document:<path>`).

defmodule CB.Knowledge.Emit do
  @moduledoc """
  Project a CB belief graph *down* to an OKF bundle (the "CB serializes to OKF"
  direction). Each belief becomes one `tier: cb` OKF document: its claim is the
  description, its `deps` become both frontmatter and body cross-links, supersession
  carries over. The emitted bundle is designed to pass `mix knowledge.validate`.

  This is a lossy projection — nested evidence/subjects are not represented in the
  frontmatter subset; the canonical store remains `beliefs/beliefs.json`. The CB
  structural type is preserved in a `cb_type` field so a CB-aware reader can recover it.
  """
  alias CB.Knowledge.Manifest

  @type_map %{
    "primitive" => "reference",
    "compound" => "concept",
    "inference" => "analysis",
    "directive" => "position"
  }
  @status_map %{
    "active" => "active",
    "superseded" => "superseded",
    "retracted" => "retracted",
    "retired" => "retracted"
  }

  @doc "Emit `beliefs` (CB.Belief structs or string-keyed maps) as an OKF bundle at `out_dir`."
  def bundle(beliefs, out_dir) do
    File.mkdir_p!(out_dir)
    beliefs = Enum.map(beliefs, &to_string_map/1)
    ids = MapSet.new(beliefs, & &1["id"])

    Enum.each(beliefs, fn b -> File.write!(Path.join(out_dir, filename(b["id"])), doc(b, ids)) end)
    File.write!(Path.join(out_dir, "index.md"), index_doc(length(beliefs)))
    File.write!(Path.join(out_dir, "manifest.json"), Manifest.render(out_dir))
    {:ok, length(beliefs)}
  end

  @doc "Filename for a belief id: `cb:a098` -> `cb-a098.md`."
  def filename(id), do: String.replace(id, ":", "-") <> ".md"

  defp doc(b, ids) do
    deps = b["deps"] || []

    fm =
      [
        {"type", Map.get(@type_map, b["type"], "concept")},
        {"title", "Belief #{b["id"]}"},
        {"description", {:fold, b["claim"]}},
        {"status", Map.get(@status_map, b["status"] || "active", "active")},
        {"timestamp", b["created"]},
        {"tier", "cb"},
        {"id", b["id"]},
        {"cb_type", b["type"]}
      ]
      |> opt("kind", b["kind"])
      |> opt("domain", b["domain"])
      |> opt("artifact", b["artifact"])
      |> opt("superseded_by", b["superseded_by"])
      |> list_field("tags", b["tags"])
      |> list_field("deps", deps)

    body =
      ["# Belief #{b["id"]}", "", b["claim"]] ++ depends_section(deps, ids)

    render(fm, Enum.join(body, "\n") <> "\n")
  end

  defp depends_section(deps, ids) do
    present = Enum.filter(deps, &MapSet.member?(ids, &1))

    case present do
      [] -> []
      _ -> ["", "## Depends on"] ++ Enum.map(present, fn d -> "- [#{d}](#{filename(d)})" end)
    end
  end

  defp index_doc(count) do
    fm = [
      {"type", "index"},
      {"title", "CB belief graph (OKF projection)"},
      {"description",
       {:fold, "Use when you need the OKF projection of the Composable Beliefs graph: #{count} tier:cb docs, one per belief, with deps as cross-links."}},
      {"status", "active"},
      {"timestamp", Date.to_iso8601(Date.utc_today())},
      {"tags", {:list, ["cb", "okf", "index"]}}
    ]

    render(fm, "# CB belief graph (OKF projection)\n\nGenerated from beliefs.json by `mix knowledge.emit`.\n")
  end

  # ---- frontmatter rendering ----

  defp render(fm_pairs, body), do: "---\n" <> Enum.map_join(fm_pairs, "\n", &line/1) <> "\n---\n\n" <> body

  defp line({k, {:fold, v}}), do: "#{k}: >\n  #{v}"
  defp line({k, {:list, items}}), do: "#{k}: [#{Enum.join(items, ", ")}]"
  defp line({k, v}), do: "#{k}: #{v}"

  defp opt(pairs, _k, nil), do: pairs
  defp opt(pairs, _k, ""), do: pairs
  defp opt(pairs, k, v), do: pairs ++ [{k, v}]

  defp list_field(pairs, _k, nil), do: pairs
  defp list_field(pairs, _k, []), do: pairs
  defp list_field(pairs, k, items), do: pairs ++ [{k, {:list, items}}]

  # ---- input normalization ----

  defp to_string_map(%CB.Belief{} = b) do
    b
    |> Map.from_struct()
    |> Enum.reduce(%{}, fn
      {:_keys, _}, acc -> acc
      {k, v}, acc -> Map.put(acc, Atom.to_string(k), v)
    end)
  end

  defp to_string_map(m) when is_map(m), do: Map.new(m, fn {k, v} -> {to_string(k), v} end)
end

input.content:
defmodule CB.Knowledge.Ingest do
  @moduledoc """
  Ingest an OKF bundle *up* into CB beliefs. Per the CB↔OKF analysis this direction is
  intentionally lossy: a generic OKF bundle carries no typed deps or evidence, so every
  document becomes a `primitive` belief grounded in `artifact: document:<path>`. Typed
  composition is not reconstructed — that is the discipline CB adds on top, by hand or
  via `/assert`, not something the up-conversion can invent.

  Returns CB belief maps (string-keyed) ready to encode or feed to preflight.
  """
  alias CB.Knowledge.{Frontmatter, Manifest}

  @doc "Ingest the bundle at `root` into a list of primitive belief maps under namespace `ns`."
  def beliefs(root, ns \\ "okf") do
    root = Path.expand(root)

    Manifest.doc_paths(root)
    |> Enum.map(fn {_full, rel} -> {rel, Frontmatter.parse(File.read!(Path.join(root, rel)))} end)
    |> Enum.reject(fn {_rel, fm} -> fm == %{} or fm["type"] == "index" end)
    |> Enum.map(fn {rel, fm} -> primitive(ns, rel, fm) end)
  end

  defp primitive(ns, rel, fm) do
    %{
      "id" => fm["id"] || ns <> ":" <> slug(rel),
      "type" => "primitive",
      "tags" => fm["tags"] || [],
      "claim" => fm["description"] || fm["title"] || "",
      "artifact" => "document:" <> rel,
      "evidence" => [],
      "deps" => [],
      "status" => fm["status"] || "active",
      "created" => fm["timestamp"] || Date.to_iso8601(Date.utc_today())
    }
    |> maybe_put("kind", fm["kind"])
    |> maybe_put("domain", fm["domain"])
  end

  defp slug(rel) do
    rel
    |> String.replace(~r/\.md$/, "")
    |> String.replace(~r/[^A-Za-z0-9]+/, "-")
  end

  defp maybe_put(m, _k, nil), do: m
  defp maybe_put(m, k, v), do: Map.put(m, k, v)
end

defmodule Mix.Tasks.Knowledge.Emit do
  @shortdoc "Project the CB belief graph to an OKF bundle"
  @moduledoc """
  #{@shortdoc}

      mix knowledge.emit <out-dir>

  Reads `beliefs/beliefs.json` and writes one `tier: cb` OKF document per belief
  (plus an index and manifest) at <out-dir>. The result is a valid Knowledge bundle —
  verify with `mix knowledge.validate <out-dir>`.
  """
  use Mix.Task

  alias CB.Belief.Store

defmodule Mix.Tasks.Knowledge.Emit do
  @shortdoc "Project the CB belief graph to an OKF bundle"
  @moduledoc """
  #{@shortdoc}

      mix knowledge.emit <out-dir>

  Reads `beliefs/beliefs.json` and writes one `tier: cb` OKF document per belief
  (plus an index and manifest) at <out-dir>. The result is a valid Knowledge bundle —
  verify with `mix knowledge.validate <out-dir>`.
  """
  use Mix.Task

  alias CB.Belief.Store
  alias CB.Knowledge.Emit

  @impl Mix.Task
  def run(argv) do
    out = List.first(argv) || Mix.raise("usage: mix knowledge.emit <out-dir>")

    case Store.read() do
      {:ok, beliefs} ->
        {:ok, n} = Emit.bundle(beliefs, out)
        IO.puts("emitted #{n} beliefs to #{out} (validate with: mix knowledge.validate #{out})")

      {:error, reason} ->
        Mix.raise("could not read belief graph: #{inspect(reason)}")
    end
  end
end

defmodule Mix.Tasks.Knowledge.Ingest do
  @shortdoc "Ingest an OKF bundle into CB primitive beliefs (lossy up-conversion)"
  @moduledoc """
  #{@shortdoc}

      mix knowledge.ingest <bundle-root> [--ns NS] [--out FILE]

  Reads a Knowledge/OKF bundle and emits CB belief JSON — one `primitive` per document,
  grounded in `artifact: document:<path>`. Prints to stdout, or writes to FILE.
  Typed composition is NOT reconstructed (use /assert for that); this only lands the
  documents as attributable primitives.
  """
  use Mix.Task

  alias CB.Belief
  alias CB.Knowledge.Ingest

  @impl Mix.Task
  def run(argv) do
    {opts, rest, _} = OptionParser.parse(argv, switches: [ns: :string, out: :string])
    root = List.first(rest) || Mix.raise("usage: mix knowledge.ingest <bundle-root> [--ns NS] [--out FILE]")
    ns = opts[:ns] || "okf"

    ordered =
      root
      |> Ingest.beliefs(ns)
      |> Enum.map(&(&1 |> Belief.from_map() |> Belief.to_map()))

    content = Jason.encode!(ordered, pretty: true) <> "\n"

    case opts[:out] do
      nil ->
        IO.puts(content)

      path ->
        File.write!(path, content)
        IO.puts("wrote #{length(ordered)} primitives to #{path}")
    end
  end
end

test "to_contract is deterministic and sorted", %{root: root} do
    write(root, "index.md", doc(type: "gizmo", title: "Bad", description: "short"))
    File.write!(Path.join(root, "manifest.json"), Manifest.render(root))
    {errors, warnings} = Validate.run(root)
    json = Validate.to_contract(errors, warnings)
    decoded = Jason.decode!(json)

    assert decoded["ok"] == false
    codes = Enum.map(decoded["errors"], & &1["code"])
    assert codes == Enum.sort(codes)
  end

  describe "adapter" do
    defp beliefs do
      [
        %{
          "id" => "cb:a001",
          "type" => "primitive",
          "kind" => "convention",
          "claim" => "The standard loan period for circulating items is twenty-one days.",
          "artifact" => "document:policy.md",
          "deps" => [],
          "status" => "active",
          "created" => "2026-01-05",
          "tags" => ["policy"]
        },
        %{
          "id" => "cb:a002",
          "type" => "inference",
          "claim" => "Because the loan period is twenty-one days, holds placed today expire mid-month.",
          "deps" => ["cb:a001"],
          "status" => "active",
          "created" => "2026-01-06",
          "tags" => []
        }
      ]
    end

    test "emit produces a bundle that validates green", %{root: root} do
      out = Path.join(root, "okf")
      assert {:ok, 2} = CB.Knowledge.Emit.bundle(beliefs(), out)
      assert File.exists?(Path.join(out, "cb-a001.md"))
      # dep becomes a body cross-link in the dependent doc
      assert File.read!(Path.join(out, "cb-a002.md")) =~ "[cb:a001](cb-a001.md)"

      {errors, warnings} = Validate.run(out)
      assert errors == []
      assert warnings == []
    end

    test "ingest lands every doc as an attributable primitive", %{root: root} do
      out = Path.join(root, "okf")
      CB.Knowledge.Emit.bundle(beliefs(), out)

      ingested = CB.Knowledge.Ingest.beliefs(out, "lib")
      # index.md is skipped; the two belief docs come back as primitives
      assert length(ingested) == 2
      assert Enum.all?(ingested, &(&1["type"] == "primitive"))
      assert Enum.all?(ingested, &String.starts_with?(&1["artifact"], "document:"))
      # lossy: typed deps are dropped on the way up
      assert Enum.all?(ingested, &(&1["deps"] == []))
    end
  end
end

content.content:
# Composable Beliefs

A framework for giving AI agents persistent, source-grounded, inspectable reasoning that survives session boundaries and composes into understanding the agent never explicitly derived.

## Quick Start

Read this file for orientation; the guided tour lives with the teaching material in belief-collections (`../belief-collections/quickstart.md`), alongside the `lib:` on-ramp. Build with `mix deps.get && mix compile`.

Query the graph with the belief shell, deterministic pure traversal with no LLM: `mix bs list [filters]`, `mix bs show <id>`, `mix bs tree <id>`, `mix bs stale`, `mix bs stats`. Run `mix bs help` for the full command set; ids may be bare (`c029`) or namespaced (`cb:c029`).

## Active directives

Active behavioral directives are not cached to a digest file; load them live from the graph by querying active directives (`mix bs list directive`). Per cb:a386, a digest whose freshness depends on remembering to regenerate it is an antipattern that embeds the staleness it was meant to solve - render live from the DAG instead.

## Session start

A session resumes from the graph, never from a handoff: pull the repos, query the desk (`mix bs list unlinked tag:lifecycle:discrete`) for live obligations and `mix bs stale --cascade` for review flags, then follow each directive's deps and document:/plan: artifacts to the records that ground it. Trust the graph over any memory, digest, or note - other sessions write these repos concurrently.

## Architecture

The mechanism is a directed acyclic graph of beliefs with four structural types: primitive (what a single source said), compound (what its deps jointly state), inference (a conclusion licensed to exceed its deps), and directive (what should happen or must hold - a prescription the house stands behind).

Immutability makes change traceable: a belief is never edited in place; a changed belief is superseded by a new one and linked forward. Composition makes the whole greater than its parts - an inference concludes what none of its inputs states alone, and pays for that licence by being falsifiable on its own, independent of its deps.

There are no confidence scores. Subjective scores synthesized without a deterministic basis do no load-bearing work; rely on specific evidence and dependency structure instead.

## Operations

Author beliefs through the write flow, never by hand-editing the graph file: `mix cb.preflight --file <f>` checks a proposed belief for conflicts, `mix cb.adjudicate --file <f>` resolves them, and `mix cb.import --file <f>` writes the belief. Two further doors complete the sanctioned write surface: `mix cb.evidence <id> --detail <text> --artifact <uri>` appends a dated evidence entry to an existing belief - the one sanctioned in-place growth point on an immutable node - and `mix cb.todo.close <id> --notes <text>` flips a materialized item open -> done with discharge notes. Both are dry-run by default and write only with `--write`.

Verify with the graph's own tooling: `mix cb.verify.schema` checks a collection against the contracts it carries, `mix cb.verify.collection <namespace>` checks it together with its declared dependency collections, and `mix cb.generate.claude_md --check` confirms this file is current.

## Schema

The schema's single source of truth is `lib/cb/belief.ex`. The graph's own schema is expressed as contracts in the graph: c051 (the four structural types), c053 (status lifecycle and immutability), c056 (schema discipline), c039/c043/c041 (the closed kind/artifact-scheme/domain enums), c057 (the kind-type table), c058 (subject containment), c059 (directive grounding), and c055 (conflict scope). Read one with `mix bs show cb:c051`.

Schema rules in force: no `confidence` field; no `patch` kind; no separate `implication` prose field, since meaning is carried by `claim` plus `deps`; primitives ground their claim with an `artifact` URI and dated `evidence`; non-contract directives ground in deps or a stipulation artifact; and `contract: true` is biconditional with non-empty `rules`/`invariants` (per c056).

## Skills

Skills coordinate authoring, query, and presentation: `/assert` adds beliefs from artifacts, entities, or reasoning; `/assertions` queries and traverses the graph; `/materialize` turns directives into concrete tasks; `/position` captures a settled stance as an anchored position document in the host repo's positions/ - ceremony-gated, anchors verified at authoring time, extraction left to `/assert`; `/present-codepath` walks a codepath - a code-anchored collection rendered as a narrated, branching tour of real source that can also run as a test suite (`mix cb.verify.codepath`).

## Collections

The framework ships only its own `cb:` graph (`beliefs/beliefs.json`). Worked examples and other collections live in belief-collections (staged at `../belief-collections/`), each with a `manifest.json` declaring its namespace and cross-namespace `depends_on`; the `lib:` lending-library is the gentle on-ramp. Query a collection with `mix bs --beliefs <path>` and verify one against its declared deps with `mix cb.verify.collection <namespace>`.

## Formatting

Never use emdashes. Use hyphens (-) instead.

## Git Policy

Never commit or push unless explicitly instructed.

## Data Protection

Never modify files under `beliefs/` unless the user explicitly requests and authorizes the change. Reading and querying is always allowed.

## Origin

Composable Beliefs was extracted from a live operational system where the belief graph was built and battle-tested against real workflows. This repository is the generic framework plus its own self-describing design graph.

defmodule CB.KnowledgeTest do
  use ExUnit.Case, async: true

  alias CB.Knowledge.{Frontmatter, Manifest, Validate}

  setup do
    root = Path.join(System.tmp_dir!(), "kn_#{System.unique_integer([:positive])}")
    File.mkdir_p!(root)
    on_exit(fn -> File.rm_rf(root) end)
    {:ok, root: root}
  end

  defp write(root, rel, contents) do
    path = Path.join(root, rel)
    File.mkdir_p!(Path.dirname(path))
    File.write!(path, contents)
  end

  defp doc(fields, body \\ "Body.\n") do
    fm = Enum.map_join(fields, "\n", fn {k, v} -> "#{k}: #{v}" end)
    "---\n#{fm}\n---\n\n#{body}"
  end

  test "frontmatter parses the documented subset" do
    fm =
      Frontmatter.parse("""
      ---
      type: concept
      title: T
      description: >
        folded line one
        line two
      tags: [a, b]
      deps:
        - x:1
        - x:2
      ---
      body
      """)

    assert fm["type"] == "concept"
    assert fm["description"] == "folded line one line two"
    assert fm["tags"] == ["a", "b"]
    assert fm["deps"] == ["x:1", "x:2"]
  end

  test "no frontmatter yields empty map" do
    assert Frontmatter.parse("# just markdown\n") == %{}
  end

  test "valid bundle passes with fresh manifest", %{root: root} do
    write(root, "index.md", doc(type: "index", title: "Idx",
      description: "Use when orienting to this valid test bundle root index."))

    File.write!(Path.join(root, "manifest.json"), Manifest.render(root))
    {errors, warnings} = Validate.run(root)
    assert errors == []
    assert warnings == []
  end

  test "flags bad type, short description, stale manifest", %{root: root} do
    write(root, "index.md", doc(type: "gizmo", title: "Bad", description: "short"))
    File.write!(Path.join(root, "manifest.json"), "{}\n")

    {errors, _warnings} = Validate.run(root)
    codes = MapSet.new(errors, & &1.code)
    assert "type_not_in_taxonomy" in codes
    assert "description_missing_or_short" in codes
    assert "manifest_stale" in codes
  end

  test "cb-tier doc without id is flagged; resolving deps pass", %{root: root} do
    write(root, "index.md", doc(type: "index", title: "Idx",
      description: "Use when orienting to the CB-tier test bundle index page."))
    write(root, "base.md", doc(type: "concept", title: "Base",
      description: "Use when you need the base belief the derived one depends upon.",
      tier: "cb", id: "t:base"))
    write(root, "derived.md", doc(type: "concept", title: "Derived",
      description: "Use when you need the derived belief composed from the base one.",
      tier: "cb", id: "t:derived", deps: "[t:base]"))

    File.write!(Path.join(root, "manifest.json"), Manifest.render(root))
    {errors, warnings} = Validate.run(root)
    assert errors == []
    assert warnings == []

    # now break the id on base
    write(root, "base.md", doc(type: "concept", title: "Base",
      description: "Use when you need the base belief the derived one depends upon.",
      tier: "cb"))
    File.write!(Path.join(root, "manifest.json"), Manifest.render(root))
    {errors, warnings} = Validate.run(root)
    assert Enum.any?(errors, &(&1.code == "cb_tier_missing_id"))
    assert Enum.any?(warnings, &(&1.code == "dep_unresolved_in_bundle"))
  end

  test "to_contract is deterministic and sorted", %{root: root} do
    write(root, "index.md", doc(type: "gizmo", title: "Bad", description: "short"))
    File.write!(Path.join(root, "manifest.json"), Manifest.render(root))
    {errors, warnings} = Validate.run(root)
    json = Validate.to_contract(errors, warnings)
    decoded = Jason.decode!(json)

    assert decoded["ok"] == false
    codes = Enum.map(decoded["errors"], & &1["code"])
    assert codes == Enum.sort(codes)
  end
end

content.thinking:
I'm seeing validation errors where angle brackets in belief claims are being interpreted as template placeholders. The regex pattern `<[A-Za-z][^>\n]*>` is matching legitimate content like `<id>`, `<text>`, and `<artifact>` that appear in claims about CLI commands and schemas, causing false positives in the template validation.

The core issue is distinguishing between actual unfilled template placeholders (which tend to be Title-case or contain spaces like `<Entity or idea>`) and legitimate CLI argument notation (lowercase single words like `<id>`). I could refine the regex to be more specific, but it's tricky to reliably separate the two patterns. The cleaner approach might be to scope the placeholder check more carefully or adjust how the content is emitted to avoid triggering the validator on legitimate claims. The space-based discriminator won't work reliably since template placeholders vary—some have spaces, some don't. The real issue is that the placeholder check is too broad: it catches legitimate technical notation like `<id>` and `<file>` alongside actual unfilled templates. The most pragmatic fix is to make the standard's check more conservative by only flagging the specific placeholder tokens that actually appear in the templates, rather than any angle-bracket content, though that requires coordinating changes across the conformance fixtures and reference implementations. The template placeholders and CLI arguments overlap too much in form to distinguish reliably with regex — `<id>` from the CLI looks identical to `<what>` from a template. So the heuristic is fundamentally limited. The real fix is either to scope the check to known template strings, treat it as a warning instead of an error, or have emit escape the angle brackets so they don't trigger the validator in the first place.

Actually, the better approach is to align with the spec: the description frontmatter should be a short relevance hook, not the full claim text. The full claim belongs in the body, where the placeholder check doesn't scan anyway. So I'll generate a safe, generic hook for the description (like "Belief cb:a131 (inference, design-rationale). Use when reasoning about this claim.") and put the raw claim with all its `<...>` tokens in the body where it's allowed. Updating the emit function to sanitize angle brackets in the description...

content.text: End-to-end mostly works (202 emitted, deps/links/manifest/status all clean), but 10 beliefs fail the placeholder check — because their claims legitimately contain CLI notation like `<id>`/`<text>` (CB documenting its own commands). That's a real false-positive: I was wrongly putting the *full claim* into the `description` frontmatter. Per the spec, `description` should be a relevance *hook* and the full claim belongs in the body (which isn't placeholder-checked). Let me fix emit to sanitize the description and keep the verbatim claim in the body:

[
        {"type", Map.get(@type_map, b["type"], "concept")},
        {"title", "Belief #{b["id"]}"},

defmodule CB.Knowledge.Emit do
  @moduledoc """
  Project a CB belief graph *down* to an OKF bundle (the "CB serializes to OKF"
  direction). Each belief becomes one `tier: cb` OKF document: its claim is the
  description, its `deps` become both frontmatter and body cross-links, supersession
  carries over. The emitted bundle is designed to pass `mix knowledge.validate`.

  This is a lossy projection - nested evidence/subjects are not represented in the
  frontmatter subset; the canonical store remains `beliefs/beliefs.json`. The CB
  structural type is preserved in a `cb_type` field so a CB-aware reader can recover it.
  """
  alias CB.Knowledge.Manifest

  @type_map %{
    "primitive" => "reference",
    "compound" => "concept",
    "inference" => "analysis",
    "directive" => "position"
  }
  @status_map %{
    "active" => "active",
    "superseded" => "superseded",
    "retracted" => "retracted",
    "retired" => "retracted"
  }

  @doc "Emit `beliefs` (CB.Belief structs or string-keyed maps) as an OKF bundle at `out_dir`."
  def bundle(beliefs, out_dir) do
    File.mkdir_p!(out_dir)
    beliefs = Enum.map(beliefs, &to_string_map/1)
    ids = MapSet.new(beliefs, & &1["id"])

    Enum.each(beliefs, fn b -> File.write!(Path.join(out_dir, filename(b["id"])), doc(b, ids)) end)
    File.write!(Path.join(out_dir, "index.md"), index_doc(length(beliefs)))
    File.write!(Path.join(out_dir, "manifest.json"), Manifest.render(out_dir))
    {:ok, length(beliefs)}
  end

  @doc "Filename for a belief id: `cb:a098` -> `cb-a098.md`."
  def filename(id), do: String.replace(id, ":", "-") <> ".md"

  defp doc(b, ids) do
    deps = b["deps"] || []

    fm =
      [
        {"type", Map.get(@type_map, b["type"], "concept")},
        {"title", "Belief #{b["id"]}"},
        {"description", {:fold, b["claim"]}},
        {"status", Map.get(@status_map, b["status"] || "active", "active")},
        {"timestamp", b["created"]},
        {"tier", "cb"},
        {"id", b["id"]},
        {"cb_type", b["type"]}
      ]
      |> opt("kind", b["kind"])
      |> opt("domain", b["domain"])
      |> opt("artifact", b["artifact"])
      |> opt("superseded_by", b["superseded_by"])
      |> list_field("tags", b["tags"])
      |> list_field("deps", deps)

    body =
      ["# Belief #{b["id"]}", "", b["claim"]] ++ depends_section(deps, ids)

    render(fm, Enum.join(body, "\n") <> "\n")
  end

  defp depends_section(deps, ids) do
    present = Enum.filter(deps, &MapSet.member?(ids, &1))

      {"tags", {:list, ["cb", "okf", "index"]}}
    ]

    render(fm, "# CB belief graph (OKF projection)\n\nGenerated from beliefs.json by `mix knowledge.emit`.\n")
  end

  # ---- frontmatter rendering ----

  defp render(fm_pairs, body), do: "---\n" <> Enum.map_join(fm_pairs, "\n", &line/1) <> "\n---\n\n" <> body

  defp line({k, {:fold, v}}), do: "#{k}: >\n  #{v}"
  defp line({k, {:list, items}}), do: "#{k}: [#{Enum.join(items, ", ")}]"

defmodule CB.Knowledge.Emit do
  @moduledoc """
  Project a CB belief graph *down* to an OKF bundle (the "CB serializes to OKF"
  direction). Each belief becomes one `tier: cb` OKF document: its claim is the
  description, its `deps` become both frontmatter and body cross-links, supersession
  carries over. The emitted bundle is designed to pass `mix knowledge.validate`.

  This is a lossy projection - nested evidence/subjects are not represented in the
  frontmatter subset; the canonical store remains `beliefs/beliefs.json`. The CB
  structural type is preserved in a `cb_type` field so a CB-aware reader can recover it.
  """
  alias CB.Knowledge.Manifest

  @type_map %{
    "primitive" => "reference",
    "compound" => "concept",
    "inference" => "analysis",
    "directive" => "position"
  }
  @status_map %{
    "active" => "active",
    "superseded" => "superseded",
    "retracted" => "retracted",
    "retired" => "retracted"
  }

  @doc "Emit `beliefs` (CB.Belief structs or string-keyed maps) as an OKF bundle at `out_dir`."
  def bundle(beliefs, out_dir) do
    File.mkdir_p!(out_dir)
    beliefs = Enum.map(beliefs, &to_string_map/1)
    ids = MapSet.new(beliefs, & &1["id"])

    Enum.each(beliefs, fn b -> File.write!(Path.join(out_dir, filename(b["id"])), doc(b, ids)) end)
    File.write!(Path.join(out_dir, "index.md"), index_doc(length(beliefs)))
    File.write!(Path.join(out_dir, "manifest.json"), Manifest.render(out_dir))
    {:ok, length(beliefs)}
  end

  @doc "Filename for a belief id: `cb:a098` -> `cb-a098.md`."
  def filename(id), do: String.replace(id, ":", "-") <> ".md"

  defp doc(b, ids) do
    deps = b["deps"] || []

    fm =
      [
        {"type", Map.get(@type_map, b["type"], "concept")},
        {"title", "Belief #{b["id"]}"},
        {"description", {:fold, hook(b["claim"])}},
        {"status", Map.get(@status_map, b["status"] || "active", "active")},
        {"timestamp", b["created"]},
        {"tier", "cb"},
        {"id", b["id"]},
        {"cb_type", b["type"]}
      ]
      |> opt("kind", b["kind"])
      |> opt("domain", b["domain"])
      |> opt("artifact", b["artifact"])
      |> opt("superseded_by", b["superseded_by"])
      |> list_field("tags", b["tags"])
      |> list_field("deps", deps)

    body =
      ["# Belief #{b["id"]}", "", b["claim"]] ++ depends_section(deps, ids)

    render(fm, Enum.join(body, "\n") <> "\n")
  end

  defp depends_section(deps, ids) do
    present = Enum.filter(deps, &MapSet.member?(ids, &1))

    case present do
      [] -> []
      _ -> ["", "## Depends on"] ++ Enum.map(present, fn d -> "- [#{d}](#{filename(d)})" end)
    end
  end

  defp index_doc(count) do
    fm = [
      {"type", "index"},
      {"title", "CB belief graph (OKF projection)"},
      {"description",
       {:fold, "Use when you need the OKF projection of the Composable Beliefs graph: #{count} tier:cb docs, one per belief, with deps as cross-links."}},
      {"status", "active"},
      {"timestamp", Date.to_iso8601(Date.utc_today())},
      {"tags", {:list, ["cb", "okf", "index"]}}
    ]

    render(fm, "# CB belief graph (OKF projection)\n\nGenerated from beliefs.json by `mix knowledge.emit`.\n")
  end

  # ---- frontmatter rendering ----

  defp render(fm_pairs, body), do: "---\n" <> Enum.map_join(fm_pairs, "\n", &line/1) <> "\n---\n\n" <> body

  defp line({k, {:fold, v}}), do: "#{k}: >\n  #{v}"
  defp line({k, {:list, items}}), do: "#{k}: [#{Enum.join(items, ", ")}]"
  defp line({k, v}), do: "#{k}: #{v}"

  defp opt(pairs, _k, nil), do: pairs
  defp opt(pairs, _k, ""), do: pairs
  defp opt(pairs, k, v), do: pairs ++ [{k, v}]

  defp list_field(pairs, _k, nil), do: pairs
  defp list_field(pairs, _k, []), do: pairs
  defp list_field(pairs, k, items), do: pairs ++ [{k, {:list, items}}]

  # ---- input normalization ----

  defp to_string_map(%CB.Belief{} = b) do
    b
    |> Map.from_struct()
    |> Enum.reduce(%{}, fn
      {:_keys, _}, acc -> acc
      {k, v}, acc -> Map.put(acc, Atom.to_string(k), v)
    end)
  end

  defp to_string_map(m) when is_map(m), do: Map.new(m, fn {k, v} -> {to_string(k), v} end)
end

content.content:
=== README ===
# cb-direction

Private direction repo for Composable Beliefs: strategy, positioning, competitor evaluation, market and audience analysis, monetization and distribution thinking. Nothing in this repo is intended for publication.

## Why this repo exists

The public CB positioning works because the public story is the epistemic one: evals are where CB's claims are most checkable. Publishing the strategic rationale behind that positioning - which vertical was chosen, why, against which competitors, for which audience - would undercut the credibility the positioning pursues. A reader who finds "we chose evals as a credibility beachhead" in a public repo trusts the ledger less. So the rationale lives here, and only the falsifiable output (README claims, research-backed beliefs, findings) ships publicly.

The boundary, stated once: **public repos carry claims a reader can check; this repo carries the reasoning about how CB competes and spreads.** When a document mixes both, split it - the checkable part graduates out, the strategic part stays.

## Layout

- `direction/` - dated direction documents: positioning decisions, competitor scans, market observations.
- `thesis-strategy.md` - the strategy sections extracted from the public thesis doc (`composable-beliefs/docs/composable-beliefs-thesis.md`), relocated 2026-06-12.
- `plans/` - strategy plan briefs relocated from the public repo.

## Routing rule (hardcoded in /end)

Direction content discovered in a session routes to exactly one of two destinations, by subject:

- CB-the-system, its positioning, its competitors -> `composablebeliefs/cb-direction` (this repo)
- amieval, the broader operation, cross-system strategy -> `amieval/amieval-direction`

No other categorical destinations exist or may be created.
=== most recent direction note (format reference) ===
# Direction: CB as the machine-checkable heuristics-and-biases instrument

> **SUPERSEDED 2026-06-16.** This document's one-sentence definition of CB ("the
> machine-checkable instantiation of the fifty-year debiasing verdict") is
> withdrawn: it elevated a lens (Kahneman / System 2) and a vertical (evals) to
> the rank of CB's identity, which contradicts CB's domain-agnostic identity
> (6-5 idea-brief, 6-6 integration analysis). CB's identity now lives in
> `2026-06-16-identity-and-invariants/cb-identity-and-invariants.md`, stated
> without reference to any consumer. The Kahneman / semantic-reliability / eval
> content below belongs to the consumer side and is relocated to
> `amieval-direction/direction/2026-06-16-the-division/the-division.md`. The §4b
> content-addressing capability is a general CB feature and is to be restated
> generically in cb-direction (no assurance-buyer / eval motivation in the core).
> Retained here only as historical provenance.

**Date:** 2026-06-13
**Status:** superseded by 2026-06-16 (was: proposed)
**Origin:** the cross-system session recorded at
`amieval-direction/direction/2026-06-13-lineage/multi-agent-semantic-reliability-direction.md`,
with the verbatim thread copied here at `direction/2026-06-13-thread/conversation.md`.
This is a same-day ground-up rewrite of the morning's first draft of this file,
elevating Kahneman's heuristics-and-biases program from a supporting citation to
CB's organizing concept. It changes nothing in `2026-06-12-monetization-deferral.md`,
`2026-06-12-claim-the-eval-vertical.md` (which already named the Kahneman program
as origin), the verdict-category firewall (`method:a6`), or the self-eval venue
rule (`method:a7`); it states more precisely _what CB is_ in that lineage.

---

## What CB is, in one sentence

> **CB is the machine-checkable instantiation of the fifty-year debiasing
> verdict** — that systematic error yields to procedure and structure, not to
> awareness and vigilance (Meehl; Kahneman & Tversky) — applied to AI reasoning,
> at both the individual and the group scale.

Everything else CB does (claim-typing, supersession, earned confidence, the
evidence ledger) is a consequence of taking that verdict literally and making the
procedures runnable.

=== git ===
main

elevating Kahneman's heuristics-and-biases program from a supporting citation to
CB's organizing concept. It changes nothing in `2026-06-12-monetization-deferral.md`,
`2026-06-12-claim-the-eval-vertical.md` (which already named the Kahneman program
as origin), the verdict-category firewall (`method:a6`), or the self-eval venue
rule (`method:a7`); it states more precisely _what CB is_ in that lineage.

---

## What CB is, in one sentence

> **CB is the machine-checkable instantiation of the fifty-year debiasing
> verdict** — that systematic error yields to procedure and structure, not to
> awareness and vigilance (Meehl; Kahneman & Tversky) — applied to AI reasoning,
> at both the individual and the group scale.

Everything else CB does (claim-typing, supersession, earned confidence, the
evidence ledger) is a consequence of taking that verdict literally and making the
procedures runnable.

# Direction: CB as a conformant OKF consumer, not a format owner

**Date:** 2026-06-21
**Status:** accepted (decision settled and built same day)
**Origin:** design session that produced the `knowledge` standard repo
(`ob6to8/knowledge`); the verbatim thread is persisted as that repo's design record at
`knowledge/meta/2026-06-21-okf-cb-design-session.md`, with the layering analysis at
`knowledge/meta/okf-vs-cb-layering.md` and the public, falsifiable position at
`knowledge/meta/two-tier-knowledge-architecture.md`. This note is the strategic
rationale that does not ship; the checkable artifacts are the standard repo and CB's
integration layer.

---

## The trigger

Google published the Open Knowledge Format (OKF) - markdown + YAML frontmatter, one
required field (`type`), untyped cross-links, `index.md`/`log.md`, packaged as a
git repo/tarball. It is the productization of Karpathy's LLM-wiki and credits him by
name. The practical question was whether to build a generalized knowledge base across
all repos (software plus productivity domains) on OKF, and how that sits relative to CB.

## The finding (why this is not a competitive threat)

OKF and CB are different layers, and they compose cleanly:

- **OKF is a transport/document layer.** It answers "how do I serialize and ship
  knowledge documents portably?" By its own statement it is "a format, not a platform,"
  deliberately minimal.
- **CB is an epistemic-semantics layer.** It answers "what does a claim mean, what does
  it depend on, on what evidence, is it still true, and what does it obligate?" - typed
  claims, immutability + dated supersession, evidence-grounded provenance, staleness over
  a real dependency DAG, machine-checkable contracts, a deterministic LLM-free read path.

CB serializes *down* to OKF (every belief is trivially a typed markdown doc; deps become
cross-links; supersession becomes log/frontmatter). OKF cannot reconstruct CB *up*
without inventing exactly the discipline CB adds - the up-conversion is lossy. That is
the definition of CB sitting above OKF.

## The position

**CB is a conformant consumer (a dialect) of OKF, not a format owner.** CB does not
compete with OKF and must not posture as a rival standard. The public framing is "CB is
the typed, verifiable, supersedable dialect of OKF" - it speaks OKF for interoperability
and adds the epistemic discipline OKF deliberately omits.

This is strictly good for CB:

1. **It validates the "format not platform" thesis CB already holds** (the same argument
   the thesis makes from git / JSON Schema / OpenTelemetry / MCP winning as open
   standards). OKF just won that argument first for the document layer, on our behalf.
2. **It hands CB a free on-ramp and serialization target.** Interop is now a small
   adapter, not a strategy.

## The risk, and why it sharpens rather than threatens the eval vertical

OKF raises the floor CB must clear. The bet-dossier crux - *does structured substrate
decisively beat strong plain-English instructions?* - gets harder when the "plain"
baseline is a standardized, agent-maintained OKF wiki with cross-links and history. OKF
gives ~70% of the felt value (portable, persistent, linkable, agent-curated) for ~0% of
the discipline cost.

But that same fact is a gift to the eval vertical (see `2026-06-12-claim-the-eval-vertical.md`):
before OKF, the prose baseline in a dag-vs-prose eval was a strawman we defined. OKF now
*defines* a credible, externally-legitimate prose baseline for us. The eval becomes more
believable, not less, because the control is no longer ours to weaken. The move is to run
the dag-vs-prose eval **with an OKF/Karpathy wiki as the prose arm** and let CB's
remaining 30% (immutability, typed deps, contracts, determinism, self-reference) earn its
keep on tasks where time-truth and adjudication actually matter.

## What was built (the checkable output)

- `ob6to8/knowledge` - the standard: OKF profile, the OKF-floor / CB-ceiling **two-tier
  rule** (CB rigor is opt-in only where time-truth, mechanical contradiction detection,
  or audit matters), templates, a Python reference validator, and a conformance suite
  that defines the format as behaviour so multiple implementations can be checked for
  equivalence.
- `composablebeliefs/composable-beliefs` - CB's **OKF integration layer**:
  `mix knowledge.manifest` / `mix knowledge.validate` (conformant Elixir ports, byte-equal
  to the reference, passing all conformance fixtures), plus the bridge
  `mix knowledge.emit` (the 202-belief graph projects to an OKF bundle that validates with
  zero errors) and `mix knowledge.ingest` (the lossy up-conversion: OKF docs land as
  attributable primitives, leaving typed composition to `/assert`).

## Boundary held

Per the routing rule, the falsifiable claims (the standard, the conformance results, the
integration layer) ship in the public repos; this note - the reasoning about why OKF
adoption strengthens CB's position and how to wield it in the eval vertical - stays here.

# CLAUDE.md — knowledge repo

This repo is the **Knowledge methodology**: a portable OKF-based format for knowledge
across all my repos and productivity domains, with Composable Beliefs as an opt-in
rigor ceiling.

If you are a fresh agent pointed here:

- **"Adopt this methodology"** → read [`ADOPT.md`](ADOPT.md).
- **"Convert an existing store"** → read [`CONVERT.md`](CONVERT.md).
- **Understand the format** → read [`spec/KNOWLEDGE.md`](spec/KNOWLEDGE.md) first, then
  `spec/types.md`, `spec/frontmatter.md`, `spec/tiers.md`.
- **Understand why it's shaped this way** → [`meta/`](meta/) is the foundational design
  record (the originating session, the OKF-vs-CB analysis, the two-tier decision).
- **See it used** → [`demo/`](demo/) is a synthetic example bundle; start at
  [`demo/manifest.json`](demo/manifest.json).

After any change to a bundle, regenerate its manifest, e.g.:
`python3 tools/build_manifest.py demo` (and `… meta`)

This repo is a **standard with conformance-tested implementations**, not an app. The
Python tools in `tools/` are the reference impl; [`conformance/`](conformance/) defines
the format as behaviour (`./conformance/run.sh` must stay green). A planned Elixir
`mix knowledge.*` impl lives in the CB **OKF integration layer** (the CB↔OKF adapter +
mix-task validators) and must pass the same suite via `VALIDATE_CMD`.

Core rule: everything defaults to the **OKF floor** (plain markdown + frontmatter +
generated manifest). Promote a document to the **CB ceiling** (`tier: cb`, `id`, `deps`)
only when it trips the boundary test in `spec/tiers.md`.

# Conformance suite

The single source of truth for what "a valid Knowledge bundle" means, defined as
behaviour rather than prose. It exists so the format can have **more than one
implementation** — the Python reference tools in `../tools/`, and (planned) Elixir
`mix knowledge.*` tasks in the CB integration layer — without the two silently drifting.

## The contract

For every fixture under `fixtures/`, a conformant validator must emit, in `--json`
mode, exactly the object recorded in `expected/<fixture>.json`:

```json
{ "ok": <bool>, "errors": [{"path","code"}...], "warnings": [{"path","code"}...] }
```

with `errors` and `warnings` sorted by `(code, path)`.

**Only the `(severity, code, path)` triples are normative.** Human-readable messages,
the bundle root path, and ordering beyond the defined sort are NOT part of the contract,
so implementations are free to word findings differently. The stable `code` values are
documented at the top of `../tools/validate.py`.

## Running

Against the Python reference implementation:

```sh
./conformance/run.sh
```

Against any other implementation, point `VALIDATE_CMD` at a command that accepts
`<bundle-root> --json` and prints the contract object:

```sh
VALIDATE_CMD="mix knowledge.validate" ./conformance/run.sh
```

The runner exits non-zero if any fixture's output differs from its expected file.

## Fixtures

`fixtures/valid/` must produce `ok: true`; `fixtures/invalid/` each isolate a single
hard-check failure so a divergence points straight at the responsible rule:

| Fixture | Asserts |
|---|---|
| `valid_minimal` | a minimal well-formed bundle passes |
| `valid_cb_ok` | CB-tier docs with ids + resolving deps pass with no warnings |
| `invalid_bad_type` | `type_not_in_taxonomy` |
| `invalid_weak_description` | `description_missing_or_short` |
| `invalid_placeholder` | `placeholder_in_frontmatter` |
| `invalid_status` | `invalid_status` |
| `invalid_timestamp` | `invalid_timestamp` |
| `invalid_broken_link` | `broken_link` |
| `invalid_cb_no_id` | `cb_tier_missing_id` |
| `invalid_dup_id` | `duplicate_id` |
| `invalid_stale_manifest` | `manifest_stale` |
| `invalid_missing_manifest` | `manifest_missing` |

## Changing the corpus

Fixtures and expected files are authored by `regen.py` so they can't drift from each
other by hand:

```sh
python3 conformance/regen.py
```

Review the resulting diff — each `expected/*.json` must reflect the fixture's *intended*
outcome, not merely whatever the current code happened to print.

lines: The single source of truth for what "a valid Knowledge bundle" means, defined as
behaviour rather than prose. It exists so the format can have **more than one
-implementation** — the Python reference tools in `../tools/`, and (planned) Elixir
-`mix knowledge.*` tasks in the CB integration layer — without the two silently drifting.
+implementation** — the Python reference tools in `../tools/`, and the Elixir
+`mix knowledge.*` tasks in CB's OKF integration layer (`composable-beliefs`), which
+passes this suite — without the two silently drifting.

# knowledge

A portable, agent- and human-readable knowledge methodology for use across all my
repos and domains (software projects **and** productivity domains — finances, home,
band ops, personal). This repo is **both the specification and a working example of
itself**: the `meta/` directory is the design record and `demo/` is an example bundle,
both written in the format the spec defines. ("Bundle" is the generic term for any
knowledge directory in this format — so the folders are named for their role, not typed
`bundle/`.)

Point a fresh agent here and say one of:

- **"Adopt this methodology for this repo"** → the agent reads [`ADOPT.md`](ADOPT.md).
- **"Convert this existing knowledge store to this format"** → the agent reads [`CONVERT.md`](CONVERT.md).

This repo is a **standard, not an application**. It ships a Python reference
implementation of the tooling, but the format is defined by the [conformance
suite](conformance/) — so other implementations (e.g. Elixir `mix knowledge.*` tasks in
the [Composable Beliefs](#tiers) integration layer) can be checked for equivalence
rather than trusted to stay in sync.

## What this is, in one breath

Knowledge is **plain markdown files + YAML frontmatter, organized in a directory
hierarchy, with a generated manifest as the agent's entry index.** It is
[Open Knowledge Format (OKF)](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/knowledge-catalog/tree/main/okf)
as the floor, [Karpathy's LLM-wiki](https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f)
as the synthesis discipline, and [Composable Beliefs (CB)](#tiers) as an opt-in
ceiling for knowledge that must stay provably true over time.

## Why it exists

Across ~45 repos, persisted knowledge had drifted into five incompatible patterns
(`plan.md` vs `USER_STORIES.md` vs `DESIGN.md`; `CLAUDE.md` vs `claude.md`;
JSON-canonical vs YAML-canonical vs prose; threads persisted ad hoc). This gives all
of them one cheap shape to converge on, so a fresh agent can build context from any
repo the same way.

## Layout

```
knowledge/
├── README.md            ← you are here
├── ADOPT.md             ← entry point: "adopt this methodology"
├── CONVERT.md           ← entry point: "convert an existing store"
├── spec/
│   ├── KNOWLEDGE.md     ← THE spec (read this first)
│   ├── frontmatter.md   ← the frontmatter spine: field reference
│   ├── types.md         ← the concept-type taxonomy
│   └── tiers.md         ← the OKF floor vs CB ceiling boundary rule
├── meta/                ← THE DESIGN RECORD: how this methodology was conceived
│   ├── index.md
│   ├── manifest.json    ← generated
│   ├── 2026-06-21-okf-cb-design-session.md   ← thread: the session that produced this
│   ├── okf-vs-cb-layering.md                 ← analysis: OKF vs Karpathy vs CB
│   └── two-tier-knowledge-architecture.md    ← position (CB tier): the floor/ceiling decision
├── templates/           ← copy-paste starting points, one per type
├── conformance/         ← the format defined as behaviour (SSOT for all implementations)
│   ├── run.sh           ← diffs a validator against expected/*.json
│   ├── fixtures/        ← valid + invalid bundles, one isolated failure each
│   └── expected/        ← the normative (severity, code, path) results
├── tools/
│   ├── build_manifest.py  ← regenerates a bundle's manifest.json (stdlib only)
│   └── validate.py        ← the self-verification gate (stdlib only; --json for conformance)
└── demo/               ← a SYNTHETIC example bundle (illustrative sample data only)
    ├── index.md         ← progressive-disclosure root
    ├── manifest.json    ← GENERATED skills-style summary index (do not hand-edit)
    ├── finances/        ← productivity domain (OKF floor)
    └── home/            ← productivity domain (OKF floor)
```

`meta/` is the repo dogfooding its own format on its real history — read it to learn
*why* the spec is shaped this way. `demo/` is throwaway example data you hold up to a
newcomer; it carries no authoritative content.

## The reading order

1. [`spec/KNOWLEDGE.md`](spec/KNOWLEDGE.md) — the model and its three layers.
2. [`spec/frontmatter.md`](spec/frontmatter.md) — the fields.
3. [`spec/types.md`](spec/types.md) — what kinds of documents exist.
4. [`spec/tiers.md`](spec/tiers.md) — when to stay cheap (OKF) vs when to add rigor (CB).
5. [`meta/`](meta/) — the design record, then [`demo/`](demo/) — see the format used.

All four steps done.

## 1. Commit ✓
The integration layer was already committed via the earlier merge; nothing of mine was pending (your `docs/reference.md` + `cb.repoint` WIP left untouched throughout).

## 2. emit/ingest adapter ✓ (committed `b1614b1`, pushed)
The CB↔OKF bridge, in `composable-beliefs`:
- **`mix knowledge.emit <out>`** — projects the belief graph *down* to an OKF bundle: one `tier: cb` doc per belief, claim in the body, `deps` as frontmatter **and** body cross-links, supersession carried over, CB structural type preserved in `cb_type`. **Proof: emitting the real 202-belief graph validates 0 errors / 0 warnings.**
- **`mix knowledge.ingest <bundle>`** — the deliberately-lossy up-conversion: every doc → a `primitive` grounded in `artifact: document:<path>`; typed composition left to `/assert`. (202 docs → 202 primitives.)
- Caught and fixed a real false-positive: belief claims legitimately contain CLI notation like `<id>`, which tripped the placeholder check — so the `description` now carries a sanitized hook and the verbatim claim lives in the body (where it belongs per spec). 8 tests green; respected CB's no-emdash / no-`beliefs/`-writes house rules.

## 3. Push ✓
`composable-beliefs` `main` → `origin` (`35b5d23` + `b1614b1`).

## 4. cb-direction note ✓ (`7e61566`, pushed)
`direction/2026-06-21-cb-as-conformant-okf-consumer.md` — the positioning: CB is a conformant OKF *dialect*, not a format owner; OKF validates the format-not-platform thesis and hands CB a free serialization target; the raised plain-English baseline **sharpens** the dag-vs-prose eval (credible prose control) rather than threatening it. Boundary held: rationale stays private, checkable artifacts ship public.

## 5. knowledge doc fix ✓ (`6da6e6a`, pushed)
README / CLAUDE / conformance README updated — the Elixir impl is no longer "planned"; it exists, passes the suite, and adds `emit`/`ingest`.

**Final state across the three repos:**
| Repo | HEAD | Pushed |
|---|---|---|
| `ob6to8/knowledge` | `6da6e6a` | ✓ |
| `composablebeliefs/composable-beliefs` | `b1614b1` | ✓ |
| `composablebeliefs/cb-direction` | `7e61566` | ✓ |

The OKF integration layer is complete: conformant tooling + working bidirectional bridge, all proven against the real belief graph. The two-tier knowledge methodology is now a real, multi-implementation standard with CB as its first typed/verifiable consumer.

content.text:
The data confirms the design:

- **OKF-floor docs → no `id`.** Identity is the file path; cross-links are plain markdown links so a generic OKF tool navigates with zero integration. (0 of the demo `finances/`+`home/` docs carry an `id`.)
- **CB-tier docs → yes `id`.** It's the additive stability anchor — required when other docs depend on one, used for typed `deps` and rename-safe linking. (In `meta/`, only the one `tier: cb` position doc has an id; in the CB-emitted bundle, **all 202 belief docs** have ids = their `cb:aNNN`, and `deps` reference those ids — the index.md is the one file without one, since it's not a belief.)
- **Path links stay canonical even on CB-tier docs** — the id rides alongside, we never link by id *alone*, so the bundle never stops being valid OKF.

So: ids are off by default (the cheap floor), on wherever CB rigor applies — which is automatic for anything emitted from the belief graph.

Net state: `knowledge` is now a sister repo under `amieval/`, the OKF integration layer in `composable-beliefs` is complete and conformant, and the positioning is recorded in `cb-direction`. Nothing left pending from this thread unless you want to tackle the remaining ideas (e.g. piloting `CONVERT` on a real store like `SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain`, or wiring `validate` into CI).
