# 2026-06-21 — Knowledge methodology designed, built, and integrated with CB

> **Provenance.** This is the *summary* (Layer 2). The raw verbatim transcript (Layer 1,
> immutable) is captured at session end by `/end` from Claude's session log
> (`~/.claude/projects/-Users-mark-dev-repos-mine-amieval/<session>.jsonl`) into a
> `type: source` doc beside this one. Docs this session authored carry
> `threads: [meta:okf-session-2026-06-21]`; subsequent sessions append their ids.

## What this session was
Evaluate whether to build a generalized, cross-repo knowledge base on the Open Knowledge
Format (OKF), how it relates to Karpathy's LLM-wiki and the existing Composable Beliefs
(CB) system, and how to centralize the currently-haphazard threads/plans/analyses. It
grew into building the standard, CB's integration layer, and the CB↔OKF bridge.

## What was decided
- **OKF is the floor, CB is the opt-in ceiling** (two-tier). OKF is mostly a renaming of
  a pattern already reinvented in `SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain`. CB is a conceptual
  superset with a separate serialization — build an adapter, not a migration; CB's
  `beliefs.json` stays canonical.
- **The synthesis layer IS the wiki** — one body of OKF markdown both agent and human
  read; a rendered site is a throwaway view. Agent reads synthesis first, drills to sources.
- **`description` is a relevance hook + a generated `manifest.json`** give the
  skills-style "understand a doc without loading it" capability.
- **Identity: path-canonical, `id` additive.** Floor docs use path identity; CB-tier docs
  carry a stable `namespace:local` `id` (decoupled from path, survives renames).
- **`knowledge` is a standard, not an app** — defined by a conformance suite so multiple
  implementations can be checked for equivalence. It is a sister repo under `amieval/`.
- **CB is a conformant OKF consumer/dialect, not a format owner** (positioning recorded
  in `cb-direction`).
- **Threads persist as `source` (raw) + `thread` (summary)** with `threads:[]` provenance;
  the operational `/end` directive lives in CB's graph, the format rule in this standard.

## What it produced
- **The standard** (`amieval/knowledge`, `ob6to8/knowledge`): [spec](../standard/KNOWLEDGE.md),
  [types](../standard/types.md), [frontmatter](../standard/frontmatter.md), [tiers](../standard/tiers.md),
  [ADOPT](../ADOPT.md), [CONVERT](../CONVERT.md), templates, `tools/build_manifest.py`,
  `tools/validate.py` (self-verifying, `--json`), and a [conformance suite](../conformance/README.md).
- **CB's OKF integration layer** (`composable-beliefs`): `mix knowledge.manifest` /
  `knowledge.validate` (byte-equal Elixir ports, pass all 13 fixtures) plus the bridge
  `mix knowledge.emit` (the 202-belief graph validates 0/0) and `mix knowledge.ingest`.
- The [layering analysis](okf-vs-cb-layering.md), the [two-tier position](two-tier-knowledge-architecture.md),
  and the cb-direction note `2026-06-21-cb-as-conformant-okf-consumer.md`.
- The **soft id-format check** (`id_format_invalid`, warns) added to both implementations.

## Open / next
- Use the OKF/Karpathy wiki as the prose baseline arm in the dag-vs-prose eval.
- Pilot CONVERT on a real store (e.g. `SECOND-BRAIN/second-brain`, including `[[wikilink]]`
  → path-link + `deps` transforms).
- Decide: rename `standard/` → `standard/` (role-not-type folders); assert the `/end`
  thread-persistence directive into CB's graph; wire the transcript renderer into `/end`.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
---
type: spec
title: Concept Type Taxonomy
description: Use when deciding what `type:` a document should be, or what artifact kinds the bundle supports — the closed list of document types and which layer each belongs to.
tags: [spec, taxonomy, types]
status: active
timestamp: 2026-06-21
id: spec:types
---

# Concept Type Taxonomy

`type` is the one required field. It answers "what kind of document is this?" and places
the doc in a layer. Keep to this closed list; extend it deliberately (a new type is a
spec change, not a per-doc decision).

| `type` | Layer | What it is | Lifecycle |
|---|---|---|---|
| `source` | 1 (raw) | An immutable capture: an ingested article, statement, transcript, email, doc. Read, never rewritten. | Append-only. Never edited after capture. |
| `concept` | 2 (synthesis) | A compiled, maintained page about one entity/idea, synthesized over sources. The "wiki" page. | Rewritten in place (OKF) or superseded (CB). |
| `thread` | 1→2 | A persisted session: what was decided/done, with links to what it produced. The fix for "threads persisted ad hoc". | Captured once; its `description` is the outcome. |
| `plan` | 2 | Intended work. Replaces the `plan.md`/`USER_STORIES.md`/`DESIGN.md` sprawl. | Active → done/superseded. |
| `analysis` | 2 | A worked argument or investigation toward a conclusion. Your dated `analyses/*.md`. | Captured; may be superseded by a better analysis. |
| `position` / `decision` | 2 | A settled stance and why. The thing future-you must not silently reopen. | Active → superseded (with a reason). |
| `reference` | 2 | A stable how-to / runbook / spec / fact sheet. Low churn. | Edited as facts change. |
| `index` | — | A folder landing page for human progressive disclosure. One per directory. | Edited as the folder grows. |
| `spec` | 3 (schema) | A document defining the methodology itself (these files). | Versioned. |

## Placement rules

- **`source` is sacred.** Once captured, do not edit its body — supersede with a new
  capture if the source itself changed. This is what makes provenance trustworthy.
- **`concept` is where synthesis lives.** When a new `source` lands, the work is
  updating the relevant `concept` docs — not piling up more sources.
- **`thread` closes the centralization gap.** End a working session by writing one
  `thread` doc whose `description` lets a future agent find it without replaying the
  transcript, linking to the `plan`/`analysis`/`concept`/beliefs it produced.
- **`position` is the CB-promotion candidate.** Stances that must not be silently
  reopened are exactly where dated supersession (CB tier) earns its cost.

## Thread & session persistence

A working session persists as a **pair**, not just a summary — the raw conversation is a
Layer-1 source and must be kept, not only synthesized:

- A `source` doc holds (or points at) the **raw, verbatim transcript** — immutable. For
  agent sessions this is rendered/copied from the host's session log (for Claude Code:
  `~/.claude/projects/<project>/<session>.jsonl`). Because the log is only complete at
  session end, capture is an **end-of-session** step, not a mid-session one.
- A `thread` doc is the **summary**: its `description` is the outcome, its body links the
  raw `source` and everything the session produced.

**Traceability is bidirectional and lives in the docs, not git:**

- *Forward* (thread → docs): the `thread` doc's "What it produced" links.
- *Backward* (doc → threads): each doc carries `threads: [<origin-id>, <later-id>, …]` —
  a scalar list in frontmatter, origin first, appended on each session that edits it. For
  per-touch detail (date · thread · change), add a `## Provenance` table in the doc body
  (the body is unconstrained, so it stays readable without growing the frontmatter parser).
- The rich structured ledger (`evidence[]` with `{date, detail, artifact}`) is the **CB
  tier**'s job; the floor keeps the lightweight `threads:[]` + body table.

The *format* rule above is owned by this standard. The *operational* directive — "at
session end, render the transcript and write the pair" — lives as a directive in CB's
belief graph and is executed by `/end`, not duplicated here.

## How this maps to CB

When a domain is promoted to the CB tier, these document types become artifacts and
beliefs: a `source`/`thread` is a CB **artifact** (referenced by `artifact:` URI); a
`concept`/`position`/`analysis` conclusion becomes a typed **belief** (primitive,
compound, or implication) that `deps` on the artifacts and concepts it was built from.
OKF is the artifact substrate; CB is the epistemic overlay on top of it.

content.content:
1	---
2	name: end
3	description: End-of-session sweep - persist unpersisted concepts to the right cb-dag, route direction content (strategy, positioning, competitor evaluation) to the private direction repos, audit session memory, package learnings into cb-site blog drafts, graduate scratch surfaces, verify, commit, and report the desk. Use when the user invokes "/end" or says they are wrapping up the session.
4	---
5	
6	# /end - close the session with nothing left only in the chat
7	
8	Invoking this skill is the user's authorization to commit and push the
9	persistence artifacts it produces (per repo git policy, which otherwise
10	requires explicit instruction). Destructive operations still confirm.
11	
12	Pull all touched repos before writing anything: other sessions write these
13	repos concurrently, and obligations are authored against the live graph,
14	never from memory (cb:a504).
15	
16	## Step 1 - worktree and tree hygiene
17	
18	If the session ran in a worktree, merge to main and clean up (orphans are
19	/merge-worktrees' job). Otherwise confirm working trees are clean or account
20	for every uncommitted file before proceeding.
21	
22	## Step 2 - thread sweep: what exists only in this conversation?
23	
24	Re-read the thread for: decisions made, conventions settled, findings and
25	incidents, stances argued, direction content worked through, obligations
26	created or discovered, tool facts learned, and agent reasoning errors the
27	user caught. For each, check whether it is already persisted (graph node,
28	plan record, position doc, operations note, direction doc). Route the
29	unpersisted by proportionality (cb:a496):
30	
31	- **Direction content - strategy, positioning, competitor evaluation,
32	  audience/market analysis, monetization and distribution thinking** ->
33	  NEVER a public-facing surface: not a README, not docs/, not positions/,
34	  not chronicles/, not a public graph, not a blog draft. It routes to
35	  exactly one of two private repos, by subject, as a dated doc in that
36	  repo's `direction/`:
37	  - CB-the-system (its positioning, competitors, distribution) ->
38	    `composablebeliefs/cb-direction`
39	  - amieval and everything else (the broader operation, cross-system
40	    strategy) -> `amieval/amieval-direction`
41	  These two are the only categorical direction destinations; do not create
42	  others. When a piece mixes checkable claims with strategy, split it: the
43	  checkable part graduates to the public surface, the strategic part goes
44	  to direction. Rationale on record in cb-direction's README and
45	  `direction/2026-06-12-claim-the-eval-vertical.md`: publishing the
46	  strategic rationale undercuts the credibility the public positioning
47	  pursues.
48	- **Stance whose reasoning matters verbatim** -> position document (in the
49	  repo whose system it is about, per cb:a492), then extract claims into the
50	  dag through the write flow.
51	- **Finding or convention with an obvious prescription** -> directive
52	  straight into the dag (gap as rationale prose or a small design-gap node).
53	- **Obligation / future work** -> backlog directive: kind action-item,
54	  tag `lifecycle:discrete`, grounded in deps or a stipulation artifact.
55	  Verify it is not already done or already on the desk before minting.
56	- **Tool facts** (editor behaviors, CLI quirks) -> evidence detail on the
57	  relevant convention node and/or docs/operations.md. Never claims.
58	- **Agent reasoning errors caught by the user** -> error-pattern node or
59	  evidence append. Look for rejected approaches ("no, that's wrong
60	  because..."), missed existing patterns ("we already have X"), unverified
61	  assumptions, and implicit corrections (the user restating a requirement
62	  after a misread). Link to existing patterns first (`mix bs list
63	  kind:error-pattern`, `kind:reasoning-error`); a recurring shape with no
64	  match seeds a new node, subject `{"ref": "agent", "type": "agent"}`.
65	  Distinguish what happened from why: observed behavior is a primitive
66	  ("observed: X"); a theory about the agent's reasoning is a compound
67	  ("theory: X"). Evidence detail tells the specific story ("agent proposed
68	  concatenated location field"), never the generic one ("agent made a
69	  modeling error"). Don't over-assert: one-off slips that feed no future
70	  reasoning stay in the chat.
71	
72	All graph writes go through the front door: preflight, record the
73	tag-overlap review in the evidence detail (overlap is necessary but never
74	sufficient for conflict), import, verify. Never hand-edit a graph file in a
75	repo that has a write flow.
76	
77	## Step 3 - dag routing
78	
79	Home each belief by the boundary: claims about CB-the-system land in `cb:`
80	(composable-beliefs); mission, domain, and field-use content lands in the
81	appropriate belief-collections collection (operator field observations carry
82	the `anecdote` tag per paradigm:a368; meta-observations about the human-agent
83	system go to `paradigm:`). Discover what dags exist via `collections.json`
84	and sibling `manifest.json` files rather than assuming.
85	
86	- If content belongs to several dags and the homes are clear, write each
87	  piece to its home and say so.
88	- If the homing is ambiguous, ask the user, with a recommendation.
89	- If a touched repo carries **no dag**, tell the user explicitly. Distinguish
90	  "missing" from "no dag by design" (cb-site carries none deliberately:
91	  proto-content churns; settled claims graduate to positions later).
92	
93	## Step 4 - session memory audit
94	
95	Session memory is an ephemeral cache (cb:a509): project and work state is
96	banned from it. Scan the memory directory for anything load-bearing that
97	lives only there and promote it through the write flow (the cb:a504-a506
98	precedent). Then PRUNE: delete the project-state files, leaving MEMORY.md as
99	a one-line pointer at the graph (CLAUDE.md Session start + the desk). At
100	most a thin operator-preferences note survives, and only until the private
101	overlay collection gives preferences a graph home.
102	
103	## Step 5 - transcript, chronicle, and scratch graduation
104	
105	If the session did plan-scale work, persist or postscript its transcript in
106	the co-located plans convention (plans/<set>/transcript-execution.md).
107	
108	If the session was **decision-weight** (cb:a540) - it minted or superseded
109	beliefs grounded in a user:/session: stipulation, settled a stance, or
110	adjudicated contradictory positions - also persist the thread **verbatim**
111	(user messages and assistant prose as written, tool activity bracketed), not
112	only condensed. Home it by subject: strategy/direction threads to the private
113	direction repos (amieval-direction / cb-direction, by the cb-direction routing
114	rule), plan-scale dev threads to plans/<set>/. A continuation of an existing
115	arc appends to that arc's thread dir rather than starting a new one. The
116	directive stays the SSOT and self-bootstrapping; the verbatim thread is
117	provenance only. This is the bridge until cb:a518 makes the real transcript
118	resolvable.
119	
120	Then write or extend the thread's **chronicle** (cb:a520): a dated prose
121	narrative in chronicles/ of the repo where the thread centered - where
122	things stood, the arc with its incidents as story beats, where things stand
123	now, what the next session inherits. Written for the operator's steering,
124	never as a dry event list: narrative carries the load, ids stay subordinate
125	(the inverse of the receipts register). The transcript is the record; the
126	chronicle is how the human keeps up.
127	Graduate the tmp/ glue surfaces (lap-log, transcript.md, cards): anything
128	they surfaced that matters moves to the transcript or the graph; the scratch
129	files themselves stay untracked and die. Adjudication wire records and
130	proposal files graduate to the proposals/ dir of the thread's
131	center-of-gravity plan set - the graph carries the adjudication, the file
132	carries the provenance.
133	
134	After the chronicle and transcript exist, retro-pair: append a `document:`
135	pointer to any evidence minted this session that cites a bare `session:`
136	slug (cb:a507 - a session ref is a dead end for a fresh agent until the
137	persistence pipeline lands; cb:a518 is the systemic fix).
138	
139	## Step 6 - blog packaging (cb-site)
140	
141	Package learnings that apply to CB or amieval into dated drafts in
142	`cb-site/posts/drafts/` (private repo: composablebeliefs/cb-site). Instruction,
143	verbatim from the operator:
144	
145	> these are as much for me to read to understand what the functionality now
146	> is as to explain how we got here and why decisions were made as they were.
147	> this should be less of a sequential recounting and more of an actual blog
148	> post. concepts should be explained, assume an audience that does not yet
149	> know what cb is, though is technical enough to read posts with code. do
150	> not worry about writerly flourishes, do not try to "write", instead focus
151	> on relaying what you think is consequential in a straightforward style
152	
153	Separate posts by topic, never by chronology. One draft per genuinely
154	distinct learning; skip sessions that produced none (routine work is not a
155	post). Mark each as Draft with origin and audience at the top. House prose
156	rules apply: no emdashes; state what things are directly. Report performance
157	claims as anecdote until an eval says otherwise.
158	
159	Drafts are publication-bound: no direction content (strategy, positioning,
160	competitor evaluation) in a draft - that routes to the direction repos per
161	step 2, even when it would make good narrative.
162	
163	## Step 7 - verify, commit, report
164	
165	For every touched repo, run its gates before committing: `mix test`,
166	`mix cb.verify.schema`, `mix cb.generate.claude_md --check`,
167	`mix cb.verify.collection <ns>` for touched collections. Leave green or
168	report exactly what is red and why.
169	
170	Commit per repo with messages that say what landed and why; push. Close with
171	the session report:
172	
173	- what was persisted where (ids, files, commits)
174	- the live desk: `mix bs list unlinked tag:lifecycle:discrete` per relevant
175	  graph (note the cross-collection gap, cb:a500, until it is built)
176	- anything that needs the user: ambiguous homings, red gates, decisions
177	  deferred
178	
179	## Step 8 - the close sweeps itself
180	
181	The close itself surfaces findings: calibration issues, machinery friction,
182	skill stumbles, observations "worth carrying forward." Persist them before
183	reporting them - a finding that lives only in the final chat message is the
184	exact failure this skill exists to prevent. Route by step 2's rules (usually
185	a desk directive or an evidence append on the node it concerns), then let
186	the report reference the id.
187

## Step 2 - thread sweep: what exists only in this conversation?

- **Direction content - strategy, positioning, competitor evaluation,

  repo's `direction/`:
  - CB-the-system (its positioning, competitors, distribution) ->
    `composablebeliefs/cb-direction`
  - amieval and everything else (the broader operation, cross-system
    strategy) -> `amieval/amieval-direction`
  These two are the only categorical direction destinations; do not create
  others. When a piece mixes checkable claims with strategy, split it: the
  checkable part graduates to the public surface, the strategic part goes
  to direction. Rationale on record in cb-direction's README and
  `direction/2026-06-12-claim-the-eval-vertical.md`: publishing the
  strategic rationale undercuts the credibility the public positioning
  pursues.
- **Stance whose reasoning matters verbatim** -> position document (in the
  repo whose system it is about, per cb:a492), then extract claims into the
  dag through the write flow.
- **Finding or convention with an obvious prescription** -> directive
  straight into the dag (gap as rationale prose or a small design-gap node).
- **Obligation / future work** -> backlog directive: kind action-item,
  tag `lifecycle:discrete`, grounded in deps or a stipulation artifact.
  Verify it is not already done or already on the desk before minting.
- **Tool facts** (editor behaviors, CLI quirks) -> evidence detail on the
  relevant convention node and/or docs/operations.md. Never claims.
- **Agent reasoning errors caught by the user** -> error-pattern node or
  evidence append. Look for rejected approaches ("no, that's wrong
  because..."), missed existing patterns ("we already have X"), unverified
  assumptions, and implicit corrections (the user restating a requirement
  after a misread). Link to existing patterns first (`mix bs list
  kind:error-pattern`, `kind:reasoning-error`); a recurring shape with no
  match seeds a new node, subject `{"ref": "agent", "type": "agent"}`.
  Distinguish what happened from why: observed behavior is a primitive
  ("observed: X"); a theory about the agent's reasoning is a compound
  ("theory: X"). Evidence detail tells the specific story ("agent proposed
  concatenated location field"), never the generic one ("agent made a
  modeling error"). Don't over-assert: one-off slips that feed no future
  reasoning stay in the chat.

All graph writes go through the front door: preflight, record the
tag-overlap review in the evidence detail (overlap is necessary but never
sufficient for conflict), import, verify. Never hand-edit a graph file in a
repo that has a write flow.

## Step 3 - dag routing

Home each belief by the boundary: claims about CB-the-system land in `cb:`
(composable-beliefs); mission, domain, and field-use content lands in the
appropriate belief-collections collection (operator field observations carry
the `anecdote` tag per paradigm:a368; meta-observations about the human-agent
system go to `paradigm:`). Discover what dags exist via `collections.json`
and sibling `manifest.json` files rather than assuming.

- If content belongs to several dags and the homes are clear, write each
  piece to its home and say so.
- If the homing is ambiguous, ask the user, with a recommendation.
- If a touched repo carries **no dag**, tell the user explicitly. Distinguish
  "missing" from "no dag by design" (cb-site carries none deliberately:
  proto-content churns; settled claims graduate to positions later).

## Step 4 - session memory audit

Session memory is an ephemeral cache (cb:a509): project and work state is
banned from it. Scan the memory directory for anything load-bearing that
lives only there and promote it through the write flow (the cb:a504-a506
precedent). Then PRUNE: delete the project-state files, leaving MEMORY.md as
a one-line pointer at the graph (CLAUDE.md Session start + the desk). At
most a thin operator-preferences note survives, and only until the private
overlay collection gives preferences a graph home.

## Step 5 - transcript, chronicle, and scratch graduation

If the session did plan-scale work, persist or postscript its transcript in
the co-located plans convention (plans/<set>/transcript-execution.md).

If the session was **decision-weight** (cb:a540) - it minted or superseded
beliefs grounded in a user:/session: stipulation, settled a stance, or
adjudicated contradictory positions - also persist the thread **verbatim**
(user messages and assistant prose as written, tool activity bracketed), not
only condensed. Home it by subject: strategy/direction threads to the private
direction repos (amieval-direction / cb-direction, by the cb-direction routing
rule), plan-scale dev threads to plans/<set>/. A continuation of an existing
arc appends to that arc's thread dir rather than starting a new one. The
directive stays the SSOT and self-bootstrapping; the verbatim thread is
provenance only. This is the bridge until cb:a518 makes the real transcript
resolvable.

Then write or extend the thread's **chronicle** (cb:a520): a dated prose
narrative in chronicles/ of the repo where the thread centered - where
things stood, the arc with its incidents as story beats, where things stand
now, what the next session inherits. Written for the operator's steering,
never as a dry event list: narrative carries the load, ids stay subordinate
(the inverse of the receipts register). The transcript is the record; the
chronicle is how the human keeps up.
Graduate the tmp/ glue surfaces (lap-log, transcript.md, cards): anything
they surfaced that matters moves to the transcript or the graph; the scratch
files themselves stay untracked and die. Adjudication wire records and
proposal files graduate to the proposals/ dir of the thread's
center-of-gravity plan set - the graph carries the adjudication, the file
carries the provenance.

After the chronicle and transcript exist, retro-pair: append a `document:`
pointer to any evidence minted this session that cites a bare `session:`
slug (cb:a507 - a session ref is a dead end for a fresh agent until the
persistence pipeline lands; cb:a518 is the systemic fix).

## Step 6 - blog packaging (cb-site)

Package learnings that apply to CB or amieval into dated drafts in
`cb-site/posts/drafts/` (private repo: composablebeliefs/cb-site). Instruction,
verbatim from the operator:

> these are as much for me to read to understand what the functionality now
> is as to explain how we got here and why decisions were made as they were.
> this should be less of a sequential recounting and more of an actual blog
> post. concepts should be explained, assume an audience that does not yet
> know what cb is, though is technical enough to read posts with code. do
> not worry about writerly flourishes, do not try to "write", instead focus
> on relaying what you think is consequential in a straightforward style

Separate posts by topic, never by chronology. One draft per genuinely
distinct learning; skip sessions that produced none (routine work is not a
post). Mark each as Draft with origin and audience at the top. House prose
rules apply: no emdashes; state what things are directly. Report performance
claims as anecdote until an eval says otherwise.

Drafts are publication-bound: no direction content (strategy, positioning,
competitor evaluation) in a draft - that routes to the direction repos per
step 2, even when it would make good narrative.

## Step 7 - verify, commit, report

For every touched repo, run its gates before committing: `mix test`,
`mix cb.verify.schema`, `mix cb.generate.claude_md --check`,
`mix cb.verify.collection <ns>` for touched collections. Leave green or
report exactly what is red and why.

Commit per repo with messages that say what landed and why; push. Close with
the session report:

- what was persisted where (ids, files, commits)
- the live desk: `mix bs list unlinked tag:lifecycle:discrete` per relevant
  graph (note the cross-collection gap, cb:a500, until it is built)
- anything that needs the user: ambiguous homings, red gates, decisions
  deferred

## Step 8 - the close sweeps itself

The close itself surfaces findings: calibration issues, machinery friction,
skill stumbles, observations "worth carrying forward." Persist them before
reporting them - a finding that lives only in the final chat message is the
exact failure this skill exists to prevent. Route by step 2's rules (usually
a desk directive or an evidence append on the node it concerns), then let
the report reference the id.

Provide the mapping … so `session:` evidence dereferences the way `document:` and `code:` artifacts do."* That **is** "duplicate the real `.jsonl`, don't reconstruct."
- **`cb:a540`** (the verbatim-at-close directive, "Enforced operationally by the `/end` skill step 5") explicitly says it's a **faithful reconstruction** *"until the session-artifact pipeline (`cb:a518`) makes the real transcript resolvable … this convention is the bridge to that mechanization."*

So `/end` Step 5 already persists threads; it just does manual reconstruction *as a deliberate bridge*, with `cb:a518` already on the backlog as the systemic fix. **Minting a new "/end thread-persistence directive" would duplicate `cb:a518`/`cb:a540`** — so I did not.

What you actually want is to **implement `cb:a518`** (materialize the backlog item): wire `/end` to duplicate the actual `.jsonl` instead of reconstructing — which retires the "faithful reconstruction" bridge. And the **knowledge standard we just built supplies the missing piece**: the `source`+`thread` doc format (with `threads:[]`) is precisely the "slug metadata / registry" shape `cb:a518` asks for. That's a real, clean integration: `cb:a518` = the operational fix; the knowledge standard = the format its output takes.

I stopped here rather than barrel ahead, because the remaining actions are consequential and I want your nod on the framing first:

1. **Edit the *global* `/end` skill** (Step 5) to **copy the real `.jsonl`** into the project's `meta/` as a `type: source` per the knowledge standard, resolving `session:` → the file. This is a global behavior change (all projects).
2. **Record it through CB's write flow** as the **materialization of `cb:a518`** (evidence append + `mix cb.todo.close`/materialize), and update `cb:a540`'s bridge note to point at the now-landed mechanism — *not* a new duplicate belief.

Given the graph already owns this, my recommendation is to treat it as "implement + close `cb:a518`," grounded in the knowledge standard's format rule. Want me to proceed on that basis — edit the global `/end` skill and materialize `cb:a518` through the write flow? (Knowledge-repo half is already done and pushed: `b908b7e`.)

Items tagged as `cb:` namespace go to `composable-beliefs/beliefs/beliefs.json`, while other collections (lib:, method:, paradigm:, etc.) each have their own `beliefs.json` under `belief-collections/<collection>/`. The routing rule determines placement based on the subject domain—so non-CB-related items would go to their appropriate collection's beliefs file rather than the core CB system.

Now I'm realizing there's a structural gap here: the existing collections are all ecosystem-focused around CB itself (library examples, methodology, paradigm), but there's no clear home for general personal obligations or productivity-related beliefs that don't fit neatly into those domains. This suggests the collection structure might need expansion to handle broader operational or personal knowledge work. So the non-CB obligations genuinely have nowhere to live right now—that's the gap the global-CB-graph item is meant to address. Until it exists, those items either wait or get a temporary home, and /end step 3 will surface this ambiguity to the user explicitly rather than pretending there's a clean answer. I should verify the actual structure of belief-collections and collections.json to give the user precise paths rather than relying on memory, since they value accuracy and the discovery process matters as much as the answer itself.

content.thinking:
I'm identifying the actual belief graph files across the codebase—there's the main composable-beliefs collection, the codepath collection, and several others in belief-collections covering agent behavior, evaluation provenance, library utilities, and methods.

Looking at the collection registry structure, I see the namespaces map to these domains: cb for composable-beliefs itself, and belief-collections contains agent-behavior, eval-provenance, library, method, paradigm, and toy-eval as worked examples and domains exploring the human-agent paradigm.

The key insight is that all existing graphs are CB-ecosystem focused—there's currently no graph home for general obligations or cross-project items unrelated to CB, though the retroactive jsonl registry fits semantically as CB infrastructure for artifact resolvability.

Items like the CONVERT pilot on a personal knowledge repo or global dotfiles compilation don't have a clean home in the current structure, which reveals a real gap: either a new general/operational collection needs to exist (like `ops:` or `me:`), or the knowledge repo itself needs its own graph for knowledge-methodology work.

The retroactive jsonl registry is already CB-related (cb:a518), so it has a proper home, but the non-CB obligations will surface as homeless through the `/end` flag rather than getting misfiled—which is the honest approach until that missing collection or global graph exists.

content.text:
Concrete answer: a "desk item" is a belief, and beliefs live in a `beliefs.json` file in some repo. The **real** graphs (ignoring `tmp/` test scaffolding) are all CB-ecosystem:

| Graph file | Namespace | Subject |
|---|---|---|
| `composable-beliefs/beliefs/beliefs.json` | `cb:` | CB-the-system |
| `composable-beliefs/codepath/beliefs.json` | codepath | code tours |
| `belief-collections/library/beliefs.json` | `lib:` | the lending-library worked example |
| `belief-collections/method/beliefs.json` | `method:` | CB method |
| `belief-collections/paradigm/beliefs.json` | `paradigm:` | the human-agent system |
| `belief-collections/eval-provenance/beliefs.json` | … | eval provenance |
| `belief-collections/agent-behavior/`, `toy-eval/` | … | more CB examples |

Routing is by **subject** (`/end` step 3 + cb:a492), discovered via `belief-collections/collections.json` + each collection's `manifest.json`.

Now your actual question — **where do *non-CB* items live? Today: nowhere.** Every graph above is about CB, its examples, its method, or the human-agent paradigm. There is **no graph for general / personal / productivity / cross-project obligations.** So:

- **The retroactive `.jsonl` registry is *not* a non-CB item** — it's CB's own evidence/artifact-resolution infrastructure (`domain: system`), so it correctly lives in `cb:` (`composable-beliefs/beliefs/beliefs.json`), where it already is as `cb:a518`. ✓
- **The genuinely non-CB items — the CONVERT-pilot-on-SECOND-BRAIN, and arguably the global-CLAUDE compile — have no clean home.** That's a real gap, and it's the same gap the global-CB-graph item names.

So the options for a homeless obligation are:
1. **Give the `knowledge` repo its own graph** — make it a belief-collection (`knowledge/beliefs.json`, namespace `knowledge:`) so knowledge-methodology obligations (CONVERT pilots, the `threads:[]` sweep) have a home. Natural, since it's already a CB consumer. **My recommendation** for knowledge-system work.
2. **The global dotfiles graph** (the open item) — for truly cross-project / operational obligations.
3. **Flag as homeless** — `/end` step 3 explicitly says "if a touched repo carries no dag, tell the user explicitly," so it *won't* misfile them; it'll surface them to you.

So when you run `/end`: `cb:a518`-class items land in `cb:`, eval items in `cb:`/`eval-provenance`, and the non-CB ones (CONVERT pilot, global compile) will come back to you as **homeless** with a recommendation — most likely "stand up a `knowledge:` collection" — rather than being forced somewhere wrong.

Net: run `/end`; expect it to file the CB-related obligations to `cb:` and ask you where the knowledge/productivity ones should live (recommend: a new `knowledge:` graph). Want me to stand up that `knowledge:` collection as part of this, or leave it for `/end` to raise?

aiTitle: Design knowledge base system using Open Knowledge Format

content.thinking:
I'm seeing how the collection system works: `collections.json` maps namespaces to file paths, with cross-repo references using relative paths like `../composable-beliefs/beliefs/beliefs.json`. Each collection has its own `manifest.json` containing dependencies, description, and namespace info, and the default registry lives at `../belief-collections/collections.json` relative to the CB repo root.

To set up the `knowledge:` namespace, I'd create a `manifest.json` and `beliefs.json` in `amieval/knowledge/beliefs/`, then add the entry to the collections registry. But I'm noticing a potential overlap: the knowledge repo already has OKF markdown docs with `tier: cb` frontmatter in the meta/ directory, which are conceptually CB-tier beliefs, while the new `beliefs.json` would be the actual CB graph objects. These are different formats coexisting in the same repo—the OKF docs versus the structured belief objects—which is fine but worth being explicit about.

The real distinction is that methodology content (standards, positions, analyses) stays as OKF markdown with CB-tier frontmatter, while operational obligations (like CONVERT pilot directives) go into the `knowledge:` CB graph. That's a clean split between format/content and operational behavior.

For `depends_on`, I'm weighing whether `knowledge:` should declare a dependency on `cb:`. Knowledge obligations might reference CB beliefs (e.g., grounding a directive in `cb:a518`), which would justify `depends_on: ["cb"]`. But that couples the two graphs and means the registry has to resolve CB when verifying knowledge. For a clean start, I could leave it standalone with `depends_on: []` and add the CB coupling later if needed, though knowledge obligations will likely end up referencing CB beliefs anyway.

The trickier part is enum contracts — `kind`, `domain`, and `artifact-scheme` values need to be declared somewhere. The CB graph declares those enums (c039, c041, c043). If `knowledge:` depends on CB, it inherits those contracts and can use CB's enum values without declaring its own. That's simpler than having knowledge declare its own contracts, and it makes sense since knowledge is naturally downstream of CB. So `depends_on: ["cb"]` is the path of least resistance.

For the structure: manifest goes in `knowledge/beliefs/manifest.json` alongside `knowledge/beliefs/beliefs.json` (matching how composable-beliefs organizes its own manifest and beliefs as siblings). Start with an empty beliefs array, register it in collections.json, then verify with `mix cb.verify.collection knowledge` — it should resolve the manifest, load the union via depends_on, and pass trivially on the empty graph.

Done — `knowledge:` graph stood up, registered, and verified.

## Where desk items live now

| Subject | Graph | Path |
|---|---|---|
| CB-the-system (incl. the `.jsonl` registry `cb:a518`) | `cb:` | `composable-beliefs/beliefs/beliefs.json` |
| Knowledge methodology (CONVERT pilots, `threads:[]` sweep) | **`knowledge:`** (new) | `knowledge/beliefs/beliefs.json` |
| CB worked-examples / method / eval | `lib:` `method:` `sdl:` … | `belief-collections/<ns>/beliefs.json` |

The `knowledge:` collection (`f259a51`) is co-located in the knowledge repo so it owns its own work — empty to start, `depends_on cb:` for vocabulary, registered in `belief-collections/collections.json` (`abc0992`), and `mix cb.verify.collection knowledge` passes 21/0. A fresh agent finds it the same way as any graph: the collection registry + the desk query.

## You're clear to run `/end`

When you do, it'll mint the open obligations into the right homes via the write flow:
- `.jsonl` registry → already `cb:a518` (cb:).
- CONVERT-pilot, `threads:[]` sweep → `knowledge:`.
- dag-vs-prose eval arm → `cb:`/`eval-provenance`.
- The one that may still come back **homeless**: the global-dotfiles-graph idea (it's cross-project meta-infra, not knowledge-specific) — `/end` will surface it with a recommendation rather than misfile it.

This session also becomes the first real exercise of the `.jsonl`-duplication mechanism we just landed — `/end` step 5 will copy this thread's transcript verbatim instead of reconstructing it.

Go ahead and run `/end`.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/mark/.claude/skills/end

# /end - close the session with nothing left only in the chat

Invoking this skill is the user's authorization to commit and push the
persistence artifacts it produces (per repo git policy, which otherwise
requires explicit instruction). Destructive operations still confirm.

Pull all touched repos before writing anything: other sessions write these
repos concurrently, and obligations are authored against the live graph,
never from memory (cb:a504).

## Step 1 - worktree and tree hygiene

If the session ran in a worktree, merge to main and clean up (orphans are
/merge-worktrees' job). Otherwise confirm working trees are clean or account
for every uncommitted file before proceeding.

## Step 2 - thread sweep: what exists only in this conversation?

Re-read the thread for: decisions made, conventions settled, findings and
incidents, stances argued, direction content worked through, obligations
created or discovered, tool facts learned, and agent reasoning errors the
user caught. For each, check whether it is already persisted (graph node,
plan record, position doc, operations note, direction doc). Route the
unpersisted by proportionality (cb:a496):

- **Direction content - strategy, positioning, competitor evaluation,
  audience/market analysis, monetization and distribution thinking** ->
  NEVER a public-facing surface: not a README, not docs/, not positions/,
  not chronicles/, not a public graph, not a blog draft. It routes to
  exactly one of two private repos, by subject, as a dated doc in that
  repo's `direction/`:
  - CB-the-system (its positioning, competitors, distribution) ->
    `composablebeliefs/cb-direction`
  - amieval and everything else (the broader operation, cross-system
    strategy) -> `amieval/amieval-direction`
  These two are the only categorical direction destinations; do not create
  others. When a piece mixes checkable claims with strategy, split it: the
  checkable part graduates to the public surface, the strategic part goes
  to direction. Rationale on record in cb-direction's README and
  `direction/2026-06-12-claim-the-eval-vertical.md`: publishing the
  strategic rationale undercuts the credibility the public positioning
  pursues.
- **Stance whose reasoning matters verbatim** -> position document (in the
  repo whose system it is about, per cb:a492), then extract claims into the
  dag through the write flow.
- **Finding or convention with an obvious prescription** -> directive
  straight into the dag (gap as rationale prose or a small design-gap node).
- **Obligation / future work** -> backlog directive: kind action-item,
  tag `lifecycle:discrete`, grounded in deps or a stipulation artifact.
  Verify it is not already done or already on the desk before minting.
- **Tool facts** (editor behaviors, CLI quirks) -> evidence detail on the
  relevant convention node and/or docs/operations.md. Never claims.
- **Agent reasoning errors caught by the user** -> error-pattern node or
  evidence append. Look for rejected approaches ("no, that's wrong
  because..."), missed existing patterns ("we already have X"), unverified
  assumptions, and implicit corrections (the user restating a requirement
  after a misread). Link to existing patterns first (`mix bs list
  kind:error-pattern`, `kind:reasoning-error`); a recurring shape with no
  match seeds a new node, subject `{"ref": "agent", "type": "agent"}`.
  Distinguish what happened from why: observed behavior is a primitive
  ("observed: X"); a theory about the agent's reasoning is a compound
  ("theory: X"). Evidence detail tells the specific story ("agent proposed
  concatenated location field"), never the generic one ("agent made a
  modeling error"). Don't over-assert: one-off slips that feed no future
  reasoning stay in the chat.

All graph writes go through the front door: preflight, record the
tag-overlap review in the evidence detail (overlap is necessary but never
sufficient for conflict), import, verify. Never hand-edit a graph file in a
repo that has a write flow.

## Step 3 - dag routing

Home each belief by the boundary: claims about CB-the-system land in `cb:`
(composable-beliefs); mission, domain, and field-use content lands in the
appropriate belief-collections collection (operator field observations carry
the `anecdote` tag per paradigm:a368; meta-observations about the human-agent
system go to `paradigm:`). Discover what dags exist via `collections.json`
and sibling `manifest.json` files rather than assuming.

- If content belongs to several dags and the homes are clear, write each
  piece to its home and say so.
- If the homing is ambiguous, ask the user, with a recommendation.
- If a touched repo carries **no dag**, tell the user explicitly. Distinguish
  "missing" from "no dag by design" (cb-site carries none deliberately:
  proto-content churns; settled claims graduate to positions later).

## Step 4 - session memory audit

Session memory is an ephemeral cache (cb:a509): project and work state is
banned from it. Scan the memory directory for anything load-bearing that
lives only there and promote it through the write flow (the cb:a504-a506
precedent). Then PRUNE: delete the project-state files, leaving MEMORY.md as
a one-line pointer at the graph (CLAUDE.md Session start + the desk). At
most a thin operator-preferences note survives, and only until the private
overlay collection gives preferences a graph home.

## Step 5 - transcript, chronicle, and scratch graduation

If the session did plan-scale work, persist or postscript its transcript in
the co-located plans convention (plans/<set>/transcript-execution.md).

If the session was **decision-weight** (cb:a540) - it minted or superseded
beliefs grounded in a user:/session: stipulation, settled a stance, or
adjudicated contradictory positions - also persist the thread **verbatim**.
cb:a518 is now landed, so this is a **file duplication, never a
reconstruction**: copy the real session log - the uuid-keyed `.jsonl` for this
session under `~/.claude/projects/<encoded-project>/` (the file whose name
matches this session's id, newest by mtime) - byte-for-byte into the
subject-appropriate repo as an immutable `type: source` doc, following the
knowledge standard's source+thread persistence rule
(amieval/knowledge `standard/types.md`). Home it by subject: strategy/direction
threads to the private direction repos (amieval-direction / cb-direction, by
the cb-direction routing rule), plan-scale dev threads to plans/<set>/. A
continuation of an existing arc appends to that arc's thread dir rather than
starting a new one. The copied `.jsonl` is what every `session:` artifact now
resolves to, so retro-pair each belief minted this session (cb:a507) with a
`document:` pointer to it. The directive stays the SSOT and self-bootstrapping;
the verbatim thread is provenance only.

Then write or extend the thread's **chronicle** (cb:a520): a dated prose
narrative in chronicles/ of the repo where the thread centered - where
things stood, the arc with its incidents as story beats, where things stand
now, what the next session inherits. Written for the operator's steering,
never as a dry event list: narrative carries the load, ids stay subordinate
(the inverse of the receipts register). The transcript is the record; the
chronicle is how the human keeps up.
Graduate the tmp/ glue surfaces (lap-log, transcript.md, cards): anything
they surfaced that matters moves to the transcript or the graph; the scratch
files themselves stay untracked and die. Adjudication wire records and
proposal files graduate to the proposals/ dir of the thread's
center-of-gravity plan set - the graph carries the adjudication, the file
carries the provenance.

After the chronicle and transcript exist, retro-pair: append a `document:`
pointer to any evidence minted this session that cites a bare `session:`
slug (cb:a507 - a session ref is a dead end for a fresh agent until the
persistence pipeline lands; cb:a518 is the systemic fix).

## Step 6 - blog packaging (cb-site)

Package learnings that apply to CB or amieval into dated drafts in
`cb-site/posts/drafts/` (private repo: composablebeliefs/cb-site). Instruction,
verbatim from the operator:

> these are as much for me to read to understand what the functionality now
> is as to explain how we got here and why decisions were made as they were.
> this should be less of a sequential recounting and more of an actual blog
> post. concepts should be explained, assume an audience that does not yet
> know what cb is, though is technical enough to read posts with code. do
> not worry about writerly flourishes, do not try to "write", instead focus
> on relaying what you think is consequential in a straightforward style

Separate posts by topic, never by chronology. One draft per genuinely
distinct learning; skip sessions that produced none (routine work is not a
post). Mark each as Draft with origin and audience at the top. House prose
rules apply: no emdashes; state what things are directly. Report performance
claims as anecdote until an eval says otherwise.

Drafts are publication-bound: no direction content (strategy, positioning,
competitor evaluation) in a draft - that routes to the direction repos per
step 2, even when it would make good narrative.

## Step 7 - verify, commit, report

For every touched repo, run its gates before committing: `mix test`,
`mix cb.verify.schema`, `mix cb.generate.claude_md --check`,
`mix cb.verify.collection <ns>` for touched collections. Leave green or
report exactly what is red and why.

Commit per repo with messages that say what landed and why; push. Close with
the session report:

- what was persisted where (ids, files, commits)
- the live desk: `mix bs list unlinked tag:lifecycle:discrete` per relevant
  graph (note the cross-collection gap, cb:a500, until it is built)
- anything that needs the user: ambiguous homings, red gates, decisions
  deferred

## Step 8 - the close sweeps itself

The close itself surfaces findings: calibration issues, machinery friction,
skill stumbles, observations "worth carrying forward." Persist them before
reporting them - a finding that lives only in the final chat message is the
exact failure this skill exists to prevent. Route by step 2's rules (usually
a desk directive or an evidence append on the node it concerns), then let
the report reference the id.

Let me map out the scope systematically—this was a large session touching multiple repositories, so I should track what's already committed and pushed versus what's still pending.

Looking at what's already persisted: the knowledge repo got the full standard, conformance suite, routing principles, and thread-persistence rules; composable-beliefs has the OKF integration layer with manifest/validate/emit/ingest logic and id-format validation; cb-direction has the positioning note; dotfiles-claude has the /end skill; and belief-collections has the registry entry. That's substantial ground already covered.

Now for the /end close itself, I need to handle tree hygiene first—checking that all touched repos are clean while accounting for the user's existing work in composable-beliefs that isn't mine. Then I'll sweep through what exists only in chat: most decisions and conventions are already persisted in the standard docs and positioning note, but I need to mint backlog directives for the open items like the CONVERT pilot, threads sweep, and global-CLAUDE graph. The findings around id-format conventions and the standard/consumer boundary are already captured in the standard docs.

I'm also catching some agent reasoning patterns worth noting: I defaulted to lossy summary-only persistence when the user wanted raw data kept, which maps to existing compression errors; I dismissed backlinking as "too heavy" when the real constraint is one-to-many complexity; and the spec/ folder type collision that the user caught is a design improvement. These are candidates for error-pattern nodes if they're systematic enough to persist.

Now I need to work through the close systematically: pull the repos, check what obligations already exist, then decide what genuinely needs minting versus what's already captured. The big question is where to house the global CB graph infrastructure—whether it belongs in cb: as system infra, paradigm:, or flagged as homeless until the private overlay collection is ready.

For the conventions and findings, I'm realizing the standard OKF docs are already the canonical home for methodology content like "folders denote role not type" and "id is namespace:local"—I don't need to duplicate those as CB beliefs. The routing principle itself (format rules → standard, operational directives → CB graph) is what needs to live in KNOWLEDGE.md as the persistence layer.

On direction content, the cb-direction note already captures the OKF-as-conformant-consumer framing, so I think that's complete. For the agent reasoning errors—the under-persist instinct and the dismissal of provenance backlinking—these are recurring biases worth persisting as evidence on an existing error pattern, since they'll shape future reasoning. I should append to the relevant pattern rather than over-assert one-off slips. Now I need to execute the close pragmatically and focused.

The main remaining work is minting the open obligations as backlog directives, persisting the transcript, writing a chronicle, maybe a blog draft, capturing agent-error evidence, auditing memory, and verifying everything. Given the scale, I should start with reconnaissance—checking git status across the touched repos (knowledge, composable-beliefs, cb-direction, belief-collections, dotfiles-claude), pulling them, surveying the desk and memory directory, and identifying the session's .jsonl. # Chronicle: the thread becomes provenance

**Span:** 2026-06-21, a short methodology-and-tooling session continuing an
earlier governance arc.
**Register:** chronicle (cb:a520) - narrative for the operator; the durable
record is cb:a540 with the /end skill patch, and cb:a541/a542 on the desk.

## Where things stood

A prior close had, for the first time, persisted a decision thread *verbatim*
into a private direction repo and retro-paired every belief it minted back to
it - following the a504/a507 precedent rather than any standing rule. The
operator asked the obvious next question: should that be a general policy, or
was it a one-off?

## The arc

The answer was yes, but scoped. A verbatim thread earns its cost only for
   [...]
nodes, reusing the PlanApp.BeliefJoin machinery positions already use) and
cb:a542 (classify/1 anchors status patterns with ^, so a "largely superseded"
header mis-buckets as pending). The verbatim thread of this continuation was
appended to the governance arc's existing thread record in amieval-direction,
which a540, a541, and a542 all cite.

## What the next session inherits

The plan-as-directive-view (a541) is the substantive thread to pull if plan-app
is to become the auditable dashboard the operator wants - it turns a plan page
from a static doc into a live status board over the obligations it grounds. The
desk carries it alongside the older items; the strategy obligations
(belief-collections org split, firewall predicate upgrade) still wait on the
operator.
